### Post by sparky295 on 2011-01-05
hello everyone ,name is Chris.
been a linux user for over 10 years but still consider myself a beginner.
have been cursing microsoft since win98 but decided just recently to try win 7 (who knows mabe they finally got something right) GRRRRRR
here is how it went lol

mistake #1 - attempting to install microsoft !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!! -
mistake #2- 10 gig partition fat32 ( apparently requires 16 gig minimum NTFS)
mistake #3 - reformatted NTFS 18 gig (2 more than required minimum) "NOT ENOUGH SPACE !"
                  - reformatted again (must mention that the first time i shrunk 2 linux partitions to 2 & 3 gig respectively and both ubuntu and mint had no issues with this) this time wiping two partitions in order to give windows a total of 30 gig ; 22 of which it used on install :(

mistake #3 - allowed windows to install important security updates !!! ( totally destroyed MBR !!!!! GRRRRR!
mistake #4 - attempted to use windows system restore disk which also now refuses to boot !!!! GRRRRRR
 first smart moove - installed crunchbang on different partition restoring MBR windows finally runs ! :)

all this took a total of 13 hours of messing around

Afterwards i repartitioned and installed Xubuntu (my fav linux distro), vector, mint, fedora, mandriva, puppy, suse, and re installed crunchbang to set the mbr ( which took less than 3 hours)
all works flawlesly except windows which is painfully slow and has already had a virus as well as the minor problem of Mandriva, and suse which use clasic grub (i am not all that good with grub or grub2 so havent been able to get mandriva and suse to load :( ) 
all in all i have always favoured Ubuntu and would run any linux distro instead of microsoft

i run an acer aspire 5532 Amd athlon 64 tf-20 (1.6ghz)
ati radion hd 3200
3gig ram
250 gig hdd

I would love to ad mac/os to my myltiboot to see how it compares to ubuntu as well but i have my doubts that i could make it play nice with an acer so it would be an unfair comparison anyone have any theories on this  ?

### Post by s1wood on 2011-01-14
Can't think how I missed it, but I don't seem to have posted here yet. I'm Susan, a middle-aged Englishwoman with no technology background or training. I've been using computers for hobby and work for just a few years and after having learned about defragging and registry cleaning etc for Windows XP (and got pretty tired of doing it), last year I suddenly decided to learn about computers and having got a few books I got a £10 'spares or repair' desktop, got it working and then rebuilt it. *(If I can do it, anyone can!*) I'd bought a netbook with a Linux OS and liked it and had been playing with several Linux distros, so when the machine was running I installed Ubuntu 9.10 as the most user-friendly OS I had found and have stuck with Ubuntu since. I shifted all my work from the old windows computer without a pang of regret. When Xandros stopped supplying updates I put Ubuntu on my netbook as well.
It is the user-friendly aspect that I like about Ubuntu, some of the other distros I tried were, to put it politely, challenging. It may not be what some of you like to hear, but basically Ubuntu is easy enough for your mum to use!

Thanks to forum members for help and advice.

Susan

### Post by peterabbitcook on 2011-03-01

### Post by carolinabranden on 2011-03-05
Hey Ubuntu members! My name is Branden and I'm living in North Carolina near the coast. Other than the beach traffic it's a lovely place to live. I was raised in a disciplined home. I attained my father's and mother's do-it-yourself attitude that you shouldn't depend on anyone when you're perfectly capable of  doing it yourself. I enjoy working on things myself such changing my own motor oil. :P

                Many years back while I was a child my grandfather bought us a Compaq desktop with Windows 98. Ever since then it had been a gradual learning experience up till now. I decided that I'd learn advanced computer knowledge so I can work on them whenever I felt like it and didn't have to give it to a repair man every time something went haywire. As of today I've built three computer rigs.  
                
My interests however, span out much farther than just computers. I've always had a passion for off-road racing along with various adrenaline sports. Riding off-road is a love of mine that gives me a sense of freedom. Going the fastest I can go through the roughest terrain gives me a thrill like no other. If I were given the chance to get behind the wheel of an Formula one car or other vehicle, they'd be hard pressed to keep me from pushing it to the max. lol Watching it on TV on the other hand is boring as well as another story.  ](*,)
                
Other hobbies slash interests for me have been history and language. I've always found it to be fascinating looking back at past cultures seeing how people lived, worked and socialized. My interest in the different languages other than my own started when I was still a youngster. It is my goal to be fluent in more than a few languages so I can get insight into past cultures. At the moment I speak basic Japanese, Spanish and Latin. It is a gradual process that I need more discipline at. lol  
                
Animals have brought me comfort over the years with their company. Wildlife is another thing I love to see. I was asked if I had a choice what I could be reincarnated as, what would that be... I said I'd like to be a tiger. The gentlemen I worked with said monkeys. lol Funny enough a woman I knew came up to us not knowing our answers  then said she'd love to be a tiger too. :)
                
That's a few things about myself, so I'll stop here. Glad to be apart of the forums. See you guys around! ):P

### Post by TechSupportx86 on 2011-03-17
Been here for a little while now and thought i might as well say hi!

I was introduced to ubuntu by my brother and never really found a good use for it (being a gamer) untill about a year ago. Currently i still use windows 7 just because i play games and use a few PC-only programs (well, the ones WINE can't run). 

I do however use Ubuntu 10.10 32-Bit Server edition for my Home Server, and 10.10 Netbook Remix on my Acer Aspire one netbook dual-boot with Windows 7... and 10.10 on my "test" computer i use for hard drive recovery and just whatever.

My home server is really where i can see the versatility of ubuntu. I have samba setup to share 4 drives, My Laser Printer, and the front USB panel. It shares all of my music to iTunes and streams movies to pretty anywhere i want. It can be used for torrentflux or a quick apache server, it's amazing how fast you can deploy something with such little effort!

I'm not quite at the point where i can use ubuntu as my main OS (just for gaming and a few other small reasons), but ubuntu is a great compliment to my setup... Without it, i would still be using Windows home server :shock:

### Post by jquest71 on 2011-03-17
):P

New member, first post! Just set up my Samsung netbook for dual-boot with Ubuntu 10.10. I had the issue with the realtek network card, found a solution here. Will ops check tonight on wireless to see if it took, but so far ethernet works well. I really like the layout of Ubuntu Netbook desktop, the OS seems very quick, much more efficient use of system resources than Win7, and I was surprised how many apps and utilities came with the distribution. When I was an intern I did sys admin and performance evals on AIX and TRU-64 Unix, so I'm comfortable in the terminal window and was surprised how quickly most of the commands came back to me. All in all, looking forward to a new, happier relationship with my netbook. Thanks to the community in advance, I'm sure I'll post up some newbie questions soon. Next, figuring out how to install Firefox 4 RC.

### Post by TheScottyTurner on 2011-03-19
Hi all, 
         I'm a newbie Ubuntu user, UNR 10.10 to be specific, and I gotta say I'm enjoying the switch. Currently, I'm going to community college where I'm majoring in Computer Information Systems. Now, here is short, watered-down version of my tale of awakening (in a technical sense lol).Last year, during my first semester, I was sitting in the commons, mindlessly surfing the net when I found an article by the name of *How To Become A Hacker* by Eric S. Raymond. Really quite a motivational piece regardless of what its advertising ;P and that is where I was first introduced to Linux/Unix. From there I searched and searched about Linux, the philosophy, the different, yummy flavors.... it was all very exciting. 
         That was about 7 months ago, I've been very slow in the switch from windows, it's the norm, it's what I've always used, but now, now I have myself some Linux and I'm a proud owner of a dual-booted Acer netbook. I've been able to do a few small thing since the switch like: install Docky, get rid of Unity and go back to Gnome, and transfer all my iTunes music from windows to Banshee, but other than a few small customizable things like that, I haven't done much. Now, I'm no computer expert, I don't know all the jargon (yet!), and, to be honest, I'm not very technical person, but I hope to change all that, I am trying to teach myself, but it's a bit of a jump when you literally have no other computer knowledge than how to check a Facebook and Stumble through sites.... obviously I'm exaggerating a bit there, but the point I'm trying to make is that I'm just a 19 year old guy who's wildly fascinated by computers and I want to learn to actually "use"  them. I can't program or anything like that, but I'm hoping to change that (one reason is because thats what I'm going to school for ;P), and I think Linux is the first step in making all these changes. I read a great quote online not too long ago and it went like this "Linux makes you learn your computer not the OS or programs on it" and I kinda liked that. 

P.S. Sorry about the long post, hope you could make it through it ;D

### Post by kostageas on 2011-03-27
Hi, I'm Kostageas.  I've been using Ubuntu since 8.10 when I installed it on my laptop erasing Windows.  I absolutely hated it because I couldn't figure out how to get the wireless to work.  :p  But then 9.04 got wireless to work, and the intel graphics had problems.  So I had a rocky start.  But I got a new laptop back in February and I'm hooked.  I run 10.04LTS and won't upgrade until 12.04.1LTS just because it would be a pain to get everything working again every 6 months.  

I still have some quirks with Ubuntu, but for the most part it's much better than Windows.  Ever since Ubuntu, I've become a bit more security conscious (aka paranoid) which isn't necessarily a bad thing.  I'm really getting into the activism side of free software, and everyone thinks I'm crazy.  But that's fine by me.

I am 16 years old, male, and a high school sophomore.  I live in the USA, Washington state to be exact.  That's really about all the personal information you'll get out of me.  ;)

I hope to become a part of the larger Ubuntu community, so thanks!!

### Post by AusDisciple on 2011-03-28
Hi everyone. ):P

My name's Dannii and I'm a 39 y/o woman from Geelong, Australia. I'm  totally new to Linux in any form so I figured this would be a great  place to gather some information and learn the ropes.
I'm a musician and sound engineer and have a small project studio I use  to write and record my own music (mostly Christian classic and  progressive rock and electronic) and do recording, mixing and mastering  for others. I play keyboards / piano, guitar, bass, drums and trumpet.  All my music is available for free from my website (there's a couple of  links in my signature for those curious).

I've been using computers now for three decades, starting with a  &#68;&#105;&#99;&#107; Smith system 80 (Z80 CPU) in the  early 80's. I then moved on to the Microbee, then a TRS-80 (grey case  color computer) which I still have, then Apple II, then various x86's, a  Mac G4 400 (which I also still have), a Pentium III, an AMD 3200 64 and  currently a laptop with a T9300 core 2 duo and a recording studio rig  with a Q9450 quad.

So, although computers and various operating systems are not new to me,  Linux is. I'm a pretty quick learner but I am a total Linux newb so  please be gentle with me!!!
The main OS's I've used are Windows, BeOS, Mac OS (8, 9 and 10). I've  also done a reasonable amount of programming starting with machine code  and BASIC on the Z80 to Assembly and BASIC on the TRS-80 in the 80's. 
These days, I mainly work with HTML, Javascript, PHP and WALTER (a theming language for REAPER DAW). 

I'm currently running a triple boot system on my laptop (Win7 32, Win7  64 and WinXP). I've just downloaded the Ubuntu 10.10 32 bit iso and am  going to try booting from a live CD and see how things go.

My main interest in trying out Ubuntu is for video editing, DVD  authoring and image processing. I've been using Adobe Premiere and  Photoshop now for around fifteen years but am really keen to try out  some of the Linux open source options.

Anyway, that's probably enough for now from me.
I look forward to joining in here.

God bless,
Dannii.

thank u 4 your time i hope i can learn here and provide needed info as well - i didnt forget everything.

### Post by sonowilson on 2011-04-09
Hello all, 

My name is Matt. I have been exploring various live distros starting with damn small linux in late 2003. I had an old compaq with a 233mhz PII and it was so cool to have a usable desktop on such an old machine ( and no hard drive to boot).

For a few years I would look at new live cds but I never really installed to hard drive as os or used Linux as main os for any extended period. A couple years I put 9.04 NBR on my eeepc 4g surf and used it a great deal but primarily for web browsing and without really getting into the inner workings of Linux. 

I've been wanting to use Ubuntu more though and also learn more about Linux. I got a great deal on a thinkpad t60 and installed 10.04. I've had systems in the past and currently where I'd install Ubuntu with wubi or dual boot with a separate Ubuntu partition, but this t60 will be the first system with Ubuntu as the only OS installed. I also foresee myself using it a great deal as I rarely use my desktop preferring instead to hang out with my wife (and laptop) in the livingroom. 

So I've spent the last few days setting the laptop up and I figured I'd say hello. I've had my login for a while but I've never really been active on the forums. I see myself browsing and hopefully participating here more in the near future.

Hello!

### Post by drs305 on 2011-04-18
rigel4,

Welcome to the Ubuntu forums.

I've moved your thread into the Introductions sticky. We have a lot of different forums. Some are support-oriented while others are more social (such as the Community Cafe). We have a forum for upcoming releases currently in testing (in Development & Testing), and a lot more.

Your post could be in several forums, but as there wasn't a specific support request I've placed it in the *Introductions* sticky of the Community Cafe.

You can see the different forums available by clicking the "Ubuntu Forums" link at the top left of most pages.

We hope you will enjoy these forums, will be able to obtain help when needed, and provide help when able.

Happy Ubuntu-ing !

### Post by BrianTheComputerWizard on 2011-04-20
Hello,

I guess I'm the new kid in class for the day!  I own a pc support and service business here in [mostly] sunny Phoenix, AZ. As you can probably tell by my user name, my business is Brian, The Computer Wizard.  

I have been a Windows user forever, though I had heard of Linux often.  What finally prompted me to try Ubuntu is a business expansion I am working on. In short I am developing IT packages for political campaigns. I contacted Microsoft to look into volume licensing for office and Win 7. Including their new "rental license" program the total amount of costs added to each machine would have been in excess of $500. This is not acceptable to me or those I will soon be marketing my services to. I knew I had to find a better option that would keep the price point reasonable and, above all, be something that my users (Windows people) would have no trouble adapting to.  Of course saving $500 per machine means I will also be able to donate to Ubuntu in the future once my expansion gets going.

This brought me to Ubuntu/Open Office. I set up my first system this evening (very easy and pain free) and soon be taking it for a spin on the internet after I read up on security and such. 

The eventual plan is to be able to network them together in groups of 2-9 with one shared drive which will backup to the cloud and a shared printer or 2. 

I will probably be around asking lots and lots of questions as I begin to get things up and working. So thank you (in advance) for your patience and assistance. 

Brian

---

### Post by DoFlooterMoose on 2011-04-23
Heya, hiya, ho there everyone ):P Just wanted to introduce myself. I am fairly new to linux. Been working on Windows OS's for the last 4 years. Fixing other peoples computer issues. tweaking them. Cleaning them. Protecting them... etc. etc. I spent almost every waking moment working on em. Its addicting! to say the least. Constantly testing new software, hardware, and anything else i could get my hands on, just to see if I could make it run faster and better. (My exwife said to me the other day "OMFG! Your a nerd!!! After 10 years I never noticed.) You would just have to know me to see the humour in it i guess... anyways,  I swore by Windows 7. I did how ever have a habit of downloading Linix distro after Linux distro. I had 32 all together in the last 2 years. But never did anything with them. Until about a month ago. When I got so fed up with every effort I put into Windows to make it perfect... fell flat. it was like wiping your *** with scented toilet paper ( no matter how many times you use it, it still smells like ****) I couldn't believe I had wasted so much time learning an OS that, all in all, would never be good enough. Would never be perfect. It has no chance to be perfect. Not saying Linux is or will be. But besides the learning curve, Its as close to perfect as I have ever been. So I jumped in head first. completely wiped out Windows. Installed 10.10, and haven't looked back since. I still work on other peoples computers (windows). And try and persuade them back over from the dark side :D. To no avail :(. Wow!!! I can ramble cant I :P. Hope to get to know the community better! You all have a great day.

### Post by frank604 on 2011-04-24
Greetings fellow comrades.  I'm 28, soon to be 29, living in west coast Canada.  I grew up on DOS and migrated to windows later.  The first "wow" moment I had was when windows 95 was released.  And after that, windows remained stagnant in design.  Goodbye teal wallpaper.  I've enjoyed windows for the remainder of my life till now, having experienced little or no issues.  Except the occasional once a year trojan/virus/rootkit/etc.  Remember around 2000 the worm that occurred if you didn't patch windows and went online?  

I've dabbled in linux in the late 90's but I think I was too stupid to actually use it.  I remember a penguin game you slide down the hill on.  

I haven't thought of linux until I got really bored one day (few weeks ago) and decided to try linux out one more time.  I googled around and found Ubuntu.  Wow, this was like opening a new world.  Within this last few weeks, I've installed and tried Fedora, OpenSUSE, Arch with gnome/kde/openbox, Mint, Ubuntu, Kubuntu, Debian.  Most of my free time was just installing and messing around with the OS.  This was beginning to become very addictive.  So I stop'd doing this.  I chose the best distro for me, ubuntu, and decided to stop downloading another distro for at least a week.  

Well, that's me in a nutshell.  Ex-windows, curious, and addictive.

### Post by The Last Samurai on 2011-04-27
Greetings folks, I would like to introduce myself, my in-forum name is "The Last Samurai" but my real world name is Aaron. 

I made a choice to change things up a bit when it came to an Operating System. Not just within my own private life, but rather my professional life as well, I was getting tired of the strangle hold that Microsoft has upon the world, formerly me included. In the beginning I loved Windows, I started back with the Windows 95 O.S., worked my way up the ladder and in the year 2000, I went to college to secure a degree in computer science. While there, I earned my Microsoft MCSE in the Windows 2000 based operating systems. 

From that moment on I was a Windows only type of guy, if it wasn't Windows than it wasn't anything! But after the years had progressed and my work load picked up, and I started mixing and mingling with some of the semi to top names in Microsoft, I started to become uneasy. As the years unfolded I noticed more and more some of the tactics that MS was using upon the world, monopolies, back door deals, questionable take overs of minor companies, propaganda based marketing, intellectual property copies and in the end, charging the regular "joe blows" of the world to much money for an operating system. 

Recently at a friends house, I noticed his computer was on, but his screen didn't look anything like a Windows screen, it looked kind of alien to me! At first I thought he might have been running a Windows customization tool like Window Blinds or Style XP, nope he said, it's Linux Aaron! What, this is Linux? Yes it is, it's the new-ish Ubuntu 10.10 O.S.! He let me sit down and kind of was a back seat driver, while he let me take it out for a test drive. I was actually blown away by several key areas. I was impressed with the level of functionality the system had. Also I was stunned to see an operating system come stock with an actual office suite. 

After my test drive I asked him where he got it, for I'm kind of interested in buying it? He told me there was no need to buy it, for it's free to all who might want it! What, you are kidding right? Nope, it's free he gave me the web address and when I got home I went to the site and loaded it onto a USB flash drive and took some more test drives while letting the O.S. reside on the USB drive. After a couple days I made a choice to release myself from the strangle hold of Microsoft and try to go totally Ubuntu! 

So now after about a solid week of using only Ubuntu 10.10 as my O.S., on both my lap top, my in home server and my primary desktop computer, I'm in love! This isn't to say that I might never go back to Windows, probably not, but at least in the mean time I like what I see and like what I can do with Ubuntu. 

Other than my lead in with what brought me to a Linux based system, more about myself......

I am a single Father of a 16 year old Son, I got 50/50 custody of him about 3 years back during my divorce to my now ex-wife. I live pretty much live alone, other than when I'm enjoying the company of say a girlfriend or two, other than that, it's just me and the kid part time. 

I enjoy a wide variance of things, such as long walks in my local parks, long drive in the hills in my car, hanging out with friends and family, reading books that deal with my spiritual beliefs, I like owning two very nice cats, 9 yr. old male named Smokey and 7 mth. old female kitten named Isis. Also let's not leave out my Son's three old gold fish in a tank in his room, and my new little gold fish in a tank in my room. Oh yeah, there's Ziggy a medium sized tropical turtle. 

Well I think that's enough about me....

It's nice to be here and I enjoy seeing what a Linux based system can bring for me, now and in the future. 

"Thank you for your time here folks"!

---

### Post by jhonan on 2011-04-28
I can't believe I've been googling these boards for so long and only decided to register today.

I'm a cynic. Cold and embittered after 20 years of the IT dev grindstone. No OS satisfies me completely. In fact, the mainstream ones are like the beauty you committed to in your 20s but slowly turned into the marriage from hell.

And there in the distance, always present, beckoning like these mermaids on the rocks, were the distros. "Free!" they cried out to me "Free as in beer!"

Free beer? Mermaids? What more could a man want? One click and an ISO later and that vicious cycle started again. The infighting, the acrimonious splits. This stuff is better than a geeky soap opera.

But really, I love it.

### Post by XubuRoxMySox on 2011-05-07
> **psytech said:**
> Hello, I come from years of using Debian. I finally got tired of minimal, totally custom desktops. I believe Ubuntu is by far the best desktop OS. 

This is so totally refreshing to read! We always read about people who say they've "outgrown" Ubuntu, "graduated" to Debian, "removed the training wheels" by switching to "pure Debian," etc.

It is rare to hear of anyone coming the *other* way! Yet I, too, have "come from Debian to Ubuntu" in a way. Last summer I let my "inner geek" run wild and decided to install and build my own customized, perfect just-for-me Debian mixture. It took several weeks to get it all configured the way I wanted and get all the hardware to work as it should. It was a tedious, grueling process of Googling, searching (and by the way, the folks at Debian Forums don't like kids), and trial-and-error. But here's what gets me:

When I finally, after all that frustration and work, had my perfect Debian/Xfce custom mixture, what I ended up with was almost no different from Xubuntu! I was like, *"Omygosh, I could have done this in 20 minutes instead of two months!"*

I did learn alot and my inner geek was more than satisfied. But I'm a simple kid for whom a computer is like any other household appliance. I want to run *applications*, not the operating system.

Still I'm very grateful for Debian, I think it's awesome, and if it weren't for Debian there's be no Xubuntu (nor Knoppix, Mepis, Mint, and dozens of other adaptations and configurations of Debian's awesomeness. 

Nevertheless it's refreshing and unusual and not a little satisfying to find another user  like me coming to the Ubuntu family *from* Debian instead of the other way around.

-Robin

### Post by Rasa1111 on 2011-05-07
Hiyah Andre!

Welcome to Ubuntu!
I was always a windows user until a year+ ago..
then I got a taste of Ubuntu, and ditched the windows for good!... 
So be careful! ;)   lol

It's ok if you're under-experienced, you should be able to get any help you could need here. :)
We all noobs at least once, Gotta start somewhere!  :)

 Just as a first tip.. 
if you're going to be using your Ubuntu for 'regular' stuff, like multimedia, 
videos, music, dvd's, etc..

 Once you get Ubuntu installed..
Navigate to the "Ubuntu software center" in your Apps. menu 
(unless you're using 11.04?)
and when it opens, Search for "restricted extras" [w/out the quotes]     in the software center search bar , and install it!

 That way you won't have to download and install 'flash', or 'java', or music and video codecs , instead this will install all of them for you at one time, and you'll be set to play anything you can throw at it. 

VLC might also be good to have. :)

I hope you like Ubuntu as much as I! (or even half as much)! lol
Just ask if you need help, there's always people happy to help if they can (and someone always can) lol. :)

Good luck, have fun :)
  <3

p.s.. here's a great PDF for beginning Ubuntu ~ [http://ubuntu-manual.org/downloads](http://ubuntu-manual.org/downloads)
made with "noobs" in mind!

---

### Post by ryanh06 on 2011-05-13
Good afternoon guys, Ryan here. My story with Ubuntu [i had dappled in Red Hat for years before and even Yellow Dog at one point!] started way back in the Jurassic Era around Warty Warthog but wouldn't have a happy after story until 2 weeks ago. I had been dipping my toes into the Ubuntu waters for years now and constantly upgraded from version to version until i got fed up and decided to stick with good ole reliable windows [in a matter of speaking that is *cough* *cough* Trojans and the odd virus aside.] The frustration of those years with hardware drivers that didn’t work, hideous GUIs [IMHO :) ]and the eternal constant need to search for help coupled with a diminishing amount of free time on my part due to college meant that it was a fun side project for me but no way near a viable alternative for Windows.

 Anyways, long story short I downloaded Natty when it launched and installed it via Wubi that way I could play with it and heck uninstall it after a few weeks. But dude was I surprised. Everything just worked, the interface looked gorgeous [yes Unity is still a little raw but its beauty and simplicity rock] the default apps were sensational..... I couldn’t believe how exciting it was and low and behold i haven’t logged into Windows 7 in weeks. In fact i am gonna migrate everything, increase the Ubuntu size, and keep Windows 7 for the games and Office [might install it via Wine haven’t decided yet] Man what a pleasure it has been and i cant wait to continue this amazing journey. Rock on fellow Ubuntinites! :guitar:

### Post by jayp961 on 2011-05-14
[SIZE=2][B]Hi everyone. I am Jean-Paul from Lebanon.

### Post by Triblaze on 2011-05-19
Hello, new here.

17, got a laptop several months ago. I'd used my mom or my dad's computers for a long time, but my mom was usually busy on hers, so I'd use my dad's, which had some peculiar "Linux openSUSE" on it (mom has Vista). I got used to it relatively quickly, as it was set up with a windows-like desktop. Used that a lot, and liked it. Got curious about it, would sometimes google "linux" to see exactly what I was using. I got pretty into it, and when I got my laptop, the first thing I did was forget the pre-installed Windows, and asked my dad to install Linux. He went and got an Ubuntu LiveCD to install, and I've been happily using it ever since.:D

There's my Linux life story, any ways, ever since I got Ubuntu, I've had some stuff I haven't gotten, so I've lurked these forums for months, and finally got an account just a day or two ago. I'm familiar with forums, so I get most of the ToS stuff already (or CoC here, apparently).


Any ways, I'm getting my dad to teach me some programming stuff, and I frequently like to push out and see what I can make Ubuntu do better for me, so I have a feeling it'll be good to frequent here to pick stuff up. And who knows, in a while I may be giving more help than receiving. Sorry if I'm not of much help for now, but hopefully that changes.


Oh, and thanks to Favux, who helped me get my Wacom working. Took a little longer than necessary though, because I forgot to restart X after making changes to the Xorg.conf.#-o



So that's me, see you all around.

### Post by szal on 2011-06-09
Moin folks. szal here from Tschörmeny. ;)

I think I can consider myself a long-time Linux user, having started my experience in late 2003 playing around with Knoppix, then installing Mandrake 9.2rc2. Further experience on the way includes some more versions of Mandrake before the merger with Conectiva, Kanotix 2004 and 2005 versions, Sidux, several Fedora flavours over time (FC1, FC3, FC6, F7, F8, F13, F14), a long line of SUSE versions (SUSE 9.3, 10.1, openSUSE 10.2, 10.3, 11.0, 11.1, 11.2, 11.4), and *buntu (Ubuntu 5.04 ‘Hoary Hedgehog’, Kubuntu 7.04 ‘Feisty Fawn’).

As for the *buntu part of the experience: Hoary didn’t have KDE yet, so I built XFce 4.0 myself when it came out, but found that Hoary’s Gnome interfered with it to the detriment of the user experience, so I ditched it, put Sidux back on and switched to SUSE once I obtained a copy of the 9.3 box DVD. — Feisty was a fine thing overall, but back then it was an outright PITA to obtain current userland software, especially KDE. Nowadays this is easier thanks to the PPA system.

Natty is so far leaving a good impression. I had set it up as a testbed on my other machine, and since it presented itself in good shape there I decided to throw it on my main machine. I won’t let go of openSUSE just yet, though, just in case I might encounter some niggles later I decide I cannot live with. ;)

I might tend not to visit very often as I’m not a web board junkie. But when I do and see something I feel I can contribute to I will. Otherwise I’m most often found back in IRC on Freenode.

### Post by NetworkEngineer on 2011-06-13
Hi all.  My name is Matt, and as my username implies I am a network engineer (primarily Cisco gear). Been working in that field for over 18 years.  I've been using Ubuntu casually (in a VirtualBox VM) since 8.04 Hardy Heron, and made the full time switch to Ubuntu as my primary OS at the beginning of this year (10.10 Maverick Meerkat, now running 11.04 Natty Narwhal).  I am running it on my primary work laptop (Lenovo W510), and did a physical to virtual conversion of my Windows7 HDD to a VirtualBox VM.  I would completely get rid of Windows except (as many who are in the same situation as I likely know) there are some things that simply will not work, nor are their adequate alternatives, under Linux.  At least not for corporate users who must put up with their company's continuing investment in Microsoft (both as a client OS and every app that uses proprietary MS technologies...even for web apps, as well as for Enterprise email).

If it weren't specifically for two items, I would likely be able to live on Ubuntu alone and rarely have to launch my W7 VM.
[LIST=1]
[*]Enterprise MS Exchange Email Client
[*]Netflix OnDemand Streaming
[/LIST]

Unfortunately, I have tried for the last couple years to get Evolution to work (without crashing soon after install and initial sync) with my company's Exchange servers.  It simply does not work adequately, and is probably due to the large amount of email I have (over 300 MB, not including archive PST files) as well as the daily number of emails I receive (between 1K to 2K emails per day) as an IT engineer.

As a Netflix subscriber, it is very disappointing that there is not a solution (that I have been able to find) that will allow me to view Netflix instant streaming video, due to its reliance on Silverlight DRM.  Although I can view most Silverlight content using Moonlight, the DRM functionality is not (and will likely never be) available for  Linux.  My only hope here are rumors that Google is working with Netflix to develop the capability to do Netflix streaming using HTML5 for their ChromeOS, and thus will (hopefully) be available on their Chrome browsers for all OS'es, including Linux.

I'm using a linux thin client to boot the bootfloppy and load ubuntu kernel. I'm using my existing Windows dhcp to send the boot floppy and configured following dhcp options
 
017 Root path:/opt/ltsp/i386
066 Boot Server Host name:<my ltsp server ip>
067 Bootfile name:ltsp/i386/pxelinux.0
 
When I'm booting my linux thin client. The client gets the ip from my dhcp server and loads the boot floppy from tfp server and it stays at Trying to load:pxelinux.cfg/default just after the line tftp prefix:ltsp/i386 ok.
 
Please help me to find out where I'm going wrong.
 
Many thanks to all the community members. Sorry for long post. I'm little excited as this is my first post.

### Post by OldBoy44 on 2011-06-29
Hi all,

I feel a little embarrassed introducing myself now as I have been around since December, 2010. I am a 67 year old pensioner hailing from that land "down under". My original OS was Wubi 10.04 but it didn't take much of a play around to convince me that I wanted to go the whole way and install a dedicated Ubuntu OS.
In January I installed 10.04 from a disk that I received in a Ubuntu book that I purchased. I was not then (and still aren't) very clued up and as a result had a few mishaps - that is when these forums jumped to the forefront of my needs, With the assistance of such helpers as plucky, Old_Gray_Wolf, dandnsmith and seawolf167 I was able to able to resolve my problems and hopefully learn a little on the way. My thanks to all the helpers and moderators in the forums.

I was very satisfied with the gnome desktop however when 11.04 came along I was very much tempted to go for an upgrade even though my knowledge was limited. My old computer did not support Unity so I made a major decision for me and purchased a new computer. I made a decision to follow the path of future Ubuntu upgrades even though at present I am using Classic mode. I plan to progress to Unity as I get more confidence in the OS. I see this progress as a challenge(in my old age) with the understanding that I can choose another system if things don't work out.

Anyway, that is enough of a rave by me - I would like to finalize this post by saying that I think these forums are the best I have seen - by a long way! 

Cheers, :D

### Post by Hakaron on 2011-07-05
Hi everybody,

I just noticed that I hadn't introduced myself yet ](*,), so here I go:
I'm 26, from Belgium and recently picked up (K)Ubuntu again. Before that, I've been using Arch for a while as a secondary OS, since most games in retail stores don't say "Games for Linux" on top of their boxes:(. I had been toying around with Ubuntu before (8.10 IIRC), but never really used for much more than trying something new back then.

Anyway, since I've grown quite attached to KDE in my Arch days, but wanted to start using Linux more often without too much initial research, I decided to move to Kubuntu about a week ago. So far, I'm loving it and have only rebooted in Windows a few times, mainly to update my WoW addons (wine really is getting better at d3d performance) and to avoid having to download a gazillion updates all at once at the end of the summer, when I'll have to use it again:frown:

Apart from playing around with Linux, my hobbies are slacking (more of a lifestyle really:wink:), gaming, watching movies/series (especially sci-fi and fantasy), fooling around and the web and if I find the time, learning programming. I am currently taking an after-hours class (or whatever "avondschool" translates into) on programming, and recently passed for the second year of the three, so I'm almost there. It's mostly .NET stuff and some Java, but I enjoy it and it might smoothen the curve for when I decide to start learning some Linux programming:D

That's about it, nice to meet all of you and I'll see you around on the forums.
And now it's time to "test" VLC's performance on my Star Trek colllection..:popcorn:

### Post by UltraNEO* on 2011-07-11

I'm Akira but my net friends tend to call me Neo (aka ultraneo) from England. Over the years I've used various platforms for various reasons, so I'm not really your open source guru or your regular 'switcher' but there's potential. 

Outta pure curiosity I recent took a trial of Ubuntu 10.10, installed as a VM (knowing it's completely safe) hosted on Parallels running under MacOSX after it's initial install, long session of playing and basic exploration, I figured how to install tweaks and utilities.

### Post by chili555 on 2011-07-14
Good morning, gals and guys! I don't know how I missed introductions, but I'm finally here! I started in Linux about ten years ago in Mandrake 7.3. That's so long ago that few here even know what that is! As I like to tell my friends, that was when Linux was actually hard! Throughout my Linux experience, I have, out of curiosity and the urge to learn new things, downloaded live CDs and now live USB images. Several years ago, I came across Ubuntu and liked it so much that I installed it on one of my four computers. Gradually, it has swallowed all four! My wife loves her System 76 Meerkat running 10.04LTS.

I try to contribute over on the Networking and Wireless forum. It is my great joy to get a reply that says, "Thanks, it works." That's almost as good as a paycheck...almost...

I always get a big smile when someone says they are an ancient user because they are some ridiculous age like 50. I will not reveal my age precisely, but I have been retired for 13 years and have a great-grandson. Trying to help new users get wireless going is a fun and usually quite rewarding way to spend some of my retirement leisure.

### Post by gippeswyc on 2011-08-09
Hi - I'm Chris & like a fair few have tried various distributions of Linux in the past & finished up going back to Windows, until now! I currently have loaded Ubuntu 11.04 64-bit (classic desktop - better IMHO).

Why did I change? Well, I don't know about anyone else but I was getting fed up of the resource hog Windows seems to be these days, long pauses, hangs etc. I was sick and tired of looking at the egg timer & hearing my HD thrashing about even to perform the simplest of tasks!

Since using Ubuntu things are zipping along nicely & one thing I don't here is my HD thrashing about as it did in Windows. Everything is so much more responsive.

I'm having a few issues with 64-bit, drivers etc but I am prepared to be be patient and seek the solutions. It kind of reminds me of the days when I use to fiddle and tweak with DOS 3.X in the early 1980s well before Windows came along. It's a steep learning curve for a 59 year old to get to grips with a lot that is behind the OS but I am going to hang on in there.

The developers deserve high praise indeed for getting to a point where they can credibly take on Windows and the fact that it does not cost us shouldn't be forgotten. If one of them happens to look in then thanks :D

---

### Post by magic8ball on 2011-08-09
Hi all,

I've used Windows for years and still do (sticking with XP until the very end).  About 1.5 years ago I installed Ubuntu (9.10) and have been tinkering with it a bit, but still mostly used XP.  I don't really care for MS and Windows is quite bloated, so at home I've decided to start using Ubuntu primarily and XP when I need to do something specific.  I love how Ubuntu is so snappy and clean compared to Windows.  I'm planning to install VirtualBox and check out the latest Ubuntu (and maybe other things) for the heck of it sometime in the future.

To that end, I recently upgraded my system from 10.04 32-bit to 64-bit, resized my partitions and formatted / as ext4.  I imaged with Clonezilla, resized with GParted and reinstalled Ubuntu.  Everything went fairly smoothly.  Thanks to /home all of my settings transferred over and my new system came up looking just like my old.  I was very impressed by how this whole process went.  Ubuntu is just so much more friendly than Windows.

In addition, the contributors on this forum are great.  I've solved my few issues and learned many things from searching here.  Thanks to all!

- Chuck

---

### Post by geekygirlau on 2011-08-10
Hi all - used to be a member here many moons ago but went the way of the Apple tree ;)
 
Getting back into Ubuntu and loving the changes I have seen since I last used it back in 2009. My first distro was Hoary Hedgehog...so I have loved seeing how much it has grown since then :D
 
Basically I am currently at a fork in the road where my dual core Macbook Pro will no longer support anything post Lion, I dont really want to fork out for a new MBP, travel has me wanting just a netbook or ultra portable, and I am really getting bored with my iPhone4 and looking to get back to Android - so why not start looking at going the whole other end of the spectrum into an open source operating system as well....so back here to read and learn again.
 
I read and learnt a lot in these forums several years ago :)
 
Not really a computer type geek, a space-geek though! I have seen 3 Shuttle launches in the last 12 months, 2 Shuttle 'scrubs', an Atlas launch and a Delta IV as well ;)
 
Spend most of my time 'commuting' between Australia, the Space Coast in FL and Canada, usually every 8 weeks or so lol

### Post by scooby1970 on 2011-08-10
Hi there.

My name is Mark aka Scooby1970. I'm a user of Ubuntu 11.04 and Pinguy 11.04 and over the last few months I have moved myself and the business I work for away from Windows and are now 100% Linux based.

Many people ask why we choose Linux, and for me its the freedom you have and the choice of software you can install. I also choose Linux because it just works no matter whats wrong with your PC! My PC always dies before any Linux distros I throw at it.

I run a new web blog aimed at new users of Linux, its seen quite a few people visit it but not too many people commenting. Yes, I enjoy Linux so much that I write about it as often as I can. :) Mark

### Post by Erik1984 on 2011-08-18
> **justin_acklin said:**
> Hi there, in a previous career I was a linux/solaris sysadmin, these days I work as a vet tech while going to nursing school. My first linux install was in 95 using slackware 0.93! text based only install, cut my teeth on that one. I'm very rusty, I've not done linux professionally in a decade. 

My first question is, how to change my forum screen name? I guess I registered with my real name as my screen name some time in the long past, and I'd like to change it but it doesn't appear to be an option in the UserCP. If I try to register a new account, it says my email address is already in use. Can anyone help with this?

You can't change the name, mods can do that in exceptional cases (if for example using your real name puts you in danger). Normally you have to create a new account and ask if the mods can deactivate your old account. Maybe you can reuse the email address when the old account gets deactivated.

### Post by Buelercuber on 2011-08-19
Hello everybody! my name is buelercuber and i have pretty much a FOB when it comes to Ubuntu, but so far I've had problems , but it's been weighed out by things that I've accomplished.  So far I've only had Ubuntu for one day really(because i screwed the install the first time, by making two partitions and installing Ubuntu twice :fp, but then after that i cleaned the partitions and made it nice and clean :)) but through out this one day I've really customized my stuff, and i am loving everything about it, well not everything, except for the confusion parts, like the terminal. and installing stuff. all the problems I've had have been hard to fix, but in the end i fixed them.  a of right now i am currently working on how to play steam games on it, and i can't seem to make it work with wine....i'll probably find out later this week XD

but anyways this is really what i did today in order to where i am now.

1.re-partitioned the two partitions into one Ubuntu partition :)
2.installed Ubuntu easily.
3.started to get my compiz effects going down, wiggly windows :D
4. got some nice programs that i use on my windows 7 boot
5.pretty much after that i just started to customize stuff, like aero snap, compiz fusion, and moving the exit buttons across XD
6. tried to make my second monitor work, couldn't make it work. read that i needed to go to my ATI control center, and made it work
7, finally fine tuned compiz fusion, and am currently working on the games right now :)

oh god that was alot, 
oh and one last note: i am in love with Nixiepixel :) 

hope i have fun in this forum :)

### Post by sanderd17 on 2011-08-27
If you have problems, we are here to help. But there is no point in trying to help someone without problems.

Maybe some little tips:

[LIST]
[*]Always search for software in the software center first
[*]when you read a tutorial, check the date. Open source software is moving so fast that an article from 2009 is probably completely outdated.
[/LIST]

And if you are here to learn, you could learn the linux command line: [http://linuxcommand.org]("http://linuxcommand.org"). This is not necessary, but it will help you in understanding how Linux works.

**EDIT: this was in reply to [http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11192094&postcount=859](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11192094&postcount=859) who started a separate thread. But since threads are merged, my post seems a bit lost in here.**

### Post by SiathLinux on 2011-08-29
Hi - funny thing is that I've been some form of Linux since RH6.0 was 'fresh'....

Anyway, I'm Randal Lovelace aka SiathLinux (I've had this 'online ID' for nearly as long as I've been using Linux).

I have two computer currently (both old and crappy) and happily both are running Ubuntu (laptop with 6.10 and a desktop with 8.04)

I've run Windows since 3.1 (95/98/SE/XP/Vista/7) and I've run RH6.0, Mandrake 7.x, Mandrake 10.x, Mandriva 'ONE', Ubuntu (several builds), Kubuntu (several builds), MintLunux (version based on Ubuntu 10.10), ArtistLinux (good grief too much.... but wow)...

Unfortunately, I my OS tends to outlast my hardware.... (too many dead motherboards/cpu/ram to talk about.

I'm also and artist - (see DeviantART - same user name)  - and use Gimp and Blender

I've installed Ubuntu (of various builds) on over 60 different computers with all types of 'older' hardware - and find that it usually (98%) finds and install all of the hardware with minimal fuse - I found that it also finds added hardware fairly nicely too and installs it.

Hopefully, I can help people that need it and maybe get help when I finally get a 'new computer' ... 

Cheers

### Post by sanderd17 on 2011-09-04
> **Vikashkr111 said:**
> Hello people, Just downloaded and installed Ubuntu 11.04. Have been using winxp nd server 2003 till nw. Installed Ubuntu bcuse I need to learn Unix. Forum threads seem to suggest this is a great OS to work on. 

Will share my experience as I learn more abt it.

Ubuntu is aimed at desktop usage. If you want to use Linux as server, you'll better go with an all-around distro like Debian, CentOS, scientific Linux or a bunch of other distros. Check [distrowatch]("http://distrowatch.com") to find good server distros.

Because Ubuntu is aimed for normal desktop users, that also means it's easier to set up though.

### Post by doc taz on 2011-09-08
Hi folks. I'm new to Ubuntu, but not to Linux. I've used Linux since 1999, on and off, starting with an old 486 laptop. Mainly I've used Debian and Slackware, along with some distributions based on those two variants. For the last few years, I've been using Vector Linux, which is Slackware based. 

Lately, I haven't felt comfortable with their release cycle, so I sought to look for something better to use. I used to have the time and patience to take apart distributions to make them run faster and more efficiently, but I don't have that kind of time anymore. An exhaustive search led me to Ubuntu. I noticed that you guys have a version with xfce. I loaded it about a month ago, in a revamped 4-year-old hp laptop that also runs WIndows 7 Professional. (Interestingly, shortly after I loaded xubuntu, I noticed that Linus Torvalds moved away from Gnome and KDE, and is now using xfce. I swear, I didn't know that until about a week later! Coincidence? :))

So far, I haven't built anything from source yet, but I hope to try it soon. My continuing interest in Linux is further lined up with getting an Android phone a few months ago. I'd love to hack up a little program for that platform, if I can ever find the time for it. In any case, I use Linux for all the usual stuff (productivity, games, etc.), but am now using it a lot to run programs related to electronic engineering. I also do a lot of photo editing via GiMP.  

This distro is certainly the best I've used so far. It runs as well (and sometimes better) as the WIn 7 install. Certainly, it's better on resources, and I haven't had anything crash on it. I want to build a line up of some multimedia software to use for audio recording, so that's the next step. I know I could use Ubuntu Studio, but I want a leaner setup.

This seems to be a great international community that's built up in the last few years. I'm glad you guys chose Debian as your base. Anyway, I'm done rambling.

---

### Post by JTNeel on 2011-09-13
Hello everybody. I'm a new user here and just wanted to send out a proper hello to everybody. I am **brand new** to the Linux / Ubuntu environment as of 2 days ago.  I have been in the computer industry since 1991. i have had my own business as a Computer and Networking Specialist since 2002. I started out in the days of DOS and Windows 3.0. I have pretty much used, at one time or another, just about all of the most popular OS's. DOS, Unix, Xenix. I have only recently started using Apple computers and I am slowly weeding out the Windows computers. 

I just finished my first install of Ubuntu 10.04 which took me 2 - 3 days to get my network settings right. Apparently using static IP's and Static DNS's  is not as easy as it first appears. In any event, I'm up and running and probably will be filled with questions soon.

So be prepared. see you soon.
Thank you
JTNeel (Jim)

### Post by oscarivera9 on 2011-09-13
Hello everyone!

I'm still dual booting with Windows, primarily because I am still trying to find great ways to record music with Linux.  I am a musician and music was actually the reason I first started getting into computers.  I have been using Ubuntu Studio for about half a year now and I absolutely love it, but I still haven't been able to do the same things with Audacity(open source recording software) that I've been doing with Reaper (shareware recording software).  
Anyway, I just built a new PC and I did my homework prior to building to make sure that it was 100% Linux friendly.  I've already convinced my brother (who's looking into buying a laptop)  to use Ubuntu, as well as my sister, and even one of my guitar students.
I am so glad I finally started using the Forums as they are much, much easier to use than Launchpad, which is what I've using primarily when I've run into problems.  Don't get me wrong, Launchpad is great, but I just find Ubuntu Forums easier to use and with a faster response time.
Greetings to all, and I look forward to being part of this wonderful community!

I am excited![/SIZE]

Welcome,

the learning is the most fun part of Linux. I'm still learning about Linux every day.

In Kenya you have lots of pirated installations right? Those are certainly insecure. 

But you should also know that not every laptop is supported as good as yours. Some have problems with their graphical card, or with their wireless card.

Anyway,

happy ubuntu-ing

### Post by Jaibo on 2011-09-22
Hi all, I'm Jai and I'm (nearly) new to Ubuntu/Linux. I did install Ubuntu on my desktop PC sometime last year, but being an avid gamer I tended to still use Windows (is that word allowed here, heh) a lot. 
I've just formatted the Wifes laptop though, as her installation of Vista was choking up, and becoming frustrating to use. I suggested she tried Ubuntu, as she mainly uses the laptop for browsing ... or should I say shopping!
We've had Natty Narwahl installed for a few days now, and she''s really impressed with the efficiency. She couldn't believe how fast the laptop booted up & shut down!
I've set her up with a dual boot of Natty and Win 7 (both 64 bit, since I also just upped her RAM from 2GB to 4GB), although it defaults to Ubuntu when booting.

I am still a complete Ubuntu noob though, and I've browsed a little around the 'New to Ubuntu? Start here' section. Sometimes Ubuntu seems quite complicated to me, although a little searching the 'net usually shows me easy options for tasks I'm trying to undertake.

First impression :- Ubuntu looks functional and stylish, and seems very stable and fast. Hopefully I can progress and learn a lot more about this OS, because I like it a lot thus far.

**One question for now :-**

### Post by oscarivera9 on 2011-10-09
> **RememberWhenItRained said:**
> Hello,
 I'm not brand new, but I have yet to introduce myself. I am a recent (~6 mos.) Linux convert from Windows (funny how that happens, eh?) and Ubuntu addict. I run Lynx, hopefully Ocelot when it's released though i don't know about Unity (i need some reassurance there...) and dual boot with win7. I'm also just playing around with Back Track 5 recently.

I am a second year college student who is an information systems major. Though i don't know yet what my focus will be, I have interests in Computer security & pen. testing, and digital forensics. Also interested in learning about the difference of Linux flavors.

Nice being here, I love this community.

Over & out.
Hello, and welcome.  Well you wrote a couple of things that I can definitely identify with.
First off, you are not alone in being a bit dubious about Unity.  The longer I read different threads here in the forums, the more I find others like us who have not been impressed much with Unity.  I at first didn't like it at all and so instead I kept using the good old Gnome desktop.  Last three weeks I've decided to give Unity another try and I must admit to not hating it anymore, yet I still cannot say I like it.  The main reason I've decided to give it another try is that Gnome 3 is apparently similar in many ways to Unity, so I guess it's something we should get used to.  In fact, even Windows 8 seems to be headed in the same direction with their Metro desktop environment.  The basic idea behind it all is to accommodate mobile computing devices.  I honestly don't understand why the desktop has to change just because of tablets and notebooks and stuff.  
Anyway, I'm also kind of curious as to Back Track 5.  I've never tried it, but I've heard some good things about it.  For someone like you who's into computer security I would imagine Back Track would be right up your alley.  Good luck and welcome to the wonderful world of Linux.  I am newbie as well, but I've been around for a little while longer than you and I also love it!

### Post by Lisiano on 2011-10-10
So. Since I'm no longer passively looking at the Ubuntu forums, guess I'll introduce myself too. Don't be mistaken by my join date, July 8th, 2010. I started using Ubunt... I mean Kubuntu since 9.10, good times. Yet I haven't started using the forums actively until this September.
My first ever ever non-desktop Linux encounter was with my Buffalo NAS which I gave SSH and played on it, made it beep, blink LED's, simulate a PSU Error code with the exact beeping. I was a very happy kid then.
Currently I don't have my NAS with me anymore (Sadface) but it's still mine and just being lent to my dad (Happyface).
My encounter with Linux was more or less painless until my old P4 died, we had very fun times and been together for a long time. Requescat in pace my dear friend. After that I started using my backup laptop without a battery, slow as hell, then I encountered more Linux when I tried to use Ubuntu (Failed) and DSL (Win). But as a win, I got a new PC, installed Kubuntu on it, played, then installed ubuntu-desktop and moved to Ubuntu, played for a while and recently (A few weeks maybe) moved back due to the Unity thing. I'm eagerly waiting for the next release. Also some time ago, maybe half a year, I inserted my old HDD and booted off it on a PC that had NOTHING that my old PC had, everything was different, you people probably can guess but I was shocked and became deeply in love with Linux since that day.
To this day, I converted a few people to Linux, use Linux in school on my netbook, administer a Linux server, Android phone with root and SSH.
I have to admit that Linux did a bad thing to me, I can't stop thinking about non-computer stuff now. Before I could stop sometimes but now I can't. Always thinking about stuff.
Well all in all, Ubuntu is great, hope everything stays great. Wish everyone luck with their systems. And don't make the mistake of falling in love with Linux, you'll be sucked into the Linux world even more then. (Not a bad thing but quite time-consuming)

---

### Post by alco75 on 2011-10-19
Also trying to break out of lurking. :)

After a few failed attempts of getting my 56k modem drivers working on a very early Red Hat from around 1999, I gave up on Linux 'til Spring 2010, just before Lucid came out. At the time, I'd changed programming jobs from mostly ActionScript to mostly PHP, and wanted to programme on LAMP instead of using nasty Adobe products on Windows. I was very impressed at how relatively production-ready a modern Ubuntu distro was compared to my much earlier Linux experiences, so stuck with it. And now, my entire team of seven permanent PHP programmers are all on Ubuntu! :D

Away from work, my Thinkpad Edge 11 now runs Xubuntu Oneiric like a dream. I can even do such advanced things as watch iPlayer fullscreen at an acceptable framerate (something that wasn't previously possible on Unity).

### Post by K-Ghidorah on 2011-10-27
Greetings Ubuntu-ites :).  I've posted a couple of times here before, once with an issue and another time just to add my $0.02, but I neglected to properly introduce myself as per proper forum protocol.  Sorry about that.

Anyhoo, I should probably tell my story about how I discovered Ubuntu (and Linux in general).  Like most, I had grown frustrated with Windows and the fact that it was causing my old Thinkpad (which was given to me by a dear friend of mine when my Gateway PC's motherboard fried) to run excruciatingly slow.  Not to mention the fact that the drivers for my wireless adapter were failing.  I then remembered hearing about Linux over 10 years ago and decided to see what it was all about.  Found Ubuntu, but couldn't get it to work on my old hardware.  Fortunately, a coworker (the IT guy at the place I work) had a spindle of Linux CDs.  I tried Ubuntu 9.10.  Other than a few bugs here and there and an initially frustrating lack of codecs, I was impressed with it.

The fact that it and most of its software are free didn't hurt at all ;).  To end that story, it was running Linux Mint 9 XFCE when it finally gave up the ghost (it was in BAD shape when I got it).

Since then, I've shown Ubuntu, Linux Mint, and Zorin OS to friends and family, particularly those who complain about their computers running slow and express the "need" to get a new one.  I've managed to convert three people, including my mother (she's using Linux Mint 10... Ubuntu would probably be too alien for her).

Anyways, that's it!
------------------------------------------------
(TL;DR:  Ubuntu rocks)

---

It is the target, not the medium which is important. Successful are those who focus on target, not on the medium or obstacles. ### Post by Glynnux on 2011-10-28
Hi everyone. (nee how YaNan Lu  :)  )


I recently removed the dual boot from my laptop and installed Natty on its own. I intend to give the lappy to my mum for browsing and emails and decided she would be happy with the simple look and speed of Unity.

I have Natty 11.04 on my own laptop and have set permissions so I can access her install to help out when required.

I personally liked Unity at first glance but I'm now using the Classic option as it's more familiar (I was doing okay until I couldn't remember the name of something I wanted to "search"). However, a restart of Ubuntu is so quick it could be possible to switch between the two at will.

I still have Windows 7 on my main gaming pc for recording, sequencing and my two fave flight sims but it does get weeks of neglect these days being in the 'spare room'. :)

Ubuntu is certainly my first love for general use. :)

Been around for a long time but resolve most issues with "search/ctrl,alt,T/copy/paste...... fixed/happy/exit

### Post by Tutoka on 2011-10-31
My name is Tutoka. My IQ is about  95. I took a courage and installed Ubuntu on my HP Pavilion dv6 with an  intent ot have a dual boot with my Win 7. Of course I ruined the  HP/Windows boot capacity and instead of dual boot I am stuck with  Ubuntu. Maybe forever now, who knows... I think that Ubuntu and Linux in general should approach not only the geeks, nerds,  saints, the people with high moral attitudes and principles.It should be  forthcoming to people who do not have time to play with the settings,  it should meet the needs of mentally challenged, the lazy, the  Windows-spoiled, the mediocre ones, the pure consumptionists et cetera...  The humanity means not to worship geniuses, to despise the ordinary on  the other hand and thus create a system good only for an elite. The  humanity means to make a system understandable to dummies and mentally  handicapped as well as to keep it in a capacity of advanced adjustment  in case it gets in to hands of real genius. I like to see Ubuntu growing and gaining a higher level of responsible behavior and maturity...

### Post by Barney-R on 2011-11-03
Hello everybody. I'm in the South-East corner of England and have been using Ubuntu (10.10 Maverick) exclusively since March or April this year.

Asus P6X58D-E Motherboard
i7 Processor
6GB RAM
300 GB hard drive
1 TB second hard drive
2 DVD re-writers
Ubuntu ONLY - no dual-boot or $eattle $pyware for me!

I still struggle with a few things, but I'll get there. I like the way everything (meaning software) is either pre-installed or just a few mouse clicks away.

The thing I don't like about this forum is that it's too popular, with hundreds of pages on each thread, making it difficult to find what I'm looking for.

At least it's better than the newbies' forum at [www.ubuntux.org/](www.ubuntux.org/), where there seems to be one resident knowledgeable person and hardly anyone else, so I can't really complain.

### Post by viperdvman on 2011-11-04
Well, I've been here since June (a month after I installed Natty), but never introduced myself.

I'm ViperDVMan (pronouced Viper 'da V-man). I'm just a working man, about to get back into school. I'm also a big time car nut, gamer, anime fan, and computer nerd. I have 2 computers (desktop and netbook). Desktop runs Windows XP and Ubuntu 11.04, and the netbook runs Windows 7 Starter and Ubuntu 11.04.

I've known about Linux for eons, but I actually started dipping my toes into it after the New Year this year, mainly playing with Lucid Lynx on a LiveUSB. I tried Natty on a LiveUSB as well (despite hearing about Unity's woes with some of the community), and decided to install Natty on the desktop in May and the netbook in June. Ever since then, I run Ubuntu far more than I run Windows on both computers.

I also like to play with other distros as well, most of them on LiveUSB's. I do set aside another partition (may set aside a second one too) for playing around with full installs of other distros. I'm liking playing with Linux (especially Ubuntu), a lot.

"Being a father is often very lonesome," he says, "And 1 of the things I feel is I am no longer unattended, that the Council of Dads is in each room with me and production every determination with me." The remedial crisis that led Feiler to reach out to the other fathers has passed. Today, Feiler is cancer-free. But he says his idea has caught ashore, and he believes that his book, The Council of Dads, and the website councilofdads.com is inspiring alike groups to form wherever dads quest strength in the fellowship of fathers.Related articles&#65306;      [&#12288;&#12288;&#25105;&#22909;&#24819;&#20320;](http://gogo161.com/viewthread.php?tid=2191&pid=53878&page=10&extra=page%3D1#pid53878)     ["A lot of the money namely mismanaged](http://www.forum.yachtmotorserwis.pl/index.php?topic=30050.msg34647#msg34647)     [](http://yangmaosheng.w157.91kweb.com/bbs/showtopic-11485.aspx)   "It is with great sadness and a heavy heart that I have decided to end my six-year marriage to Ashton. As a woman, a mother and a wife there are certain values and vows that I hold sacred, and it is in this spirit that I have chosen to move forward with my life. This is a trying time for me and my family, and so I would ask for the same compassion and privacy that you would give to anyone going through a similar situation," she said in her statement to the AP

### Post by guyver_dio on 2011-12-11
Hey I'm Billy or guyver_dio, whichever you want to call me

I'm a systems developer for a university. I've used ubuntu several times in the past but never really stuck with it and mostly used windows. I found previous ubuntu releases were still too technical for average use. I found myself constantly in the terminal trying to get things to work rather than actually doing what I was trying to do.... wasting alot of time. 

I've decided to switch back to ubuntu from windows 7 because I just wanted a light weight, quick and easy OS. Windows 7 looked like bubbly glossy **** and was a resource hog. It came installed with all this stuff never required for a desktop pc and stuff I'd never need to use, taking up alot of space and after seeing what windows 8 is gonna be like I decided it's time to **** this microsoft **** off.

Ubuntu takes up less space, uses less memory and is compeltely configurable, meaning I can remove all the stuff I know I'll never need or use making it even more light weight. I've found the new interface and new software center to be very good. I find myself needing to be in the terminal less and can do most of what I want through the interface. So I think I'm sticking with ubuntu this time and feels great to support open source projects and willing to give feedback whenever needed. 

I don't think ubuntu is ready for the average user yet but it's getting alot closer.

### Post by Atomic-Fanboy on 2011-12-24
"Currently running Ubuntu 10.04 learning the command line from a book I've bought"

That's a very sensible idea. Not only are you learning how to use the system, you are also learning how and why the system works. I have some friendly advice for you though:
- CLI Companion is a terminal emulator with a command list of common commands. You can customise the list if you come across anything obscure but useful.
- Man pages can be pretty useful. 
- GNU Midnight Commander is a text mode file manager cloned from Norton Commander by Miguel de Icaza.
- NEVER RUN sudo rm -rf /. It will destroy your operating system. In fact try to avoid rm -rf completely if possible - and be careful if you need to use it.
- Most important - Have fun and think about how lucky you are. GNU/ Linux is the best operating system around, and it's completely free.

Atomic-Fanboy

---

### Post by kalfusisagod on 2011-12-24
@Atomic-Fanboy,

Yeah the CLI is great, it's not as hard as I thought but some man pages are a bit cryptic. There is so much you can do from the CLI and yeah can even SSH into your own box while away from home and manage your system without a high speed data connection. I've already put the CLI to good use. I have a 3 TB hard drive with thousands of movies and wanted a document with all the movie titles. So i hooked up my drive and browsed to it to the command line and did a 

```
 ls > Movie_listing.txt 
```
 
and this put my Movies into a document that I can email or print out from the GUI. I just couldn't figure out how to make ls drop the extensions (I even used ls --help or the man ls page). If I couldn't port the ls to a document, I'd have to spend hours typing my thousand movies.

### Post by HeroOfCanton on 2012-01-04
Guess I have to start talking so I can edit my profile someday... I'm a full time geek (consultant). Build, repair, connect, and write PC/web applications for Windoze systems. Whatever pays. Please don't throw rocks!

Recently got the itch to expand my horizons and decided to move deeper into the world of open source and Linux programming. I've had various distros installed on one computer or another for years but never did much other than browse the web, play games, or setup home servers/intranets. It's time to get more hard core.

In the last couple of week I've:
- Converted laptop to Xubuntu 11.10 from Ubuntu 10.10 (choices in desktops, how sweet freedom is!)
- Converted media center to Ubuntu 11.10 from XP
- Started moving Java/Droid programming projects to Linux (Eclipse)
- Confirmed I can do RDP and other essential networking functions from Linux when desired.

Hoping to eventually:
- Master Apache server and leave IIS (and the costs that come with it)
- Master PHP and leave Coldfusion (and the costs that come with it)
- Convert my entire home/business to Linux and leave Windows (and the costs that come with it)
- Make my own contributions to the Linux community and software

I promise to someday pay back all of the questions I'm sure to ask in the near future. :wink:

I like the Calibre ebook software, and The GIMP, but I've tried most of the popular open-source software, and most of it is useful...  I like using the Gnome-Terminal.  My hobby is playing with CSS to redo blog designs, esp. at LiveJournal and WordPress...  I have cats, and I love old books and tea.  I used to keep a saltwater aquarium, which is totally irrelevant, but it makes me a bit less boring.

Um, I guess that's it.  I'm not very interesting, but I try to be kind of helpful.

### Post by Elfy on 2012-01-13
> **shizz said:**
> Ah yes I fully understand this but is there any way to automatically subscribe to a thread when you post in it? I can't find an option so I presume I can't change it, but Im getting lost on all of the threads.

If not at the top of all threads - Thread Tools - use the subscribe to this thread option

### Post by mörgæs on 2012-02-04
It is probably because Microsoft pre-installs Windows 7 in a way that hinders dual booting. Don't worry, there is a way around.

This thread is not meant for support questions, so I recommend that you do a live boot, open Gparted and grab a picture of the present partitioning scheme. Post this in a new thread in General Help (or search for threads dealing with a similar problem).

### Post by rm1024 on 2012-02-04
Hey everyone, just wanted to introduce myself quickly as I suspect I might become a frequent visitor.  I just installed 11.10 today having never run Ubuntu before.  It blew me away that literally the **only** difficulties I had were with a video card driver (easily fixed by allowing the 3rd party drivers to be installed).  Other than this, everything is seamless to transfer from windows.  No problems with the install and having windows and other partitions, i can access my NTFS partitions flawlessly.  My worst complaint was battling with a couple of pesky services running in windows that werent letting me defrag properly and hence keeping my partition shrink less than i wanted.  but they got worked out after a few ours of messing about.
  i&#314;l tell you, it was a HECK of alot nicer install than the last time I did a linux install (slackware 3.0 IIRC) I will say that.  Anyway, just noticed some of the noob questions while doing some perusing and wanted to let y´all know not everyone has trouble when they are doing a new install.  To all you other noobs like me; google is your friend and read carefully.
one more thing, having a package manager makes installing stuff so much nicer.  no more command line compiling source! yahoo!
sorry folks, been working on this migration all day and its really late in these parts. time for rest and more play tomorrow.
Thanks!

### Post by rrtobin on 2012-02-08
Hello all.  I am new here and pretty new to Ubuntu.  I keep trying to get involved and convert my family off of apple and microsoft products to make my house completely open source but keep getting pulled back to the dark side.  This is my third attempt to convert my wife and kids to open source to get them off of the corporate teet.
 
I was recently on TDY and dropped by a buddys house.  He introduced me to media center in Win7.  He ripped all of his DVDs onto 3 2Tb drives and put the machine into his entertainment center with wireless keyboard/mouse.  He runs HD across HDMI to his 42" plasma and the sound through 5.1 Dolby surround on his theater system.  Looked and sounded awesome!
 
Before I left he donated me a broke SR1750NX to build my media center.  In my time of troubleshooting I finally discovered the 320G SATA drive had some indexing issues and caused the machine to run super slow if at all.  It took me two days to get Ubuntu 11.10 to load on it and it was super sluggish.  Last night I picked up a 250G SATA and had Ubuntu 11.10 up and running in about 15 mins! 
 
I love how fast the machine is with the low overhead OS.  I can literally perform a "sudo shutdown -r now" command and have the machine awaiting my login in less then a mintute.  I was going to drop Win7 on top of the Ubuntu 11.10 but now am having second thoughts as I read how efficient with low processor Ubuntu is at replaying DVD/BlueRay movies.  Now I want to keep it as a Ubuntu box and configure it as my media center behind my entertainment center and put alll my dvds in storage to clean up the place a bit.
 
My current issues are: 
no network.  Currently I have a WPA enabled Cable modem through a Zoom router on Comcast.  I probably need to run a hard line to the cable modem but when I piggy backed on another machine last night I noticed Ubuntu didn't even recognize my onboard ethernet.  I think the onboard is a RelTek so I need to sort through driver issues as I have read so far.  First order is get inet network working.
 
setup wireless.  I do have a WG311v2 card in the machine that is also not recognized by the system.  Once I get the hardline to be recognized then I can download the drivers to get the wireless working, but am finding more and more posts that Ubuntu doesn't do WPA/WPA2?  Whats up with that?  I may have to enable the WEP just for my Ubuntu box until I can find a better solution.
 
Next order after internet is solved is to load XBMC for playback and verify the HDMI in on the back forwards the sound to the TV and stereo.  The SR1750 also has the 5.1 Dolby connector on the back so I need to verify that is sending sound to the receiver.  there are a couple ways I can go about getting video and sound to the entertainment center.
 
Then I need to find a DVD ripper to load the system.  Earlier this week I ordered 2 2Tb drives for the movies to be loaded onto.  For now I can rip my collection onto the 250G until the 4Tb shows up.  I think I will need another SATA controller though because this SR1750 only has two SATA controllers and I will need more.  With any luck I can get some more of these 2Tb drives and create a RAID5 to protect from loosing my movies.
 
Once I get this thing running correctly I can start upgrading with the extra SATA controller and more drives.  I also want to upgrade to a BlueRay player to rip blue rays into the system.  I have a Plasma capable of 1080P so I may as well use it!
 
Meanwhile I will be surfing and searching the boards to find the answers to all of these questions to build this machine.  Otherwise if you know how to fix some of my issues please feel free to drop me a pm or Email with the solutions.  I am a sponge for data on the fastest and most efficient way to get this media center going.
 
Thanks in advance for all your support and the great community of support for Ubuntu systems.

---

### Post by Rafterman414 on 2012-02-13
