### Post by Moose on 2013-03-14
Hey Ubuntu forummers!

As you can probably tell, I am not completely new to the forums, I signed up in August last year, but stopped using the forums around December. I am back now! :3

Here is a brief introduction of me;

My name irl is Anthony. I am 14 years of age and I come from Australia. There isn't much to tell really.. But I am a high school student, going to a school with well over 1300+ students. It can be rather busy at times. At school, I really put my head down and try my hardest. (Even though the work is pretty easy.)
But when I am outside of school, I am a pretty relaxed guy. I spend most of my free time writing music, programming, graphics editing and hanging out with friends. I am into Heavy Metal music (And most of it's sub genres, including : Deathcore, Metalcore, Death Metal, Black Metal, Progressive Post-Hardcore) and I like to do vocals. I write my own lyrics in my spare time, however I haven't recorded anything as of yet. Some of my influences include: Bring me the Horizon, House vs Hurricane, Northlane, Oh Sleeper, Emmure, Cannibal Corpse and Mushroomhead. I am also a pretty big gamer, mostly Skyrim. ^.^

I actually got into Linux because of my Uncle. He had Puppy Linux installed on his laptop one time and he showed me it's features and I was pretty impressed. I decided to download Ubuntu 11.10 (which was the newest at that time) and I installed it dual booting with Windows 7. I was so impressed with it, I deleted Windows 7 and made Ubuntu my main. I love Linux now! (except for a minor issue here and there) Some of the operating systems I have used include; gOS, Ubuntu, Fedora, Puppy, Backtrack, xpLike, Macpup (Puppy based), Linux Live Gamers and a few others I cannot poke my memory for. 

Sometimes, I can be a pretty smart person for my age, and sometimes I can be pretty stupid. Just depends on what sort of mood I am in. But if you get on my good side, and are after a friendly chat, or some computer assistance, I am your man. 

If there is anything else you want to know about me, feel free to chuck me a pm! ^.^

---

### Post by guerrerofran on 2013-03-17
Hello everyone :)

My name's Fran and I'm a 24 year old guy from Barcelona. I've been using Linux since I was a teenager but, for work and study reasons, eventually switched to OS X the moment I bought a MacBook Pro almost three years ago. Luckily for me, I missed the feeling of being part of the Open Source community so here I am, *switching back* from the closed-minded word of Apple (in terms of software development, no intention to flame here) to the free/libre world of Ubuntu and GNU/Linux.

It has been a while since I played with those Debian, Slackware, Gentoo and Arch distros and bearing into mind that Apple hardware is sadly not so well supported on *real* free software I'm giving a shot to Ubuntu, with which I had my own perks and headaches inside the GNU/Linux panorama these past years.

I've planned a personal roadmap in order to catch up with the community and review my skills, so I'll be posting my experiences on how to make everything on my MacBook Pro 5,5 work like a charm. I've already read lots of good documentantion, but I'm willing to sum up everything specific to my model in order to learn better how my system works and, who knows, maybe help others!

So I'm not extending myself no more. I'm happy to be here and willing to share bits and bytes of good advice and help!

Cheers!

---

### Post by jimmy1369 on 2013-03-19
My username is jimmy1369. I'm a Windoze user since 1990. I been dabbling in Linux off and on since 1996 because Windoze doesn't make me happy. Always was too dictatorial for my taste. Too vulnerable to hacks and malicious tinkering and (now) too obsessed with copyright protection and security issues (which it kinda sorta tries to deal with but can never really solve because their code base is furgled.).

Linux doesn't make me happy either. Starting in 1996 -- I never found a Linux flavor that was compatible with all my hardware. Caldera, Red Hat, Suze, Corel, Mandrake, Ubuntu, Kubuntu, Linux Mint , , , and now Ubuntu again. I have to say that 12.04 LTS comes closer to being 100 percent compatible than any distro I've ever tried. It works (sorta, mostly) with my Brother 4150 CDN laser printer and it works (sorta, mostly) with my HP flatbed scanner. It does NOT work with my Fujitsu sheetfed scanner but that's not an issue because I've still got a Windoze machine I can hook it to. WordPerfect is what really kept me married to Microsoft from 1990 to date. Now Linux features this Open Office gizmo that does a passible job of word crunching (WordPerfect it still ain't and probably never will be.) and latest knock-offs (Libre Office) will open WordPerfect files, which permits access to my old documents (I'm a writer. Access to my old documents is a must.).

First time I tried Ubuntu it still had the Gnome UI, which was a Very Good Thing. Now 12.04 has this "Unity" interface, which is not yet a good thing. I don't understand why Ubuntu made the switch, but they seem determined to chug ahead with it, so I suppose I'd better learn to get along with what IS. Mint KDE is also loused up. The traditional KDE UI (awfully good) is being replaced by some new gizmo (I don't even remember what they call it (starts with an 'O' I think). I installed Mint and launched it using the traditional KDE UI. One day I booted up and found this O gizmo staring at me. "O!" said I: "What is this thing and what am I supposed to do with it?" I couldn't figure out how to get rid of "O," so it was back to Windoze again.

Now I'm here with a problem. I tried to post it on the Miraculous Ubuntu Forum but it ended up in the wrong forum somehow. I couldn't even find it after I posted it. So I logged off thinking I'd be able to find it this morning. Sure enough: when I woke up and checked my email, I found a note from one of the Forum Fathers saying I had posted in the wrong place and he had taken the liberty of moving it to the RIGHT PLACE for me. He also advised me to come over here and introduce myself before I tried posting anything at all. Sorry, but there's been no help with my problem yet.

Anybody wants to talk to me can find me in the stocks with some other digital miscreants, out in front of Ubuntu headquarters. You can throw rotten tomatoes at me or try to advise me on my Ubuntu problem.

Thank you for your support.

jimmy1369

---

### Post by mahmedbawany on 2013-03-21
Good (whatever time you are reading this) , 
(WARNING : i am a good writer so i am bound to write a story)
i am a college student who just recently got introduced to ubuntu and linux as a whole. As to why i am here, i used to be a complete windows fanatic  hated Mac Os for locking down the operating system to a point of not being usable(think there is some irony here). but then one day my uncle brought his laptop and it did not have either mac or windows on it. it was this beautiful interface in which apps would launch from sides and it had built in email and loads of other things. it was, simply put, an amazing experience. But being a (some-what) gamer i used windows 7 and was really happy with it. 
...
and then came windows 8. . .
i always had the obsession to get the latest. this however seemed more locked down than a mac and more . . . like a tablet. Games that i used to run on windows 7 were dying instantly and things just were falling apart. For example Putting a computer to sleep killed my computer, microphone translated my English language to transformer language, and i frequently got the "disk overload" error which i had never seen before in my entire life.
things got even worse as I ordered a new laptop from lenovo called the "Y500" and it had the worst installation of windows 8.

So now i am here to learn more about this operating system in order to get comfortable with it so i can escape the the structured world of windows. ... (did i miss anyting ?)

### Post by Cmiller2 on 2013-03-29
Hello Ubuntu Community,

You all can call me Cmiller. I'm a young guy who loves computers, and open-source projects. I'm also a guy who grew tired of Micrsoft and their spiffy "ways" of business. So I have decided it was best I resign from using windows, and switch to Linux. After some researching I learned Ubuntu was one of the popular Linux distros alongside with Mint, and a few other varient, and Ubuntu was my choice. So just this night, I installed Ubuntu on my Windows OS. And I'd have to say its quite a challenge getting used to the UI alone. I am quite anxious about adjusting to this new OS, since I have never used it...ever. I've always grown up with Windows and it is all I'm used to. But I am very willing to learn and dive into Linux no matter how difficult it may be. So this thread serves as a breif introduction about myself and just a few questions:

Where is a good place to start? What should be the first thing I do on my long and adventerous journey of Linux? Are there any websites I can access that can teach me the fundamentals of Linux? Tutorials, Videos, Documenets...? Also, where is the Help files and documentation that comes with Linux? I've had some trouble looking. :-P

I'm eager to learn and am ready to use Linux, I just need a few pointers and some noob advice so I can get more confident with using my new OS. And perhaps even one day become a Linux master and teach other new people what I know in the future.

Okay, well enough rambling for now. I could on and on, which there is no need. I hope I covered everything. If I didn't pleace don't hesitate to mention. Thanks everyone in advanced!

Sincerely,

Cmiller

### Post by carl4926 on 2013-03-29
One thing is for sure. You will probably end up doing quite a few installs before you know the ropes fully.
Remember how you were when you for the first time sat in front of a windows PC? You really knew what to do :-)
Well consider yourself a noob again

But first... Welcome!

Pretty much all the software you need is freely available in the software centre. There are one or two exception (such as, if you want it, Chrome Browser has to downloaded manually)

Try this:
Open a terminal and do

```
sudo apt-get update && install ubuntu-restricted-extras
```

i am a longtime user (and abuser) of Windows(98, 2000, XP, Vista and 7) and have come to know the command prompt and the good ole .bat files, like a brother knows a sister. i was introduced to linuxs about a year ago on a simple google search of "free operating systems" because i was tired of microsofts stupid shananagins and such, so i found Ubuntu 10.04 to my liking, tried it and when i noticed there was an even more updated one out, i format/installed 12.04.1 on my old hp laptop, and many others along the way, till i came to my most recent edventure with my Toshiba Satellite c655-s5200 (3 gig ram, amd64, 280 gig hdd) and my Dell Inspiron 531 (2 gig ram, amd64 2.4ghz, 500 gig hdd) both originally running Win7 until i installed ubuntu (does not want to work on Dell) and have been obliterated by the amazing speed and agility of ubuntu 12.04.2 precise pangolin ever since. i am on the Forums often and try to help out as much as possible. i know plenty of reinstalling and hardware issues along with some command line, lots of software programming and editing. thanks to squakie, CharlesA, oldfred, lisati, and many others who have helped me in my quests.

### Post by starlyte2 on 2013-04-01
Hello to those who are out and about on the net.:KS
I use Ubuntu in a different sense to lots of people, probably the majority of Ubuntuists. I run on PuppyLinux based on Ubuntu Lucid. There's also a newer one, based on Precise, but, don't know why, it just didn't manage to drag me off the Lucid version. In fact I do have an idea that it's because I had such a hassle to install Openbox on it, that I gave up, and I'm still on Lucid. It's solid, stable, versatile, and I'm used to it.:D
Lucid Pup starts life as a complete OS that takes up only a bit more than 100mb's. After you have the beast you can install as many additional logiciels as you like/need. as long as you make your save file big enough as time and programs pass. I was looking for an OS that was very versatile, but I had problems with Arch, so I found Puppy, and stayed with it. Although there are Pups based on other systems (Slackware, Deb, etc) I fell for Ubuntu based ones. However I'm SLOWLY making a home server, and as there is now an ArchPup I may use that for the server. It is taking rather a long time (I bought the HDD's before the price rise due to the Thailand catastrophes, which gives you an idea!).:(
One of my other interests is in Electronic Cigarettes. 9 months ago, after 45 years on 1-3 packs a day I switched, without any hassle in the least, to ECigs, and since that I've smoked at the most, half a dozen fags. It's not perfect, but it is at least 99% less dangerous than smoking. If anyone's interested I'll write a bit more about it later.:KS
Apart from that, I'm English, expatriated to S France, in a tiny village, at the end of a dead end road, lost in the mountains. It's very calm; and extremely beautiful. I think I'll end my days here, and end this intro, as well.
Happy informatics to all and one;)

### Post by stevesy on 2013-04-17
Hi guys, 
I'm Steve and I'm from Ireland. ):P Been running Lubuntu 12.04 LTS on my Dell Mini for the past few months and it's been a very positive experience for the most part.  I've always been a fan of the foss philosophy so I'm happy to finally have a GNU/Linux system and to be a member of the community. Looks like there are some good people here. I've been around computers a while but I've only recently taken up programming and electronics.  Aside from command-line I'm learning Python and C.  Also messing about with an Arduino kit as  I'm starting a degree course in Mechatronics this year (god help me!). Really looking forward to it though.  

I hope to be able to answer some questions on the forums as soon as I've a bit more experience myself. Take it easy : ]

Steve

### Post by usernforce on 2013-04-20
Hiya everyone,

I'm a student of philosophy and software engineering. I first started using Linux in 1998 on a home desktop system which was running Debian at the time. I never used it too much but as I got older my parents moved all of our systems over to SuseLinux (at the time I think Novell was in charge of it) in 2003. I built my first computer that summer (which would become the family computer. Outside of some software and minor hardware updates, its been running everyday since that summer) and I then built my first private system in 2004 which ran Debian. So I kind of grew up around Linux but it wasn't until a few years ago that I actually started playing around as a serious user. In 2009 I installed a copy of Debian on a Laptop I owned at the time and I would later play with other distros such as Open Suse, Fedora, Ubuntu 10 series, and Mint. I bought a new laptop about a year ago and I used windows for a little while for basically the first time in my life and found that I absolutly hated it. So I switched back recently to 12.10 and I've loved it.

JMJ

---

### Post by vdubeau on 2013-04-22
My name is Vince. Just wanted to say hello to the Ubuntu community. I originally started using linux full time around 1996. Went through the various distros (Red Hat, Caldera, Corel and finally settled on SuSe). I switched to a Macbook in 2002 but never lost my fondness for Linux.

I played with various LiveCD of Ubuntu as they came out and was always impressed but early Mac support was tough. It was finally time to get a new computer last October. The choice was simple. Buy one that could run Ubuntu. Installed 12.10 and I am happy as the proverbial pig in ****.

Hoping to build a little bit of business helping frustrated Windows users switch to the better alternative.

### Post by m4gnus on 2013-04-22
Hello world.

My name is Ishmael Plata I currently reside in Anaheim, California. I have been using Ubuntu since 8.04 and since then left Ubuntu for a brief discovery stint where I wished to learn all I could about different distros before realizing I am quite comfortable with sticking with Ubuntu as my main and maybe having a Debian or some other distro as my alt installs. 
I am in the process of building up my internet reputation and hopefully get myself better acquainted with some of you fine ladies and gentlemen.

I wish to take this opportunity to state that if it wasn't for these communities (Debian, Ubuntu, etc) I would still be in a weird pseudo funk. Ever since I re-discovered Linux, I have been on this crazy thirst for any form of knowledge I can get my hands on. And lately my thirst for anything related to GNU/Linux has made it possible for me to land a job with the skills I learned as a hobby.
It's great to be able to say that I love my job.

Please say hello to me if ever you see me anywhere else! I look forward to meeting as many people as I can.

-Ishmael

---

### Post by ehwot on 2013-04-22
Hello

I'm here as I find myself being dragged kicking and screaming back into the dark world of building and maintaining servers etc.

To explain, and some history.

I started working life in 1977, a mechanic in the Australian Army for for the first 19 years, then trained and become a sys admin, systems integrator, network manipulator and general all things IT.

Then I went to IT in a State Treasury, then a State Forestry "Government Business Enterprise" (aka public service that thinks it's big business).  Then  stint in Private schools.

Then I got sick of all the crap from software vendors in general and M$, Apple, in particular. Sick of trying to get it all working together resolving the same basic issues that re-appeared every year with every "new" release.
"Users" and middle managers that just had to have the latest and greatest, even though they used but a fraction of the capabilities of the version they had 2 years ago.  Basically working hard and in real terms, getting no-where.

So I went back to being a mechanic, bit of a stint in the building trades, and eventually back to my true love, Motorcycles and the tinkering thereof. I've lost a lot of income, but I'm way, Way, WAY happier.

I'm now lucky enough to be paid to imbibe with my passion, and to work with and for others who share my passion.

But there's always one caveat, isn't there?

This place has the most discordant and useless systems in place for everything IT and telephony.
I find I must fix it, I cannot stand it any longer.

But at least I have a clean slate, the "users" will accept whatever I give them, and for the first time genuine appreciation.

So there you go.
Jim

---

### Post by stylintile on 2013-04-25
Hi Everyone,
     I've actually been using Ubuntu off and on for about three years. started with karmic, went to lucid, now using precise with gnome classic.
It's a personal preference, but I just don't like the Unity dash.  I've been strictly linux for about a year now, trying different distros but I always seem 
to come back to Ubuntu.  That being said, I own my own business installing ceramic tile, and computers are a hobby.  I buy old and used ones whenever
I can find them cheap, and I've had a few given to me.  I now have twenty-seven of them, with seventeen actually working.  Seven are running now, with only
two of those running Windows.  That's so its easier to talk friends through troubleshooting their machines.
     Anyway, I love the Ubuntu community.  The friendliness and willingness to help on this forum is truly amazing, and I have yet to have a question unanswered.
After lurking around for quite awhile I thought it was time I introduced myself, so...Greetings Everyone!!

### Post by plasticframedglasses on 2013-04-27
Hi, everyone! I've just recently come back to the Linux community and now I think I'll stay for good. 

My first experience with Linux was about 5 years ago when I went in an internet cafe that used Redhat as its OS where I remember being really fascinated by it. About a year later, I installed my first distro on my laptop (Hardy Heron) and then experimented with Mint, openSuse, and Mythbuntu. The last version I tried was Jaunty after which I went back to Windows and used Vista, then 7, and most recently, 8. 

Earlier this week, I tried Quantal Quetzal and liked it. I messed something up with 12.10 that I couldn't fix last night and decided to upgrade to Raring Ringtail in a last ditch attempt to save my files (it worked!) and now here I am running 13.04 and liking it even more!

### Post by Neil Holland on 2013-04-28
Hello all.  Am very new to the world of Linux - been having fun the last few hours installing Lubuntu 13.04 onto a c. 2003 Fujitsu Siemens Amilo P4 Laptop.

Firstly around construction of boot USB now with actual install.

Googling error messages has led me here but nothing conclusive so far...

---

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2013-04-28
> **pacpod said:**
> Hi all!


I spent the day tinkering with my early 2011 MBP to get Ringtail on it but now I think I'm going to put Fuduntu or Mint Cinnamon back on it.

Welcome. Why not have 'em all on separate partitions? Just install grub to the partition you're installing the new OS to and leave the existing grub there to run the show. Boot into the original install and run: sudo update-grub. Reboot and the new install should be there.

[QUOTE=Neil Holland]Googling error messages has led me here but nothing conclusive so far... [/QUOTE]

Welcome. Try posting new threads with descriptive titles and outline your issue in the appropriate sub-forum. Good luck. (13.04 has just been released; have you tried something well tested on lots of hardware like 12.04 LTS to see if you get the same issues? Then you will know whether it is specific to 13.04)

---

### Post by lisati on 2013-05-01
> **allie_jr said:**
> I am a member since over a year now.
today i need to post a question, I have spent over an hour looking for the guidance on how to do it (post) I almost give up.
I need to post because I dont find the answer to my question after several queries.
Thanks for your help.

Alonzo

Hi there, Alonzo, and welcome to the forum. 

If you've searched and searched, but had no joy finding a solution and want to start a new thread, go to the [main page]("http://ubuntuforums.org") of the forum, click on the link to a section of the forum that seems most relevant to the problem you're experiencing, and click on the "Post new thread" button near the top left of the screen. If you're stuck choosing a suitable section, the [General Help]("http://ubuntuforums.org/forumdisplay.php?f=331") and [absolute beginners talk]("http://ubuntuforums.org/forumdisplay.php?f=326") sections are good starting places.

### Post by Swappiness on 2013-05-05
First of all I'd like to start by saying "Hi I'm Todd and I'm new to the  forums and Linux based OS's for PC's and Ubuntu kicks major backside."  Secondly I'd like to tell everyone how I came to be on Ubuntu Forums.  I  installed Ubuntu about 2 days ago AEST along side Windows XP. I wanted  to install it a while back but wasn't "brave" enough to. I started  watching YouTube videos about Linux OS's last week and the more Ubuntu  was mentioned the more I wanted to install it. After a few days of being  on YouTube I downloaded 13.04 then burnt it to a disc and attempted to  install Ubuntu over the top of XP. FAIL. Then I ran the recovery disc  for the laptop I'm using (Toshiba M-100) which happens to be my gf's old  computer. I deleted the whole partition and tried to install Ubuntu  again. This time the installation was successful. I rebooted only to  find out it wouldn't boot Ubuntu. So I went and installed XP again then  quickly inserted the Ubuntu disc while the laptop rebooted for a second  attempt at installing Ubuntu along side XP. Finally a successful install  and I let the computer do it's thing. As the boot menu popped up I saw  the options I had to choose from. Not being able to decide I just sat  there thinking: "Do I boot XP or Ubuntu?" After a few seconds of  thinking, I decided just to get the Windows side of things over with and  concentrate on Ubuntu. But before I could select XP Ubuntu got  impatient and decided for me. So I just went with it. I spend hours on  end exploring what I can only explain as "PURE COMPUTING BLISS". I  didn't even get to boot into XP that night because I was having way too  much fun with the awesomeness of this great and powerful OS. It wasn't  until I got out of bed today that I was able to actually boot up XP and  fix it to my liking. You really do notice a huge difference in  performance when you switch between the two. UBUNTU FTW!!!!

P.S. Sorry if there's too much to read but it was more than I planned to say.

See you in the forums,
Swappiness.

---

### Post by Werne on 2013-05-08
Well, seeing as how I've been lurking around here for the past few days, I thought I might as well say hi. So, hi there! You don't get to know my name, everyone except my parents calls me Werne anyway. I wish my parens would too but nooo. :roll:

Unlike some here I'm neither new to Ubuntu nor am I new to Linux, I just never had a Linux OS on my own PC until about 6 months ago. Also, while my join date says I joined 4 months ago, I forgot about this forum so count me as a new guy. :p

Even though using Ubuntu wasn't as smooth as I hoped, I managed to get it working right after a while. And I can't say I like most of Ubuntu distributions, I especially dislike Ubuntu itself, that Unity desktop is too oversimplified for me and I really hate Amazon and the rest of those things that come with it. That would be why I'm currently using a stripped-down version of Ubuntu GNOME Remix 12.10 running on gnome-fallback, doesn't look as great as Debian 7 with GNOME Shell which I have as a second OS but it works perfectly and boots fast once I removed all the things that made problems.

Anyhow, just popped in to say hi. Since I kinda like the way this forum looks, I think I'll stick around for a while. ;)

### Post by zengrits on 2013-05-15
I've been using Ubuntu and variants off and on for years. The latest effort is Lubuntu on a Lenovo Thinkpad z60t with a Pentium M processor. I maxed out the ram at 2 GB, up from 512 mb and I replaced the standard hard drive with an Intel 120 GB SSD. Was surprised a machine this old (2005) would take a serial ATA drive (believe that's the interface). Anyway, this thing screams and the old laptop has become a favorite. Lubuntu was exactly the right choice for me. 
There are a few tweaks I'd like to make, but don't know how - shortcuts for Leafpad and perhaps a few other often-used programs in a taskbar, or someplace that would make them more convenient with a Web browser open, etc. I've tried dragging an icon to the bar on the bottom of the screen, but that doesn't work. I'm definitely open to suggestions.

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2013-05-16
> **shoushikochou said:**
> ... the 32 bit or 64 bit :(

Welcome. Please ask all support questions in a new thread with a descriptive title in the appropriate sub-forum as this is a non-support thread in a non-support sub-forum. ;)

The short answer, though, is if you have a dual-core processor (64bit) you should use the AMD64 ISO image, regardless of whether your processor is Intel or AMD.

### Post by Greenlion on 2013-05-20
Greetings all,

I am known as Greenlion however on some gaming forums I go by Greenlion420.

I'm not sure why I waited till now to join these forums as I've been getting help here for over a year now. Thanks, by the way.

I have been building systems for 20 years now and as soon as I switched to Linux/Ubuntu (Xubuntu at the moment) the answer to a question that has haunted me for years was answered. I'm sure there's more than a few hardware sculpters here who can relate to this one. I, like most halfway intelligent tech-heads, pick my hardware very carefully to ensure optimized compatability and while I'm not always able to afford the latest and greatest components I most certainly do not buy junk. Yet at the completion of each build, after installing windows and all the drivers needed by the system I would find myself a little let down. I hand picked these components yet the perfomance was never quite what I thought it should be. Every one of my builds worked, some were quite zippy but no "pop". It's like it was all preparation and no "H". I just (after a few years) assumed that I was expecting too much and I was right, but it wasn't a hardware issue after all, nope. The first time I installed Ubuntu on a new build (requested by a friend) there seemed to be this glow emanating from the system case, turned out to be a lighted fan on the psu, but after booting up guess what? There was the "pop", the "H" I had been looking for!

I knew I was doing something not quite right but I assumed it was the hardware, silly me. Anyway, I wiped my windows partition about a year ago and I'm not going back. You folks are stuck with me. :P

---

### Post by Kenc3 on 2013-06-17
Guess I should introduce myself. I had Ubuntu 11.04 along with Windows 7 for a few years. I always used the Windows side because I knew it. I bought a book on Ubuntu and was completely lost because I didn't try anything.
Recently I had some problems with Windows and thought I would try Ubuntu. I got a notice that it was no longer supported and I needed to upgrade. I tried to go to 11.10 and began to geta lot os warnings. Finally I found a way to make a start up disk of 12.04 and installed it. I downloaded the manual and read it. Then it began.. I decided to install 12.04. The options were not the same as the book. I could either wipe out Windows or format the hard drive (which also wipes out Windows.Short story. Windows is gone and I am on Ubuntu only. I had 500 GB so there are no more warnings.
 About me. I am a 73 year old American living with my wife and son in the Philippines just outside Manila. I retired in 2005. My occupation was Technician/Instructor in Precision Measurement Equipment Calibration/repair. (PMEL).
Ken

### Post by Beautymist on 2013-06-20
Oh, hi there!

This is my first day on this forum, and my very first post, too.

OK, so where do i start?

Me & Linux.

My (ex) boyfriend got interested in the Linux OS back in 1999, reading the manual while I read webcomics and filed my nails.
Once he knew his way around, he installed it all for me.
He also taught me the basics and installed a user-friendly version for me, so I could use the computer easily. Of course, whenever (rarely) there was a problem, he was around to fix it. So I didn't do or learn much...

Then came boyfriend number 2 - also a geek linux type. Can you sense a trend here? :)
He installed and upgraded many versions of linux on my computer. I think I've had my current hardware in 1999, only changing the software regularly. Yep, I'm the conservative type, I guess.

OK, so now my (ex) boyfriend number 2 is all well and married (not to me) and has a kid (not from me) so I realized that, DUH, it's about time I learn more about linux on my own. Right?

This is important not only for me, but also for my community, as there is linux installed in the computers of an association I belong to. And they need their system upgraded real soon. i promised to take care of it by Monday June 24th in the afternoon.

I use xUbuntu at home, but have no idea where to look in order to know which version I have exactly - that will be my first question (will post it tomorrow).
I've been using computers during my workday since 1989 and started using the internet in 1997 and had Linux since 1999. I'm no geek, but I guess i know a thing a two about computers.

I have a very wide range of interests. To name a few: 
reading/writing (old fashioned books, yes).
Calligraphy, painting, typography, graphic design, architecture, product design - would love to mearn how to install beautiful type faces on my computer
I speak 5 languages fluently
I am passionate about: the written word, insects, tea, Japan & art

As to video games, I only played Dungeon Keeper II back in 1999. Would love to have it on my computer again. Need to ask about how to install it.

you get the idea.

-BM

### Post by RavenLX on 2013-06-25
Hello everyone. I go by RavenLX in most forums and have been using Ubuntu for a few years now. Mostly I been working with Ubuntu Servers at work (Web hosting). I had then tried Ubuntu desktops and settled on Linux Mint KDE in January. I am 100% Windows-free and enjoying it a lot! :)

I can't update my profile yet (too few posts) but I will once I get to that point. I have a web site (just look at some of my past posts) where I put up Linux tutorials and info. I am really impressed with all the things that one can do in Linux! Especially in Video editing, image editing, music, development... There are so many programs out there and they work just as well as most commercial Windows programs.

I remember the first time I tried Linux was I think Slackware 1.0! I been trying different distros ever since. At one point I was dual-booting Windows 98 and Mandriva (but it was called by that other name at the time). I think working on a Tandy Color Computer 3 with OS-9 Level II (which was sort of Unix-like in nature with a bit of VAX thrown in, maybe), I think that is why I was pretty comfortable with Linux. I also had a Unix Shell account at an ISP a long time ago and the admin (who became a good friend of mine - RIP 10th of this month was one year) had taught me how to navigate the Unix command line.

So for me, I think Linux was more natural overall than DOS or Windows!

Anyway, that's my introduction. Hope I can be of some help here and learn more about Ubuntu and Linux in general as well.

---

### Post by Maverick Meerkat on 2013-06-29
Hello World, my name is Rick.
I am 52 years old, and I live in Delaware, USA.

My friend at work talked me into trying Ubuntu 10.10 over 2 years ago and I am glad I listened.
One of the things that captured me was the Ubuntu Code of Conduct.
I had read an article where high school kids had signed the code and I thought, what a wonderful thing for people of any age, especially the young.

I now have 3 computers running Ubuntu... laptop with 12.04, desktop in kitchen with 12.04, and main desktop with 13.04 / Windows XP.
Thanks to the good people that make it all work!

I actually have fun just using a computer with Ubuntu instead of Windows because it gives me the same feeling of freedom like using solar power rather than batteries, or sailing as opposed to power boating.
Harnessing the power of freedom, created by free thinking people whose skills provide the energy.

As far as knowledge, my computer skills are very basic.
I have Ubuntu, not so much in my brain, but in my heart.

Peace,
Rick

---

### Post by BR8 on 2013-07-08
Hello, my name is BR8, and I've been a Linuholic for about half a year now. I started by installing Mint 14 Cinnamon on my laptop that still has Vista which I don't use, and I didn't look back.

Early in June, I think it was around the 10th, Windows broke on my family's desktop PC. I hid my joy and installed Xubuntu 12.10 from a LiveCD I happened to have made previously, thankfully 32-bit. It took a few days due to installation failure and a cord coming undone inside the computer, but it finally worked.

My dad is really not a fan of Linux. I had Chromium, Firefox, and Chrome as shortcuts, and he asked me how to get online because he uses IE. I have no doubt that he's thankful that he and my mom have a Vista laptop.

My mission is to rid the world of Windows and Mac. Linux shall rule with it's trusted companions, GNU and BSD.

---

### Post by darkaudit on 2013-08-02
I've also been... away. But now I'm not. :)

Tried Arch. It's nice, but the AUR builds are... inconsistent. Tried Gentoo. It's not as hard as some make it out to be... once it's installed. But it's like one of those old sports cars that needs 90 minutes in the garage for every hour spent on the road. I spent more time emerging and tweaking than actual anything else.

So since GNOME 2.x has departed since I was here last, it's Xubuntu for me now. Making the hipsters at Starbucks think I'm running OSX untill they see the cover and see Acer. :D

### Post by Bill Leach on 2013-08-02
Lets see... After only 40+ years of computer experience I had to have my daughter get me logged back into Ubuntu forums!  LOL

I really did start Linux with Slackware 0.97.  I do consider Unbuntu to be a bit of a step up from that!

Run Linux on 4 machines, Unix on one, and Amigados on 3 others (they are really not in use currently though).

I'm still trying to get comforatable with Unity.  I really miss KDE-3!  Mint is OK but certainly not as well supported as Ubuntu.

I have spent a lot of time over the years installing and helping others with Linux installations.  More Debian than any other.  My daughter used to call the house and ask for the "help desk" meaning me! LOL  Now about half the time, it's me asking her for help!  She does about the best google searches of anyone I've ever known!

I've really been "out of the loop" for the last few year though.  I haven't compiled a kernel in probably 2 years at least and haven't done alpha/beta testing in many years more.  Course I don't have the spectacular crashes that used to show up either! LOL

Of course, like a great many others, I'm trying to learn something about the strange and esoteric world of Android.  Not doing very well on that one!

I guess I should mention that I am also a not very active ham radio operator.

And finally, I'm about the most committted anti-MicroSloth bigot that you are likely ever to meet.

---

### Post by BlinkinCat on 2013-08-03

With your 40+ years experience you sure have it over me at less than 5 lol - ;)

Now that you have decided to get back into it, I trust you will be with us for many years.

I assume your daughter is also a member here - it's good too have family share your interests isn't it?

Cheers and best wishes to you - :p

### Post by HousieMousie2 on 2013-08-03
Hi BlinkinCat,

I am Bill's daughter.  :)  He's the one who started me in Linux and is therefor ultimately responsible for all of the goofy questions I have asked over the years. (That's happens-- I was in Texas, he was in Idaho and was working long hours and couldn't help me through the strange new world of Linux.  Thankfully, the wonderful Ubuntu Community was here!)

---

### Post by Bill Leach on 2013-08-03
Thanks for the welcome message.  I actually joined somewhere around 2 years ago but never really got involved (I see we both joined in the same month and year!).  I hope you have been more active than I was! LOL

Depending upon the depth of your involvement, 5 years is plenty.  So much changes so fast that 40 year old knowledge is often not too useful.  Indeed, Linux is an almost entirely different beast than it was when I first got involved.  Some of that "old" knowledge has permitted me to make a few really bad blunders!

Yes, it is great that my daughter "took to Linux".  Almost lost her though when KDE pulled what I think is the greatest blunder that any development organization can make... when they released KDE4.  Heck, even I abandoned KDE.  Though I now have one machine that recently started using it again.

One of the really neat things about having another "computer type" in the family is that she knows windows a whole lot better than I do so, when an issue comes up related to windows she can generally find the right answer.

We are both working on her oldest brother and one of his sons.  Have them both running linux at least part time.  Actually they have a dual boot desktop but both of their notebooks are linux ONLY! YEAH!

I do hope that work and other pressing matters will still permit me to get more involved.  So looking forward to chatting and maybe working with you and the rest of the community.

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2013-08-03
Welcome to Bill and HousieMousie2. ;)

Forewarned is forearmed, so I'll get in early. While we appreciate your right to be a bigot and your freedom of speech, please be aware, Bill Leach, that OS bashing (of ANY OS) is not permitted here (along with any discussion of religion or politics). From the Forum ***Code of Conduct***:

> Attacks and derogatory terms of any kind are not welcome._ This includes references to any operating systems or the companies that produce them._

Just a heads up unless you feel the need to unleash. There are plenty of other forums that do a line in Win bashing for that. We say live and let live, use what works for you, and many users here run dual-boots with Linux and Win or Mac.

Good luck and have fun. ;)

### Post by RichardET on 2013-08-14
Hi, Richard Thornton, new member of the forums, somewhat new to Ubuntu, have settled on it because I like what Canonical is trying to do.  I worked with Suse/Opensuse for about 12 to 13 years and have decided it was time to go in a new direction and Ubuntu seems to be that direction.  My first taste of Linux was in 1998 with RH 5.2 on an old Compaq 486 with 12 meg of ram.  I currently use a Lenovo W530, with Ubuntu as a 64 bit VM.  I have another Lenovo, a V470 from two years ago, which I might try to reformat Ubuntu next year.  I had a small disaster on my hands a year ago when I tried to put OpenSuse on it and the UEFI BIOS went crazy with it;  wound up costing me 100 dollars for support disks from Lenovo, plus phone support to reinstall Windows 7.    I think that by now, the UEFI issues have been resolved, and that V470 uses the 2nd generation on board Intel graphics, so it is most likely compatible.  I am not a fan of KDE at all, preferring GNOME, but Unity is also a good third way.

I am in the world of math/statistics/data analytics, and use SAS on Windows.  Also JMP.  I am dabbling with R on Ubuntu.

---

### Post by mickee384 on 2013-08-20
I started with computers back in the day of Windows for Workgroups 3.11. I saw my first picture on the internet - a crude Canadian Flag with a browser called Quarterdeck Mosaic. I advanced thru the use of Windows computers dutifully purchasing a new OS as they came out. I had never looked at Linux, until I decided to put Puppy Linux on an old lappy I had that had Windows Me on it. That was a couple of years ago. When the laptop died, I purchased a used Apple Power Book for like $100. What an investment! I got used to using this laptop, and using my Windows 7 workstation less and less. I came into a HP Touchsmart PC just before Windows 8 came out. I had had enough. lickety split, I formatted the Win7 station and replaced with Ubuntu. No going back from here. Previously I was evaluating ubuntu on my Win7 box in Virtual box, so the change from Windows to Linux was not a big deal and a WELCOME improvement in speed!

I look forward to my ubuntu life.

Mickee

[IMG]http://i17.photobucket.com/albums/b68/The_Wizard_of_OZ/Lindows-NOT-1.jpg[/IMG]
Linux is ***NOT*** Windows. Doesn't ***PRETEND*** to be, Doesn't ***WANT*** to be; Don't try to ***MAKE*** it be.

### Post by daniel41 on 2013-08-21
Hi!  My name is Daniel Robinson.  I'm joining the forums, because my Linux skills are rusty and were never that great to begin with.  Tried out Redhat way back in the day when that was the major desktop distro and when I was sick of Windows 98.  Found that Linux was too hard to get out of the beginner stage, back then.  I'm hoping it's easier now.  

I got back into it, because I was choosing between a Synology NAS or a build your own with a NAS4Free or FreeNAS distro.  However, those OS seem very limited and not very dynamic.  So, I installed Ubuntu 13.04 on my old MacBookPro to try out Ubuntu.  I'm hoping to build a simple NAS, but my desire to use encryption and 'maybe' do more than just serve as a backup location is making me think about getting slightly upgraded components.  We'll see.

### Post by baker-quin on 2013-08-23
Hello, My name is Quinton Baker. I'm completely new to Linux but had a friend recommend it to me so I thought I would dual boot it with Windows. I've had it for a week now and still get really confused whenever I have to go to terminal but just follow online instructions and hope it works out. Most of the time I have no idea what I'm doing but I would like to learn more and I think with the knowledge of how it work and how much customization there is I could truly get to love this OS. I've installed 12.04 mint. Had no idea which one to go with so I don't know if there is one better for my needs/skills or not but this one seems pretty user friendly so far. 

My friends are trying to get me to buy a Mac but I don't like how expensive they are when I could easily build the same comp for half the price. I don't want to pay that much just for an OS. But as I'm just barely starting to learn some about webpages and thinking I may get into the some development and maintenance. I think Linux will work well for this from what I've seen. Only just beginning so sometimes I wonder if it would be easier to go to Mac, so that I'm on the same OS when we talk and I'm trying to get something to work. But the programming language is the same regardless of OS, just a little different how we go about it sometimes. Anyway, I'd like to give this a go. I've done a lot of google searches getting everything set up but I think this could be a wonderful recourse when I'm hitting dead ends or just want to put it out there and not search everywhere and not fully understand what I'm doing. 

Hoping for lots of help... and maybe someday i can return it.

### Post by paul_c2 on 2013-08-27
Hi all, new to the forums, not on Ubuntu. I have been using it on and off since 9.xx

Got a few hardware failures in the past year or so and a few months ago it was the video card ... with all these unscheduled updates I found myself with a completely different PC than I started with (Dual-Core P4 with 2GB DDR400 became a Core 2 Duo with 4GB DDR2-800 now all sitting on a mini-ITX motherboard).

Stopped using the PC after the GT520 died, even though I had onboard video, I was 10 from moving into the house my wife an I bought so I had lots of other things on my mind.

Fast forward to last weekend I finally pulled out the dead video card and benchmarked the rest of the hadware, when I saw that everything was fine I backed up my drive and installed 12.04 LTS!

I have no pictures of the setup but the whole computer now sitting in a white Ikea Kassett box in my TV stand (I call it my Ikea computer), Mini-ITX motherboards are VERY small, you have to hold one in your hands to really appreciate it! Plus a full tower was overkill for that mini-ITX and looked odd beside the TV!

### Post by CeejRab on 2013-09-16
Greetings to my fellow Ubuntu users and fans! 

My name is Christian, I just signed up to the Ubuntu forums like three days ago. I'm a fast learner however, and already I've been able to get the pleasure of aiding someone else in some troubleshooting issues they were having! What a great feeling, I love helping people! 

Please see my BIO to get a little story on where I'm coming from. 

I realize many forums/Internet communities put a lot of weight in seniority... Fact is, I don't. I don't believe in dismissing one member's views or value simply because they've been a member for one hour. Nor do I believe in swallowing everything someone else has to say and giving them undue respect just because they've been a member for 5+ years. Sure, experience matters, but not enough to look down on anyone because they have "less time on the job" than you. I learned this on another forum, I got too cocky when a newbie joined and I found out real fast that they had a lot to offer and I was too arrogant to let them be a help! As my "seniority" grows (slowly!) my promise to fellow members is that I will never treat one higher than another because of a surplus or lack of time of the forum. I believe in performance proving a person's value. 

I hope I can be of assistance here on the forums, and I trust that honesty and genuine kindness will serve me well when interacting with others. "Do unto others, as you would have them do to you" is a motto of mine. 

All the best to everyone, please do check out my bio if interested. 

-CeejRab

---

### Post by BlinkinCat on 2013-09-16

### Post by mkmanifesto on 2013-10-01
Hello everyone! I'm new to the forums and hopefully I can learn and help out other people along the way. I've been using different distros since about 2008. I recently started running ubuntu 12.04 full time on my laptop. I didn't even allow the computer to boot Windows 8 one time. Booted straight from my USB stick to the ubuntu installation, and I'm finding day to day use with ubuntu is far more enjoyable than Windows. 

My main reason for running ubuntu full time is to one, free myself from Windows and two, learn as much as I can about the linux terminal and scripting. My prior use was mainly to perform work related tasks, such as Apache web hosting, data recovery, and data backup/transfers. I work in the IT field and figure knowing linux can only help me with my career.

Anyway Hello everyone, nice to meet you!

### Post by Alexandre_Dnomme on 2013-10-19
Hi everyone!
I'm Alex, newly registred user, but reader from a long time.
This forum has been really helpful to understand ubuntu, and linux in general.  Now I have been able to overcome the difficulties of switching OS (windows -> ubuntu)  on a daily basis.
I'm using ubuntu as a home server, and for my desktop/laptop's every day computing.
The biggest issue for the switch was that I work in home automation, and software to program thoses system only run on windows. But then I discovered ubuntu + virtualbox + windows xp + **seamless mode** and it was the magical solution that I was waiting for! Now I can quickly beenfit from the 2 os, thank to the community! Now I hope to see more and more program being developped for Tux, so I can completely remove windows and benefit from the big and open world of linux completly!

By the way 1, I speak french, sorry if I do weird sentences lol
By the way 2, I'm looking for ressources (books, tutorial, etc..) about how to create, manage and troubleshoot mid-scale wireless network (installation between 2-10 AP at customer house) and the hardware that I prefer is Unifi.. so if someone have suggestions, it would be welcome!

Have a great day!

### Post by Anonimista on 2013-10-19
Recently bought a new rig and decided I was too cheap to shell out for Windows so I installed Ubuntu for the first time (long time Windows user, no experience with anything else) . Didn't like Unity and changed to Gnome classic, but didn't like it either. Found out about Kubuntu, installed it, did some customization and it felt comfortable. Installed LAMP, Chromium, Opera, NetBeans, VLC and Thunderbird and all good. Then a little notification popped on me that an upgrade was available (to 13.10  - I installed 13.04 originally). Half of the applications refused to start unless opened with sudo. Guess it had to do with my user account, but being a novice, decided to do a clean install rather than trying to solve the problem. Installed Ubuntu, didn't like Unity, installed Gnome classic, didn't like it either - finally settled with Kubuntu and am happy with it. Through the process I kept bumping into this forum so I decided, why not register. Anyway, thanks for all the help.

---

### Post by Kristian_Iversen on 2013-10-23
Hi fellow Ubuntu's!
I'm a newbi to Linux and Ubuntu, and have just installed Ubuntu 13.10 and must say I'm loving it! So simple and easy to use and I have had no problems with my old laptop! Running a HP Compaq NC6120. Amazing! 
I did a clean install and deleted my Win 7 (was getting slow) and pretty happy with what I did! Ubuntu is a bit different but so far I have managed to find my way around. Installed wine yesterday so I can run apps that are not yet supported for Ubuntu and works great! First time I have come across a platform that just works! :D
Cheers!

### Post by djtomt on 2013-10-25
Hello, all. TomT here and glad to be!

On a limited budget, I began to put together a Mini ITX H@ckintosh system several weeks ago. I finally had gathered all the parts I needed about a week ago.

Since then, I struggled getting M@c OS to run, and did manage to get up to 10.6.8. Difficulties were because my gigabyte board's bios would not allow booting from a USB drive and the fact that I had a series 6 MB mised with a series 7 CPU (I may have that wrong)..anyway, a Sandy Bridge board with an Ivy Bridge CPU.

 After having attempted several times to upgrade and putting in nearly a week of 3-8 hour days of work on this, I had a major light-bulb, 'aha!' moment and decided to go with Ubuntu.

The software loaded without a hitch and instantly made use of both my fully accelerated graphics card (8400gs) and my audio, which none of my installations of the Hacks were able to do.

I am so pleased with this software and I look forward to learning more about it. 

I want to thank the community very, very much for its past, current and future efforts! I really love this OS!

Thanks, again and best regards to all!
TomT

---

### Post by b|ackhole on 2013-10-27
Hey! I suppose I can do that introduction thing and say hello. I was delighted to see that the forum sign-in was integrated with Ubuntu One, since 3 years ago I had put Ubuntu on my computer before going technology-free cold turkey for 1.5 years. What a silly name, b|ackhole, but I guess that is what Krystal-of-three-years-ago wanted to be called so I'll let it stay. I do enjoy black holes.

Anyway, after going without a computer for a while (I actually at the time still had an iPhone which I used to complete one of my online university courses, which I would strongly suggest never doing, because just no) I gave in and got an adorable Asus Bamboo, because I suppose I was in some equally-as-silly-to-b|ackhole phase where I wanted technology that was eco friendly.  Ran it fine for 1.5 years on Windows 7 until a virus ate explorer.exe, damaged my Safe Mode and left me without any of my data a day before an essay was due.  Well I didn't have any time to go and find a way to download my original OS onto my tablet so I could transfer it from a micro SD card onto and make a bootable disk, and I remembered briefly running Ubuntu so I decided to come back, and I am delighted to have.

I ran the Saucy Salamander for half a day, but there were a few features I felt still needed to be tweaked for it to be optimum for me to run it, so I downgraded back to the Precise Pangolian, because Pangolian's are more adorable anyway.  All I need to do is get my CS5 collection running on my computer again and I'll be fine but there have been some problems with that!  I did find Kingsoft Office as my replacement to Office 2013 (which I was sad to walk away from), but find the Microsoft Clone to be fantastic, if not a little shaky for formatting.

Oh man, I guess that's enough rambling now.
Hi!
I'm back.
S'good to be back.

Krystal

PS: I guess I should add that I'm an illustrator and if anyone has suggestions to make the art and illustration process easier on linux I would love the help!  Thanks, <3.

---

### Post by StephenSimonN on 2013-11-03
Hello Ubuntu Community!

My name is Stephen and I have been dabbling with Ubuntu on and off for a very short time. (I'd say in total I have spent a cumulative total of about 1 week's worth of time with Ubuntu over the past 3 - 4 years.)

I primarily use my PC to game and Skype but I have began to become quite frustrated with Windows and decided, today, to make the full switch to Ubuntu 12.04 LTS 64-Bit. I currently am running Ubuntu 12.04 LTS 64-Bit on my custom built low-budget gaming PC and on my Compaq Presario CQ61 laptop.

I love learning and trying new things and will admit I was rather overwhelmed at first trying to make sure that everything I use my computer for on Windows I would be able to do on Ubuntu as well. I have yet to encounter a problem I could not solve either via a Google search, which would generally take me to websites specifically created for Ubuntu technical support.

I think Ubuntu is a fantastic operating system and it continues to blow me away each day!

Cheers!
Stephen

---

### Post by spencer the great on 2013-12-17
Hello All!

My name is Spencer, but I prefer to be called "ecube."
I've been using various GNU/Linux distros for about 3-4 years. During that time, I always failed to learn more about it than a basic grep here, tail there, and who sed what.
I got into it when I was extremely computer-illiterate, just to "see what would happen."
I recently switched back to Windows just because certain programs run faster there or don't run at all on linux without wine (ahem - certain games :))
Even after a few years of doing linux, I still barely know anything about it. In short, I'm a n00b (I just have to LOOK at places like the more advanced subforums here, and almost everywhere in stackoverflow to feel this way).

Other than that, I don't consider myself a nice guy.  When I get into a conversation with anybody, it can get pretty. darn. nasty. Or at least they can.  I just ignore it and hope we can all still be friends.  Sort of.  No. In reality, I pretend to hope that while in reality, whenever someone gets really, really nasty, I spend a few days contemplating murder.

Apart from that, I am also a wizard. 1: Whatever I touch, I somehow manage to break.  Once, editing some of the text of one of my websites nuked its MySQL database.  Or maybe that's just because MySQL isn't very wizard-proof. One of the two. Who knows. 2: Every time (Yes: EVERY time. Absolute. Never does this NOT happen. Ever.) I get an error, it happens to be an error no-one's ever seen before.  Or, if they have seen it, they choose not to answer because they know, from the few wizards who DID have that error (such as DSO missing's when linking libarchive, or merely the Ubuntu desktop GUI somehow getting nuked), that I'm a wizard. Or maybe I just suck at life. I DO get a lot of errors.   3: I float in water.  What else is there to say?

That said, NEVER, under any circumstances, let me into your house.  No, not even if I looked like I was bleeding enough to be the source of the color red in the world.  It's probably a plot.
Just don't do it.  
No, not even if I say I won't blow up the house. Or the washing machine.  Or the tacos.  Because if there's one thing in the world that'll vanish I touch it, it's tacos.

---

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2013-12-23
Hi and welcome, drew9. You'll find Arduino users, Raspberry Pi users and fiddlers with all manner of techno devices here! 

The best place to start is by asking questions (a question a thread is best rather than five in one as that gets confusing). Be clear, use a descriptive title, and post in the most appropriate sub-forum. Most sections are for support requests, some, like ***The Cafe*** and ***Ubuntu/Other OS chat***, are not. Have a look at the link in my sig for some tips on posting (Fastest way to get your question answered). 

I too picked up everything 'on the street' and the learning curve got steeper when I got into Linux, but loving it! You'll learn plenty here. Good luck and enjoy the OS, the forums and the learning curve. ;)

* And hi and welcome to all other newcomers!

---

### Post by varunendra on 2014-01-04
> **Kalabasa said:**
> Hello! I'm new here. We use Ubuntu at the University :DAnd I'm trying to post a thread.But line breaks aren't preserved.testinglinebreaks

Welcome to the forums ! :)

If you are writing text on a text editor on Ubuntu then opening it on Windows, the line breaks won't persist by default, because Windows handles them differently. To preserve it, make sure to select "Line Ending" to be in "Windows" format while saving the document.

The difference is that the Unix/Linux uses only one non-printable character "\n" to mark end of a line (\n=newline), while Windows uses two characters - "\r\n" to mark the same (\r=return).

### Post by UltimateCat on 2014-01-14
Hi! Sparrow40k1 & Welcome to the Ubuntu Forums! ;)

There is IMO so many amazing things about Linux that I couldn't possibly tell you everything-

The Linux kernels are modular.
The functionality is contained in modules that are loaded into the kernel dynamically.
This keeps the core small and makes it possible to load or replace modules in a running kernel w/o rebooting.

Drivers are built into the kernel permanetly. To change that you would have to reconfigure the kernel and learn how to do that.
A module is a peice of a kernel that can be optionally loaded into the kernel.
Most modules are device drivers but not all.

On a stable system users only install a new kernel if they need support for hardware or to correct a performance problem.

As far as programming goes there is C+ and C++ but for that I think you need a complier like gcc which should be installed on you OS be default--
[http://www.cplusplus.com/doc/tutorial/](http://www.cplusplus.com/doc/tutorial/)
[http://gcc.gnu.org/](http://gcc.gnu.org/)

You can learn from these links as well.
[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linux_kernel](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linux_kernel)
[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GNU_Compiler_Collection](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/GNU_Compiler_Collection)

Reading the Ubuntu Documentation  will give you knowledge too so I encourage you to read about your distribution!
Also, I highly recommend the "Ubuntu Linux Bible" It helped me a GREAT deal!
[http://it-ebooks.info/book/2752/](http://it-ebooks.info/book/2752/)
[https://help.ubuntu.com/](https://help.ubuntu.com/)   

Good luck to you in all you do with Linux!
Cheers-;)

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2014-01-28
Welcome.

Adobe products? Photoshop??? You might be better off looking for alternatives. The Gimp is a good substitute for Photoshop. Photoshop will NOT run natively on Ubuntu. You will need to jump through a few hoops, namely by installing Wine which will run some MS programs, but not all, and not all well.

Anyhow, as this is a non-support thread in a non-support sub-forum, ask any question in a new thread in the appropriate support area. 

Good luck, enjoy the OS, the forums and the learning curve.

(PS: Elementary OS is not officially supported in the main support areas of these forums, but you can post in Other OS support sub-section for any questions about it.)

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2014-02-11
> **DukeNukem_2417 said:**
> Should've posted here yesterday, but meh....

DukeNukem 2417 here---just got interested in Ubuntu starting last year and decided to take the plunge with the admittedly outdated Ubuntu 9.10.  I've never used a Linux-based system before, so....this should be interesting (if I can get it to run on my old PC).

Hi DukeNukem_2417 (and others). 9.10? Don't go there. You won't get much, if any, support here. It reached end-of-life a looooong time ago. 

Install a supported release, like the very stable 12.04 LTS (good place for a newcomer) or 13.10. They are both directly upgradeable to 14.04 LTS when it is released in April. LTS releases have five years support.

Speaking of support, this is a non-support thread in a non-support section so feel free to ask about releases or anything else in a new thread in the support sections. Good luck! ;)

---

### Post by marx404 on 2014-02-23
I am marx404 and I was a Windows user...haha.

I am Windows saavy, comfortable in the command line and registry, not new to Ubuntu, but never had any real experience other than playing around with unsuccessful installs (somewhere around 2009?) ...that is until I tried Lubuntu 13.10 last month after MS declared XP's death toll. Suffice to say that I am now kicking myself for not making the switch years ago. Loving Lubuntu.

### Post by Michael_Duchemin on 2014-03-01
Hi everybody. 

I'm Mike and I'm a newcomer to Ubuntu.  Up until now I've been a lifelong Windows user.  I played around with Ubuntu 11.04 a few years ago using Wubi on Windows Vista, but never really got things to work correctly (wireless connectivity, etc.)  A couple of weeks ago, I decided to partition and try a dual boot install of Ubuntu 13.10 using a burned installation DVD.  I have not booted Vista on my laptop since.  I feel like a kid at a candy store so far with all the free and open source programs available.  

During my day job I work a lot in Microsoft Office Visual Basic (primarily Access and Excel) to automate business reporting, and I've dabbled some in C and Java.  I'm still learning the lay of the land, but I'm really impressed with Ubuntu so far.  I used Unix for some things in college a dozen years ago, so the terminal prompt is not entirely alien to me.  I'm excited to check out PostgreSQL and some of the other software packages that come standard with the system.

### Post by ncooke98 on 2014-03-04
Hello all!!

Ubuntu newbie here.  Spent some time quite a few years ago studying computer science.  Not much came from it, but it planted the seed, so to speak.  Recently installed Ubuntu on an old, archaic PC, and was blown away at how well it ran on a 6+ year old machine.  I've since installed Ubuntu on 2 more PC's in the house....Ubuntu is taking over my life!  Lol...

I've been trying to keep tabs on the Ubuntu Phone...it's very interesting, and something that I would be excited try out!

My latest learning breakthrough:  Trying to compile a C++ program with the C compiler doesn't work.  Learnt that one ma-self!  <facepalm>

The thing that's cool with Ubuntu is that you can get as technical as you want with it.  If I just want to turn it on to surf the web, it works great.  But if I want to dig into the system, so to speak, there is so much there.  You can continue to peel back the layers, and there's always more.  Sometimes it can be a bit overwhelming....

Okay...back to my "Hello, cruel cruel world" C++ program that's now working!!

Excited to be part of a growing community.

Nathan

### Post by sgofferj on 2014-03-28
Hah, thx. That forum font size is a bit compact for my old eyes :D.

Ok... let's go then.

Hi, I am Stefan and I have - ehm - Ubuntu :). I guess you can say, I'm kinda Linux veteran. I started tinkering with Slackware in 1996. After some break because I couldn't get anything to run, my ego got the better of me and I started again with SuSE 5.1 in October 1997. Since 1999, Linux is on my desktop and since 2000, it's pretty much my exclusive desktop system. Until now, I have been using SuSE / Opensuse all the way. I'm (among others) SuSE Certified Linux Trainer and have been leading a Linux training center for a couple of years about 10 years ago. Later I specialized on networking and security and (in my job, at least) strayed a bit from the path towards Cisco and Check Point. I even have a Microsoft Certified Professional cert somewhere in my lowest drawer - but only because my boss forced me to do this :D. I'm also pretty active with IP communications, and running - again among others - an Asterisk PBX at home since 2002.
I'm still very focused, means I'm pretty good in "my" stuff but e.g. don't have much more than user-level knowledge of black magic stuff like systemd, let alone hardware... I also have only basic coding skills (besides the usual bash and Perl stuff, of course).
Over the last few releases, I got more and more annoyed with Opensuse. Mainly details, but you know how it is... many drops make an ocean - or something like that.
Anyways, some guy who I really respect told me to have a look at Ubuntu and as recently my trusty old Cisco PIX at home burned out and I quickly needed a firewall, I installed Ubuntu Server 12.04 LTS in a VM and that's now my firewall. So far, I'm happy but - boy - do you guys have different philosophies in some areas... It's like learning the basics all over again...
But I have "big plans". My home server is a system that has "grown organically", means there's a ton of services with the configs taken over release after release - some configs are 10+ years old from the very beginning of my home-server times... There's some major remodeling to do be done and I'm thinking, Ubuntu server as host system and them stuffing the services into different vboxes.

What else...? Right... I'm a bit of a privacy buff and I REALLY dislike Google, first and foremost because of their "don't be evil"-BS while they are the single most evil entity in the universe with all their spying on users and even non-users. Besides that, I'm ex-military and prefer communicating in "direct" language, means I say/write what I think - not more and not less. Admittedly, my language *can* sometimes be a bit "colorful" :).

Well, so far for that... Let's see what's going on here... Generally I do prefer mailing lists (and I am subscribed to ubuntu-users) but nowadays, everything must be nice and colorful and AJAX and BS, so mailing lists usually are rather sparsely frequented and one has better chances to find or get infos in a web forum. But hey - I'm not all against GUIs! I love GUIs! See my working place:

[IMG]http://stefan.gofferje.net/images/workplace.jpg[/IMG]

GUIs are great...! You can open many console windows side by side! :D

---

### Post by Repro77 on 2014-04-01
Hi everyone!

As XP was nearing end of life, I started to look for alternatives, and started to look into the various Linux systems, and decided on Ubuntu 12.04. I installed it as a dual boot w/xp on my Lenovo R400 laptop, and after partitioning the drive with Gparted, the install went smoothly. I have to thank a few video posters on youtube for their help, that made things much easier!

Everything is working as it should, wireless, DVD playback, etc after some tweaking and installs of variuous packages...

For some reason, XP shows in GRUB as Vista (said that during install as well), but boots into XP just fine.
Mute button on the laptop doesn't work properly. It will mute, but won't unmute. Volume controls work fine, and will unmute if pressed either way.
I'd love to turn off the touchpad....very sensitive!

Anyway, getting more used to Ubuntu every day, and I like it so far!

I have to say, one major reason I chose Ubuntu was the community, excellent doc, and how-to's.

---

### Post by Noah_Heck on 2014-04-16
So, I decided about 9 months ago that I should get comfortable with a linux command line in order to get better at my job (web dev on a SAAS platform), so I began exploring, loaded Linux Mint into a VM on my Windows PC, and started completing all of the online tutorials I could find. After a while, I decided I was comfortable enough to dual boot my laptop with a Linux distro and began exploring again. I tested every desktop environment I could find (including every official Ubuntu derivative I could read). I settled on standard Ubuntu w/ Unity because it fits my work processes to a T. So then I had Ubuntu on my laptop that went everywhere with me, including to work. Then I wiped the hard drive on my work machine and now, Ubuntu is the only OS on that computer. 

Keep in mind that at this point, less than 9 months had passed since I began my effort to get into and learn Linux. During that time, I have become quite familiar with this forum, though I've always been able to find the answers to my questions from the answers already provided on a thread somewhere, so this is in fact my first post here.

Here's the thing that made me feel like I needed to get registered and post this:

Ubuntu 14.04 LTS is being released tomorrow, and I find myself staying up later than normal, constantly searching for any new tidbit of information related to the release that I can find. While sitting here with the minor heart palpitations I'm experiencing, I realized that I haven't been this excited about anything to do with a computer since about half-way through college. This is the true power of what I believe Ubuntu is, and I am so excited to be a part of it.

---

### Post by kiwi_kirsch on 2014-04-21
introducing.. i so hoped this'd be a purely technical forum instead of such a social one either ;)

i started with Lubuntu 13.10 on my "little dell" (netbook, 1×1,6ghz & 1gb) a week before win'xp support stopped, tried Lubuntu 14.04 beta which pretty much took the **** out of me, then aksed Xubuntu 13.10 for help and almost instantly loved it. it is now running on my "big dell" either (2×3,4ghz, 4gb) and on a friends dusty and untouched thinkpad t60 i offered to check and/or set up for her (old machines, all of them). all three Xubuntus have now successfully graduated to 14.04 and i am here in case i loose patience with things that don't work the way i think they should =)

### Post by Bruce_Reid on 2014-04-22
Greetings, Ubutu folks,

How nice to find resources like this to help grease the skids for us newcomers!

I was led to Ubutu (mythbuntu specifically) by my search for subscription-free recording options. I'm a long-time Tivo user, but I have increasingly come to think that I am not getting as much value from the service as the fees I'm continually paying. To make matters worse, I discontinued service on two of my three Tivo boxes, and found that with the newest one (a Tivo Premier), the box is rendered completely useless without the service; can't even change channels, much less record or play anything. I think Tivo should be ashamed for that, and they have left a very bitter taste in my mouth.

I have some past experience with SLES, but not with Ubuntu yet, in fact I haven't yet installed Ubuntu, because I was hoping to find out if I'm on the right track to find a Tivo replacement before spending too much time to discover the answer on my own.

I'm expecting to move to a new home in a completely different area than my current place of residence, so I will be free to choose what sort of TV source works best, whereas I'm currently rather tied to cable. I'm interested to learn what might be considered the most popular recording source for what I'm trying to achieve, including those that offer premium channels that require some decrypting/decoding, and what are some good options for tuners that can do that sort of thing, making the recorded material available for viewing via a LAN in at least two different rooms.

I tried searching the mythbuntu forum for the keyword 'recommendation', and browsing the results, but that approach didn't easily produce the sort of information I'm hoping to find.

Any help will be greatly appreciated!

---

### Post by jasonvp on 2014-04-23
Hi gang -

I just joined the forums recently and figured I'd introduce myself.  An old Dilbert cartoon from the summer of '95 pretty much sums me up, sans the beard and suspenders:

[IMG]http://dilbert.com/dyn/str_strip/000000000/00000000/0000000/000000/20000/1000/000/21021/21021.strip.gif[/IMG]

I've been a happy UNIX user since '91, and a Linux user since around '92 or '93.  I spent a lot of time and effort while at college working with Linux to make it usable on the desktop.  That includes doing a lot of X11 customization and configuration, scouring the 'net (before web browsers were really in use) looking for useful software to run, and even "yelling" at the folks at WordPerfect to get a SCO UNIX version released so I could use a real word processor on my desktop.

(Remember the days when Linux could run static SCO binaries?  Does anyone even remember SCO UNIX?)

My primary field is that of network engineering and architecture.  But I'm ultimately a UNIX geek at heart.  At some point in my professional career, I got tired of pushing Linux to perform properly on the desktop and just switched to Macs for that role.  Apple moving to Intel made that migration much easier for me.  I can run all of my useful business-related applications natively without any emulation, and still have UNIX under the covers.  It's perfect.

But, I still actively use Linux in a server role.  For the last several years, I've been primarily using CentOS at home, because the places I've worked at all use RHEL in production.  It seemed like a good idea to have some similarities between the two environments, and I actually dig the way RHEL/CentOS configures and lays things out.

So why Ubuntu?  Stay with me... I have a self-built server in my basement that was running VMWare's ESXI.  It's there that I ran all of my in-house VMs.  But managing VMWare is such a pain in the butt; that Windows-based .NET application is a steaming pile of...  Historically, the *other* choices of hypervisors haven't been up to the task, which is why I went ESXI.  But, the others have matured finally!  By coincidence, I just picked up a new Mac Pro for use in video and picture editing.  My old Mac Pro: a 2 x 3.46GHz 6-core (12 total) Xeon monster with 48G of RAM would make a *perfect* hypervisor.  The problem?  RHEL/CentOS don't support grub2 yet, and I'd need that to support the Mac's EFI.

Ubuntu to the rescue.  As it turns out, it installs and boots on the Mac Pro with no issues whatsoever.  Over the past weekend, I migrated all of my ESXI VMs over to it, converted them to KVM images, and they're up and running.  The old ESXI server?  It's been nuked from orbit, had Ubuntu installed on it, and is *also* running a KVM hypervisor.

I don't care to learn about Ubuntu while driving my *hypervisor* though.  So I've spun up another VM running it so that I can 'speriment with it.  So far, so good!

---

### Post by poet1 on 2014-04-25
Greetings my fleshy anthropomorphic human beings. Or **robots**, for you Google and other search engine crawlers.

My name is **poet1**. Some call me Dan. **You can call me either.** Nice to meet you, 'n stuff.

Now for something sortof different, but kind of related:

I have been a computer user since the early 90's. Make a long story short, I grew up with computers in the house.

**I have toyed with Ubuntu off-and-on ever since 6.xx releases.** Back in 2006, a school friend handed me the drug known as Ubuntu 6.xx, and ever since then I've been hooked. I have drifted from Ubuntu to others over the years. I have tried Fedora, Solaris, straight-up Debian, Mint. But, I come back to Ubuntu whenever I veer off. It's got kind, happy people working for it, who are more than willing to dish out their experience and help. I like the community. Thanks, again, for being helpful, y'all.

(And, despite the fact that my profile info says I'm running 8.xx, I'm not. I've been a member here for a number of years. The** Big Kahunas** at Ubuntu Forums decided **to require 10 posts** before you can adjust your profile a little while ago.)

I currently run Ubuntu Gnome 14.04 LTS. I switched from Unity because I like Gnome. That's it, really. No big reason for the switch from Unity to Gnome. No harsh feelings, or banana peels in the path of Unity developers.

**I may seek out the Documents Team for joining soon.** I am a good writer and writing is my passion.

OK, thanks for reading. Happy posting, and see you around!

**poet1**
