### Post by Sir_Ismicoo on 2014-04-25
Hello all, I thought I'd drop by and post an introduction.

I'm a long term Linux user who first dipped my toes in the water in the  late 1990s and who hasn't had a Windows based system at home for more  than a decade. Unfortunately I still have to use it at work but the  ending of XP support has given me another chance to pitch the use of  Linux. This time they're listening as installing Linux on our 15  computers will cost a lot less than buying 15 Windows 8 computers. My  boss is currently looking at Ubuntu, SolydX and openSUSE before  deciding.

On a personal note, I have installed pretty much every release of Ubuntu  on a spare computer and at the risk of being branded trollish found  them all to be pretty underwhelming. I have played with what feels like  hundreds of distros but stuck with Debian on my serious rigs. 14.04  however has made it on to my main computers and I've been using it every  day. It's faster and lighter than the previous versions *and* it  hasn't had any crashes in the week or so it's been installed. I can't  say with 100% accuracy that it is as good as I think or whether it's the  new anti-depressant / painkiller combination my doctor has me on but I  do like it :lol:

In case you are wondering where *Sir_Ismicoo* comes from, well, I was registering and I saw the captcha and thought "Holy crap! I've just been given a new on-line identity!"

### Post by Cbhihe on 2014-05-02
Hi all ! 

I am a total newbie here. The last time I touched something that resembled Linux was 25 years ago. I used to work on VAX (VMS), Convex and AIX mainframes, scripting llike mad, and programming in F77 dabbing in C, and enjoying myself most of the time. I owned VI then ! (Never cared much for Emacs).

Clouds did not exist then; the sky was always blue ! Everything was blissfully command line and if you wanted to print ps from a console you had o write your own filter yourself sp the printer could interpret your ps file dump. C++ was juuust about to come out and C programmers were considered exotic. 95% of people on the earth had no clue about what email or an IP address was. Go figure. Things have changed a bit now... you'd say.
So here I am. Migrating away from XP after suffering years of abuse at the hands of MS. 
I chose Ubuntu desktop augmented with Apache, Joomla  and mySql, first installed on a 5 year old HP workstation laptop.
  because it seems well supported by an active community
  it is stable and reasonably mainstream
  it is easy to install
  not for its GUI, which I positively dislike. Fact is the first thing I want to do is change it to something less clunky-looking.

Naturally, I have a couple of questions already. Will ask them in the Cafe next door.

Cheers and thanks for the welcome.

### Post by WinEunuchs2Unix on 2014-05-24
After 20+ Years of talking about Unix I've finally made it.  After 20+ years of wanting 64 bit processing I've finally got it.  After 7+ years of having a 64 bit processor I'm finally using it to it's full potential (along with 4GB of RAM where 1 GB sat idle).  Whilst I type a fresh install of Windows Vista 32 is hibernated in the background on the new 1TB sshd.  I went through the normal trials and tribulations of reversing /home encryption, intel 5/14/2014 graphics driver, Toshiba Satellite L300 brightness adjustment, reading ACPI 3.0 specifications to see how fan is controlled, //chrome:gpu un-blacklisting the GPU so Flash Player doesn't consume 100% CPU.

So far so good.  My user name is kind of corny compared to the cool ones others have but *shrugs*.

### Post by gtravis3 on 2014-05-26
Howdy,
I may have mentioned in other posts that we found a Dell laptop in a trash can while doing the old "lets go scavenging " routine.  I ended up putting a new keyboard, upgrading to max memory, and a new battery (a POS that I bought from FactoryDirect - never use them but I'll save that rant for later).
Interestingly enough the owner had left Windows on the machine and several files that she should have deleted. That made me very uncomfortable.
I have been using Apple computers since the good old Apple II days, and got to thinking that perhaps I could make this a command line machine. I have often piddled with the terminal / BSD Unix on my Macs. Did some googling and found ubuntu and after making a bootable startup USB stick, got rid of windows and replaced it with the ubuntu DE, which is dreadfully slow on this Dell xps m140. I got some advice and replaced it with Lubuntu, which I am much happer with.
SO....  that's how I have found myself in the land of ubuntu and linux.  I must say that so far the ubuntu forums are friendly and helpful, kind of like the Apple Discussion 'forums' were like 10 or so  years ago.  I hope that it will remain that way. 

Me; I am an old Navy Nuclear Power Trained Submariner.  How old, well the first boat that went to sea on was the Nautilus, SSN 571. You can google it to see how old. Retired from Nuclear Power and am doing better than I deserve.

### Post by Kale_Freemon on 2014-08-10
So, after a few years of going back and forth between Windows and various versions of Linux, I have decided that now would be a good time to introduce myself. My name is Kale Freemon, and I made the decision to dual boot.

Here's why. I love Linux, but I also love Windows (love being used very loosely). And whenever I switch from one to the other, I, at some point, find myself wanting to go back because there is something that I find very useful on either system. Therefore, I have decided that, instead of going back and forth with clean installs every few months, I'd just use both systems. If I need one, I'll use that one. It helps that I recently upgraded to an SSD and have a 3TB external drive.

So, I am currently running Windows 8.1 Pro alongside Xubuntu 14.04. I am happy.

I hope to be a value to this community, and that this community may be of value to me as well.

I hope everyone is doing well today also.

:)

### Post by Mike_Walsh on 2014-08-24
Hallo, everybody.....I'm yet ANOTHER Mike.

I've been playing about with these things since the late 70's. Started out with a Sinclair ZX80 (gawd, what a horrible 'keyboard'), and soon graduated to something with a proper one .....a Commodore 64.

Lost track of the machines I've used over the years. I've used MyCrudSoft all the way from 3.1 through to Win 7 (didn't like it, OR Vista (who did?)). I decided to try Linux back in late April this year; downloaded a bunch of different distros (Fedora, OpenSuse, Ubuntu, Zorin, various Puppies.....Mint, LinuxPC OS; the list goes on...). Figured out early on that Ubuntu was really easy to work with, and I'm one of those odd folk who actually like Unity!

I migrated fully back in May.

I look after an elderly relative full-time, so it gives me a bit of spare time to indulge one of my favourite hobbies. I've been really impressed with the level of expertise in these forums, along with a LOT of friendliness, and a general willingness to help other folk out.....you're a great bunch. I hope I can manage to give back as much to the community as I've already received; I don't for a moment doubt that I shall be learning up until the day I start pushing the daisies through...!

Just wanted to say "hi", and to tell everybody....I'm hooked.

Regards,

Mike.

---

### Post by craig10x on 2014-08-24
@Mike: you're not odd...lots of us here like Unity :D
There's just a rather vocal minority here that doesn't...so sometimes you can get the impression that they are the majority ;)
And, Welcome...

---

### Post by Mike_Walsh on 2014-08-24
Hi, Craig.

Actually, it's funny you should have mentioned that. I've just been playing about in Xubuntu, and made it look rather 'Gnome'-like (lol)...

At least, I did, after I'd figured out the business of leaving ONE separator in the transparent AND expanded condition; THAT'S what keeps things at both ends of the taskbar; yay!

Nah, I like Unity; but I've also got a bit of a soft spot for the way Gnome looks. I'm an inveterate 'tweaker'; can't leave things alone for too long now, can I? :p And running no fewer than six distros (seven if you count Puppy 'Slacko' on the thumbdrive!), well.....gives me ENDLESS hours of fun, and LOTS to play around with. Funny thing is, I actually haven't 'tweaked' Ubuntu for almost a month now; I've actually got it how I want it, now. *Gasp; quelle horreur.....sacre bleu!*

Never mind; still got several others to tweak. I'm having more fun than I have had for YEARS...

Regards,

Mike.

### Post by bapoumba on 2014-08-25
Please be careful with links and such things here, we are quite concerned with spammish posts that appear to use our traffic to get audience. The above site hosts many trackers and intrusive pops.

---

### Post by Mike_Walsh on 2014-08-25
Agreed. Just checked it out myself; I use Ghostery & Adblock plus with both Firefox and Chrome, and there's some 8 or 9 trackers.....all blocked with Ghosty, of course, but just goes to show. Several 'pop-ups', too... It's not as bad as one of PCWorld's pages about switching to Linux; I counted a total of 87 trackers some 3-4 months ago; as of yesterday, it's since dropped to a 'mere' 32..!

A lot of folk don't realise just how many companies out there are determined to "hitch a ride" on other people's traffic, as bapoumba says; so, PLEASE.....BE aware. Many times, the web site owner isn't even aware of the trackers on his/her website... I believe it's facilitated, in large part, by the domain suppliers.

Regards,

Mike.

---

### Post by Chris_Rogers on 2014-09-01
Hello all, my name is Chris Rogers my username is Crogers731 

I am new to this forum and I am here to listen well and learn all that I can as well as help anyone, anyway that I possibly can. I am 41 years old and own a construction company that I have gotten bored with and am now in my fourth year of six getting a masters in Computer Science. I am like I stated a newbie here, and to the fully operational technical side, though I have been dealing with computers for over eight years. I am now out of the trail and error side,( with no education meaning reading, LISTENING, ASKING, and learning) to now listening and learning. I enjoy and thrive to learn the correct way now, I read everything that I can, and I listen well. I am not afraid to ask questions and I do as explained too without adding my two sense. I am fairly new to using Linux, which I kick myself in the a** everyday for not starting with Linux over eight years ago. I am here to get any support from this community with my learning about Linux, and how to best use Linux. I bought a brand new server about a month ago, it is a Lenovo TD 340 which I am in need of some guidance in setting up properly, starting with installing Redhat and Windows server 2012 R2 operating systems on it, setting up the PCI adapter, and configuring the RAID properly. I have over six desktops and nine laptops that I am looking to get setup as workstations for this server. I am building an entire office network with Active Directory, Websites, software, and programs running Virtual servers and workstation using VMware, citrus, and Linux to include in this. I am currently taking LFS101x Introduction to Linux from the Linux Foundation, and am scheduled to take RHCSA Rapid Track Course (RH199) starting in October, I am also scheduled to take the tests for Network+, and Security+. So thats a good start on me and whats going on with things, so yes I need some help, maybe even a mentor. 
                                                             Kind thoughts, Chris Rogers

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2014-09-27
> **LYXDhE3 said:**
> 
I'm glad to see that link on building a Linux computer in your signature, Bucky Ball.
8-)

It is dated, but the principals remain the same. Perhaps a good starting point and take it from there, using contemporary hardware. I bang on about this all the time, but a decent power supply is the heart of the machine. Name brand, 80+ rating. Few more bucks perhaps, but worth it. I've bought computer towers and taken the silver box PSU out and replaced with a 'safe' one. Keep the other for a spare, and if you grab a quality PSU, you won't need it anyhow. ;)

Welcome to you and all new comers.

### Post by jgreene510 on 2014-10-22
Hiya Forum,

I won't attempt to say I'm any good with Linux, but I love the FOSS idea in general and whenever I can I try to use those principles. ### Post by Nicolas_Deseez on 2014-10-24
Hello everyone!
 

 My name's Nicolas and I've been using Linux, in parallel to Windows since the early 2000s and solely since about 5 years. I've tried many distros since I started (Mandrake, Mandriva, Fedora, Suse, Ubuntu and its various flavors, Linux Mint, and Debian). I was using Mint for a few years, then switched to Debian.  
 

 After buying a new computer that shipped with Windows 8 I quickly felt the need of getting Linux up and running the machine asap and I ran into a plethora of difficulties with AMD graphic chipsets while trying to get Debian to work with full video acceleration. I threw in the towel and thought about trying Ubuntu again. It had been many years since I used Ubuntu and boy am I impressed how the OS and the community has evolved. It's a pleasure to see how far it has come and how much has been accomplished. Needless to say... everything works fine “out of the box” on my new machine. I'm impressed and very happy.  
 

 I played a bit with Unity, simple and intuitive but missed the flexibility and speed of Cinnamon which I really got hooked to while using Debian. Browsing a few pages on the net It was very simple to get it to work.  
 

 Kudos to the wonderful Ubuntu community. Delighted to be here!
 

 :)

---

### Post by Ric_Helm on 2014-10-27
Hi,
I am not sure how this particular thread will be useful. However, it looks fun, and perhaps allow us to clarify the skills we may or may not have.

My background is Apple, from Apple DOS, through Mac OS 10.6. I hhve used and setup numerous machines.For some reason people call me the GoTo guy regarding computers and networking...That said, or that others think of me as, I have hacked a number of Mac programs with Res-Edit. (the most wonderful tool!!)
I miss CopyPaste, the most incredidle Control Panel both for Mac OS7 through 9, and for windows. (copy up to 100 items without having to paste to clear the clipboard) !!  WHERE ARE YOU DUDE??!!

I have only used "dmesg", "mesg" and apt-get in the terminal..the "man" page is too cryptic for me to understand. What Linux quick reference apps downloaded for iPhone, and android..are not helpful to me..because they all assume that I already know the command... I am only able to describe the action or result I need and have no idea how to accomplish this in the terminal.  My solution has so far been.."Find an App in the repository" which does not always work, and sometimes really messes things up.

I was once told, way back when personal computers used DOS systems, before hard drive OS's that so long that everything is backed up, whatever you do will not break the machine. That was before that OS's became so huge, and hard to "clone entire system onto an external media.

I have used Ubuntu 12.10, have installed Ubuntu 12.04, and 13.04 on other's computers. i have recently installed UbuntuStudio 14.04 and am very frustrated with it.

How's that for an introduction??  Longer? / Shorter?

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2014-10-28
When you post on a thread you are subscribed to it, which means that when anyone else posts on it you are notified. Therefore, when you post on this thread, everyone that has posted on this thread will be notified there has been a post on the thread. Your's is post #2727 so a lot of people have posted on this thread, they will all be notified you did unless they've unsubscribed from the thread. 

Therefore it is a good way to introduce yourself to and connect with a lot of the community in one hit and a valuable resource.

Welcome. ;)

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2014-11-07
Glad you could make it, sebia. The bad news is that 13.04 has reached end-of-life (quite some time ago) and is no longer supported. If you need any help with installing a supported version, please post a new thread with a descriptive title in the appropriate support section (not here ;)). 

Good luck and welcome.

### Post by tidymonkey81 on 2014-12-01
Good morning (from the UK),

Thanks for welcoming me here. I've been dallying with Ubuntu Linux for a few months now and am feeling increasingly more comfortable. I will pop in here from time-to-time to seek advice on ubuntu and linux matters I'm sure. I do have one question so please bear with me a short while until the end.

To give my background, I am a secondary/high school teacher of Physics, General Science, Maths and Statistics (BSc in Physics, i.e. technically quite competent)). I am currently in the process of applying to Computer Science masters courses in england so that I may study part-time and eventually add IT and technology routes to my career prospects. I am currently interested in computer security but hope to develop a better idea of my interests as my involvement progresses.

As part of this process and also to enable me to apply to (near) entry-level jobs in the IT industry I am keen to increase my Linux competence. To this end I am looking for courses to study that will earn me certification. I understand that there are different flavours (Red Hat vs Debian vs others) and that my choice can narrow my employment possibilities down that path. I am not against this principal but I woul dprefere, if possible, a broader cerificate. It is my hope that after I gain a few certificates 

My questions to you guys are:

1. Where on these boards can I best ask these questions?
2. Where are the best places to earn a relatively low-cost (if not free!) certificate (that will show I have basic competence in Linux)?
3. Is there such a thing as a certficate in Linux e.g. broadly knowledgeable about linux systems? I had a look at the EdX courses that the Linux Foundation offered and it seemed way too introductory for me.
4. If I had to choose between e.g. Red Hat and Debian where am I most likely to find the larger breadth of future opportunities? (i realise this is a question likely to invoke mixed responses so perhaps just a little advice on this matter is necessary)
5. Can you offer any othe radvice that might enable me to get my foot into the door of the IT industry so that I may work in this arena whilst studying (I am currently looking at linux admin posts). I am looking for the broadest experience available to me to enable me to see the working nature of various sections of the IT industry.

Thank you very much for reading this far, I look forward to talking with many of you along my journey.

---

hey all, glad to be here. just got started with ubuntu and linux mint and like both distros well. don't seem to be hard to use but i can tell that there is alot to learn about each one of them.been using windows 7 and no complaints with it. don't like the windows 8.1 change that i am seeing so this is why i have decided to learn linux. i am a ham radio operator and have been in the electronics repair for consumer electronics for years. i am now into antique tube radio repair which is real electronics instead of all this modern throw away crap they call electronics. yes as you can tell i am old. i also take other peoples desktop computers and bring new life into them and donate them to the people who are in need of a computer but cannot afford to buy one. that is a little about myself. thanks for the space and glad to be here.

### Post by Kurt_Alan on 2015-01-05
I started Linux around 2000 with Red Hat Enterprise. Installs then were more difficult, so I bought the Enterprise version for tech support. I was still pronouncing "Linux" as "Leenux."

I started with Ubuntu at Dapper Drake, 6.x.  Jaunty Jackalope was my favorite distro from the point-of-view of UI aesthetics. I completed the GNOME tutorial and panel manipulation became my UI sweetspot. I then ran screaming from Unity. For the first time I considered leaving Ubuntu. I then made my peace with Unity and tried to understand it and optimize it.  I have now found my new sweetspot and that is Xubuntu. XCFE DE gives me just the precision I need as well as a minimalist aesthetic and it is very fast. I still run Unity in a dual-boot config as my backup OS.  Unity is fast and stays out of the way but it is rigid. I hate being forced to not being able to put apps on the Desktop.

In the beginning my forum was linuxquestions.org where I registered in the early 2000's. Now I use ubuntuforums.org

Linux is my primary OS.  I will use Win7 in a pinch for software that requires it (e.g. OED, for which I spent ~$250.00).

---

### Post by Mark_Harder on 2015-01-09
Howdy!  I'm a retired biochemist with many sci/tech projects in the mix at the moment.  One of these is bringing up the Dell Precision 690 I purchased used at a campus surplus sale for $125.  This is a monster of a machine.  There are 2 Xeon 5xxx quad-core processors, 8GB installed with 24GB of unused capacity right now, and a 1kW power supply. It seems a little too much bargain to be true, though.  It's a little flaky, but I can't find anything fatally wrong with it so far.  There are a lot of these boxes out there on eBay and Amazon for little $$.  Anyway, it's about 3 feet tall and weighs 55 lbs, so it's not going anywhere, certainly not to a cafe hot spot.  I have a version 8 Mathematica disk that will load to Linux and I want to try it out on a really powerful machine.  I also have a molecular dynamics package that I want to use for some play/research on protein dynamics.  Oh, I'm fed up with Windows and I want to check out the Linux world.  There are lots of interesting apps available for it.  I didn't know about the cave mapping apps before browsing the Ubuntu catalog, to take one example.  So far, Ubuntu seems to present a rather steep learning curve, but I'm persistent.

---

### Post by b.w.2 on 2015-02-11
Hey, all! My name is Bran, and I'm relatively new to Linux / *buntu, having installed Ubuntu for the first time a year and a half ago. The siren song that originally lured me to Linux was the potential to customize my OS. Looking around, it seemed like I found the most information online about Ubuntu, and so I decided to give it a try. I enjoyed Ubuntu enough to stay with it as my sole OS for a year, until I heard the siren song again, after reading about how easily tweaked Kubuntu could be. Kubuntu is now my sole OS, and it is with this that I intend to stay for the foreseeable future. I know next to nothing about programming, and only the basics when it comes to networking. I'm the kind of user who simply enjoys tinkering. This is probably the reason why I've mastered the art of reinstalling K/Ubuntu in such a short time.

I've read a lot of posts in here over the last year and a half, and I figured it was finally time to introduce myself and say 'thank you' to all the people who have already asked so many of the questions that have come up for me, and to those who answered those questions. The community and the OS family are great, and I'm happy to have strayed from the mainstream long enough to have discovered that the real party is over here :-)

Cheers!
Bran.

### Post by z3r0d4t4 on 2015-02-12
Hello and good afternoon everybody! 

My name is Brian, and I just recently completed a full installation of the Ubuntu 14.04 distro over Win7 a couple of days ago. OK, to be honest it was more like four formats and three fresh re-installs as I found myself corrupting the prior installations within a very short period of time. But hey, you know what?! Personally the transition between operating systems hasn't been an easy one due to  the fact that I have been a devoted Windows user since the  induction of their NT platform. So in my defense, the majority of people are more apt to learn new processes by way of trial and error, right? Right right! \\:D/ 

Anyways, I'm still having difficulties within the Terminal (there's a big surprise), and find myself still trying to get into the Linux groove. Hopefully it will not take another 20+ years to become as adept in Linux as I was with Windows. Just wanted to drop by and say hello...I'm sure you will run into me more often than you really want to.

Let the keyboard bashing commence!

### Post by DennisFolsom on 2015-02-21
Hi all -

I have been an Ubuntu user for several years now, currently running 12.04 LTS on a dual-boot AMD/Nvidia based e-machines PC, which runs Windows XP on the other side.  A few months ago, I had hardware issues, which prompted me to start the search for a replacement desktop PC.  I decided to get something with Windows 7 pre-loaded, then install Ubuntu 14.04 LTS.  To date, I have not delved into Wine, though I plan to on the new machine.  My goal is to ultimately wean myself from Windows altogether.

Fortunately, I started some research on these forums and elsewhere before just buying a new PC and trying to install Ubuntu on it.  I have learned a lot about UEFI, Secure Boot, partioning, etc.  Using TiddlyWiki, I have assembled a document with lots of links to tutorials, installation instructions, and other resources.  Using these resources, I have mapped out the steps I intend to take in preparing the new PC and installing Ubuntu.  I plan to post it somewhere, when the mission is accomplished.

From what I am finding right now, I think the community needs a good and straightforward "cookbook" for installing Ubuntu on a current PC in a dual-boot setup.  I'm not sure if mine will fill the bill, but it may help someone.

Phone rang - I'll post again to tell of the PC I have ordered.

Any suggestions for where I should post my reports?

---

### Post by DennisFolsom on 2015-02-21
I have ordered an Acer **AXC-605-UR2B** from Newegg.  It would be here now, but delivery got delayed, due to snowstorms on the way.  It is now in Auburn, ME, and should be delivered to my house on Monday afternoon.  This model has an Intel i5 processor, a 1 TB hard drive, a DVD writer, and 4 GB of ram (with 1 slot available for more).  It is a fairly compact box, compared to my old mid-tower.  I managed to find a listing in [http://linux-hardware-guide.com/](http://linux-hardware-guide.com/) that indicates that someone else has run linux on this machine and found it compatible.  I think this PC should be more than adequate for my use.  :p

### Post by Ashton_van_Niekerk on 2015-02-26
Hay all, I was referred here from a different thread, so i'll just copy-paste my post from there:
[I]
Hay all!

So I finally installed Ubuntu! Actually it is the philosophy of Ubuntu   that brought me here! I'm not a Philosophy major, but in the few modules   I have done on philosophy I have learned to love the concpet of  Ubuntu.  It says "we are, what we are through what we are to other's". In other words, the essence of our being can only be found in our relationship to other human beings.

Ubuntu OS embodies this philosophy because it is humans helping other   humans. The focus being on collective humanity, rather than an   individual.

Therefore I believe the experience of this OS is not complete without   it's supporting community. I would really like to make friends on this   forum, to make this experience complete :smile:.

Anyway, my experience so far has been mostly possitve! I'm still trying   to find my way around and have not figured out the notification system   yet. I'm using the LTS vesion (14.04). I'm sure I'll catch on in no   time.[/I]

Also, in case you were wondering, I'm using Ubuntu as my main OS. Windows is still on a septate partition, but I'm slowly migrating.

Hoping to make lots of friends!
Ashton

### Post by bardo2 on 2015-03-23
> **jim103 said:**
> Thanks bardo, 

i have to say, looking through all the threads on here, most of the technical language baffles me. Is it difficult to master?

Hi jim,

well, who knows, depends on many things. And i am new to the forums, so i can only talk from my own experience. And that is as follows: Some years ago, i had been a pretty good Windows software developer, but i had enough of the instability, i encountered at the time, especially since i lost valuable data several times due to it. When i decided to move to 'nix (about 5 years ago), i carefully designed a plan involving many steps (like virtual machine, WuBi - at the time, and so forth). After around 3 years time of daily working with the computer, there was a day, when i realized: I am at least as comfortable with linux as i had been with Win, which was a lot. But i am no longer developing, more like an advanced user/geek.

But my case may be somewhat rare: i had been a tec-ppl before, and was very willing to learn - on my own! But still there are areas, i do know nothing about, where it makes sense to get involved with a larger community. Everyone may contribute something, someday, and those looking knowledgable are standing on the shoulders of giants anyway. ;-)

From what i learned in the meantime, i would say: just dont think, the move would be "a piece of cake". Those who make you believe, it would be, are not telling the whole story (at least, if you are using the computer for something else than just browsing the net). It is involving, challenging at times, takes a lot of time and willingness/focus. But the rewards are there! Freedom of choice, unbelievable stability, extremely powerful tools (especially from the commandline). So today, i can say: NEVER would i be willing to return, even after looking into the upcoming Wayland release. i gained so much from the move, but - as mentionned before . i may be a somewhat special case and you may think differently.

Just hinting you to have a closer look at your own expectations... ;-)

### Post by paul207 on 2015-05-15
Just joined the forums, new to Ubuntu but not to Linux. Been using Linux as my primary OS since 1998. Stopped using Linux and moved to Mac from 2008 until now. Moved because I know I will be at the point of "forced obsolescence" with my hardware and Apple's support of the OSX version it can run. That may be a couple of years out but it is inevitable. Since my hardware runs Linux very nicely and likely will for several more years, I have dumped OSX and gone back to Linux. This thread is not to start a flame war but I will just say I absolutely loved OSX. It is the single most polished OS I have ever used to date and I have used many. But...there is always a "but" - Apple, like Microsoft, is in it for the $ and the best interest of the consumer is not theirs. I own my hardware and even though it's 7 years old, it flat out screams so I want to get as much use out of it as I can. As many of you already know, Apple hardware can be finicky about OS's other than OSX, but Ubuntu has been the slickest and easiest to get everything working on, so I am sticking with it, at least until I get some other Intel hardware in the future.

So, I am here because I truly love the FOSS community: I did my thesis on the development model, I have been involved with the Gnome desktop for several years, not in an official capacity, but as a user, mod on the now defunct "gnome support" forums, and as a HIG tester. I hope to be able to help people, if I can, and to certainly ask questions, because none of us knows it all :-) Glad to be here.

Ubuntu 14.04 LTS on a 2008 Mac Pro 3.1, 2 x 4 core Xenons at 2.8 Ghz, 24 GB ram, 4.5 TB disk space, Nvidia GTX285.

### Post by Idjit_BoB on 2015-05-24
Hi there! My name Idjit BoB is a trail name. I got it while on the Appalachian Trail (AT). With some of the stupid stuff I have done like sneaking up on a black bear to get a better photograph... well let's just say the name suits me. The AT is the granddaddy of trails, or so they say. In case you're wondering, no I didn't thru-hike. When I was doing serious hiking I mostly hiked sections of the AT. I live in Pennsylvania Amish Country so there are too many good trails in my area of the world to limit oneself to the AT. Glacier National Park is not necessarily in my back yard, but has been a special place for my hikes.

I am retired from Sunoco Chemicals and worked in the refinery for the past 20 some years in Marcus Hook, PA. I only mention it to let you know that I am not a software, network or OS oriented person.

Now that I have some spare time on my hands I been tooling around with some old laptops which just aren't worth a darn any more... at least that is what I thought. Then someone in a forum at Computer Hope mentioned a Linux OS by the name of Zorin which is an Ubuntu & Debian based OS. The laptops now run as if they were new, and I was happy to see how much more stable they have been than with that other OS. We won't mention Microsoft.

Welcome to the world of Linux. Thank you.

As a side note, my mom worked for Bell Labs in Holmdel, NJ in the typist pool. It was the late 60s and she would come home from work and talk about her day. More than a few times she mentioned how she was working on a manual for an engineer on some sort of system called UNIX. From what I understand - and believe me it ain't much - this is where UNIX began. I don't know much more than that.

Bell Labs was pretty interesting place that did a lot of basic research. In my senior year of high school I worked the summer as a general maintenance person there. I became friends with an Oxford grad from England. He drove on the wrong side of the road. Anyway, he was working on Fiber Optics. He explained to me that if they could figure out how to transmit data through this ceramic cable that it would revolutionize communications beyond anything as we have seen before. That was 1969.

Then there was this engineer who worked at Bell Labs on Telegraph Hill just up the road a piece. Telegraph Hill is where they built the first Telestar communications satellite. He dragged me into a thing called a horn to explain how he was confirming the big bang by listening to the echo on the edges of the universe. He was very excited. All I know is I got into trouble for not finishing the lawn cutting job I was tasked with for that day.

Back to UNIX. I used to have the third edition of UNIX laying around somewhere, but I regifted it to a Finnish friend who insisted that Linux was a Finnish invention. She also claims baseball was invented by the Finn's so I'm a little skeptical of her as an authority on Linux.

Anyway, I don't backpack anymore, but I still day hike. My health (all small stuff) won't let me take a serious hike. Rats!

My interests are varied though... along with hoping to learn more about Linux I enjoy photography and my grandkids... and of course those day hikes.

Sorry I was so long winded. Thanks for listening.

Best Regards
Idjit
Amish Country-PA, USA

### Post by Willy_Schott on 2015-06-05
Hello everyone, the name's Willy, located on some tropical island in Southeast Asia. Have been involved with Linux of several flavors for more than fifteen years now, but almost exclusively in server environments, where there is no ALT+F7. Most acquainted with the Debian family, I've started a passionate relationship with their youngest daughter Jessie on my desktop about three months ago. But lately we had to separate because of irreconcilable differences about my decision to adopt an AMD Radeon R9 child (from Curacao). I would have wanted two siblings attached via DVI and hardware accelerated, but she was adamant about only one VGA child and raising it the old-fashioned frame buffer way. Well, I'm old, but not THAT old. So I was starting to look around again, and right after terminating our relationship, I fell in love with a vivid vervet monkey--still feels a bit weird, but we're starting to get along well. Being closely related to Jessie's family, she was very suspicious in the beginning, and also wasn't on good terms with my adopted R9 baby, torturing me with black screens and forcing me to wear the crippling nomodeset flag in my grub2 command line all day long--until I managed to open her mind to the wondrous world of fglrx-updates. Things developed quickly, and now we have our two lovely DVI children who have already grown to their final FullHD resolution. Life is good again :-)

### Post by Welly Wu on 2015-06-13
I don't remember if I ever introduced myself to this community in the past, but I'm here today to do just that. My name is Mr. Welly Wu. I live in Nutley, New Jersey in the United States of America. I have been dabbling with both Microsoft Windows 7 and 8.1 64 bit and Ubuntu 64 bit LTS GNU/Linux along with the other major GNU/Linux distributions since April 2009. Eventually, I got frustrated and tired with Microsoft Windows and I permanently switched to the latest Ubuntu 64 bit LTS GNU/Linux distribution versions. I played with the other big GNU/Linux distributions like Red Hat Fedora, Red Hat Enterprise Linux, OpenSuSE Tumbleweed, Elementary OS Luna and Freya, Debian 7 and 8, etc., but they were not as easy and user friendly as Ubuntu especially the 64 bit LTS versions. The reason why I switched is because I found that the SteamOS + GNU/Linux platform had some of the major AAA PC titles that I was interested in and I was constantly concerned with the poor insecurity of Microsoft Windows compared to GNU/Linux. I eventually bought a System76 Lemur Ultra Thin (lemu4) notebook PC back in August 2012 and I gave it to my close friend. I now have a new 2015 ZaReason Zeto desktop PC and a Lenovo IdeaPad Y510P notebook PC both of which run Ubuntu 14.04.2 64 bit LTS GNU/Linux. Both are 100% GNU/Linux compatible. Ubuntu 64 bit LTS GNU/Linux in particular suits me just fine because it is extremely reliable, stable, and secure. PC gaming is a lot easier because I no longer use Microsoft products and services. I remember when installing a Microsoft Windows PC game required DirectX and .NET technologies and that could cause problems especially with older titles designed to run on older versions of Microsoft Windows. I no longer have to worry about backwards compatibility issues when using SteamOS + GNU/Linux. Ubuntu just works right out of the box. I doubt that I will ever purchase a new Microsoft Windows certified desktop or notebook PC in the foreseeable future either. Ubuntu does everything that I need without any problems. I am saving a tremendous amount of time and money by using Ubuntu compared to Microsoft Windows. While I do prefer FLOSS technologies, I am pragmatic and I choose the best tools for the job so I do purchase, download, install, and use closed source, proprietary, and commercial software applications when it is the obvious choice to do so. SteamOS + GNU/Linux PC gaming is growing organically and I have high hopes that it will become a major platform that PC game developers will target in the coming years ahead. There are a number of AAA, AA, and A SteamOS + GNU/Linux PC game titles available right now and each passing year seems to get better than the previous year in terms of the future software products that become available for GNU/Linux. I'm talking about software titles not just PC games. I think that GNU/Linux will eventually become a major player in the highly competitive desktop market worldwide and Ubuntu will lead the way.

---

### Post by Scott_Paulson on 2015-07-29
Hello,
I am a newbie to Linux, but have been aware of it for many years.  I use computers to be productive in a SOHO environment.  Mostly email, spreadsheets, some documents, CAD, and trading (FX and stocks).
I built this computer (Desktop) about 5 years ago, because I wanted to learn and wanted a challenge.  I made a mid-range unit with dual monitors.  I am not a gamer, but I wanted reasonable speed and graphics quality.
Recently, I have begun the move from Windows 7 to Ubuntu.  I have been watching Category5.tv television for about a year, and finally decided to make a Linux installation in my current machine.  
I initially installed 14.04 LTS.  I have just installed 15.04.  The learning curve is quite steep, but the Ubuntu FAQ's and help files have been helpful.  It is quite a job sifting thru old help and current help answers...but overall it has made life easy as I make the shift. :-)
Now, I need to get plugged into the forums and read / learn / ask / try!

### Post by Peter_Brixey on 2015-08-09

I just joined the forum :D and would like to introduce myself. I currently own and operate a number of PCs, Laptops and Macs running Windows XP Pro, Vista Business, Win 7 Pro & Ultimate, Windows 10 Enterprise (on test) and OSX Yosemite 10.10 on a Mac Pro 5,1. ### Post by bublz2 on 2015-08-17
Hey everyone :) A quick little introduction about myself getting involved with the ultimate Linux :D

I own a dell 3543 laptop and a friend of mine introduced me to Linux, so I spent ages researching about Linux and he was saying how great Linux is than Windows, then he told me just install Ubuntu wipe Windows altogether. So I did that and the first time I did it i managed to screw up the installation somehow :/ Had to re-install again. Worked out well second time round :P 

So tried Ubuntu for a while and I was loving it way better than Windows :D Now I don't like Windows anymore, crappy OS anyway xD So yeah I'm loving Linux, only issue is the games I used to play on windows (none of them AAA titles lol) aren't on Linux, but I found great alternatives instead :D

I'm on Ubuntu Mate now, might see me a bit on the forums now but not that much 

So yeah, LINUX FTW :D

---

### Post by brian-mccumber on 2015-08-18
Hello. I have been an avid Windows user/developer for sometime now but due to a recent failure when I tried to do a factory restore on my laptop workstation, the factory restore was corrupted and my Windows 7 installation was borked beyond repair, I decided to give Ubuntu a try. I have to say this is pretty sweet! The transition was really not as painful as I thought it was going to be and since I used CMD window alot when making VB modules I feel right at home in the terminal. In fact some things I have had difficulty doing in Windows was a snap on the Linux platform, mainly setting up a wamp server, but I guess I set up a lamp server since this is Linux. I had to troubleshoot the wamp server for a week before I got everything working correctly on Windows 7 and then it broke some things in Visual Studios so after I got the wamp server running if I wanted to make an ASP site I had to develop the ASP site in www root to run the ASP site because it would not run from VS anymore. I have been doing a bit of research on Linux dev software and I don't think I am going to miss Visual Studios at all who needs ASP, I got PHP. The only thing I am going to miss I think is the Visual Basic language, which I have been using for years now, but I have found FreeBasic and since I cut my teeth on MS QuickBasic back in the day I think that it will be a viable alternative. I am still trying to figure out what IDE I want to use but since I mainly used notepad++ on Windows, right now gedit is my goto code editor which is fine because it is comparable to notepad++ and it has syntax highlighting for all the languages I use for development. Anyway I guess this is getting a little long winded, I could sit here and talk about this stuff all day, so I'll leave it with this, Linux Rocks!  
:guitar:

---

### Post by Harry_Riley on 2015-08-22
Good evening,
I am a full Ubuntu user at home as of August 10th, 2015.  But alas, I lost my mother to Windows ten because I could not load the drivers for her stupid printer.  She hates windows ten.  

I am a teacher who's district still insists on using microsoft products and raising our millige rates on our property taxes to accomplish their periodic upgrades.  Several teachers at my school are Ubuntu/Fedora users and were kicking around ideas of how we could free ourselves and students off of the microsoft trap.  We really would like to see each of our students have a laptop they can use at home and school. 

Ubuntu is the first Distro to open my eyes and wake me up to a new approach to technology education.  We just need to get the Prometheian Boards and Active Slates to work with Linux to be successful.

Harry

P.S. I to am from GEORGIA!

### Post by @Tornado on 2015-09-08
Ubuntu Forums Admin, Staff, and Members,

I would like to thank you already for the nice warm welcome I am about to receive the minute I Submit this Thread. My name is Jason and I am from Northeast, Tennessee. I have been using Linux for sometime now. I started down the long path of freedom back in 2012. I had two machines one with Ubuntu 13.10 and the other with Fedora 17.

I took a little break from Fedora in early 2015 and decided that I would continue my quest using Ubuntu. I currently run two systems with Ubuntu. One has Lubuntu 14.04 LTS. This machine is a very old Acer Laptop. It has early release of Windows XP. My newer Dell Desktop 780 32Bit has Ubuntu 15.04 with the Cinnamon 2.6 Desktop Environment.

My background in computers started in the mid 1990s with Windows 95. In 2007-2009 I took some collage courses to get certified in Computer Applications. Those where mainly Microsoft products. In late 2009 I started self study of servers, networking and hardware. I spend most of my time repairing and rebuilding older machines. I use the Lubuntu 14.04 LTS for most of these systems.

I felt after several years playing with Linux and Ubuntu I decided it was time to join the Ubuntu Community. Thanks for having me. I hope we will learn together.

~Jason

### Post by Vawa on 2015-09-20
Hello all :)

I'm all new to Ubuntu so I decided to come and say hi, to get to know everyone.
I'm French from the North-East of France (Lorraine) but I registered on the English forum because 1) why not :) , and 2) I installed Windows 8 in English on this laptop and I'd like to get Ubuntu in English as well. And also I guess because 3) it's nice to get out of your comfort zone once in a while and do computer stuff (which I'm close to clueless at) in a foreign language!

I said I'm all new to Ubuntu but I'm not really. I had Ubuntu on a desktop computer a couple of years ago but I switched to laptops and didn't get Ubuntu on those. By now I must have forgotten pretty much everything I knew. I wanted to go back to Ubuntu because of various reasons -- get rid of Windows, use an OS I can really control and understand and which doesn't send private info flying off every which way every time I'm on the Internet, and also because it's really nice to be part of a community like this -- perhaps in due time I can even help.
So I've been reading documentation on various websites on how to make sure the install doesn't louse up my computer (I'll keep Win8 for the moment being) and so on; I guess now it's just a case of getting everything ready, getting a CD, and so on. I'll be joining you guys soon :)

Nice to meet you all! Have a great day! (afternoon where I am)

Vawa

### Post by 0-ashley on 2015-09-28
Hi All

I'm Ashley and I've been using Ubuntu seriously for about three years, started when I learned that Team Fortress 2 (TF2) was on Steam for Linux. I'd played it on the Xbox but heard the desktop version was much better.

My Mac Pro, powerhouse though it is, would have cost a fortune to upgrade is relatively weedy (for games) graphics card as they're Mac-architecture specific. So instead I looked towards a PC - but no way was I running windows! - the Mac Pro also had Flightgear (open source flight sim) running on it but at very low graphics settings.

My lovely wife bought home an older PC they were disposing of at work, a workstation with 4x Pentuim processors - to this day I wonder how much of our electricity bill went to my playing of TF2 on that box :D - I bought a GT-630 card, fine for TF2, and never looked back.

Fast forward to now, it's a core i5 box with 8GB ram and a GTX-970 monster of a graphics card. Flightgear, Steam, TF2 maxed-out, The city of Haran in Dying Light looking beautiful - except for the brain eating zombies, X-Plane - all good, all Linux, Ubuntu 15.04 with XFCE desktop.

Some other penguins have crept into our house too: A little machine runs Ubuntu Server 14.04, Zoneminder, WeeWx weather, NUT UPS monitoring. A Raspberry Pi runs Raspian (Debian) and takes data from a temperature probe in the greenhouse, then presents it to WeeWx via OWFS to join the data from a USB weather station. Our satellite receiver PVR is pure Linux, Enigma 2 based, running OpenVix - that's been a joy after years of closed-source proprietary PVRs.

Cheers

Ash

---

### Post by mikodo on 2015-10-17
Welcome!

Have a look at these for possibly helping with more "learning".

                        Ubuntu Official Recognized Flavours. This Official Ubuntu page has, many informative links:

[http://www.ubuntu.com/about/about-ubuntu/flavours](http://www.ubuntu.com/about/about-ubuntu/flavours)

Ubuntu Community page about Ubuntu Forums that, also has a list at the top for, other Ubuntu Community Support Venues:

[URL="http://community.ubuntu.com/contribute/support/ubuntu-forums/"]http://community.ubuntu.com/contribute/support/ubuntu-forums/

[/URL]Ubuntu Community Title Index Wiki. It takes a few moments to populate:

[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/TitleIndex](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/TitleIndex)

Ubuntu Community Title Help Wiki, PopularPages:

[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Po...direct=NewDocs]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/PopularPages?action=show&redirect=NewDocs")

Ubuntu Manpage Repository. Under "Other Resources" provides, links to more resources:

[http://manpages.ubuntu.com/](http://manpages.ubuntu.com/)

### Post by Paul_Hunter on 2015-10-19
Greetings from Australia,

I am new to the Ubuntu forums, I thought I would never see myself using Ubuntu to be honest!

I consider myself a definite power user/tweaker, I cannot be content with the default.
I have switched to Ubuntu (with unity) full time on my only PC at home, I have been using Debian, Fedora, Linux Mint and Manjaro.

I have mostly stayed in the Debian line other than using Manjaro for a few months, which I liked but I thought I would give Ubuntu another try (after 2 or 3 tries in the past!)

I am impressed, my PC works well, Unity has definitely grown on me, I usually use Xfce/Mate and have tried Cinnamon and Gnome Shell aswell.

Thankyou to the community and Canonical and everyone who works on Ubuntu to make it what it is today, well done.
I will try to be active on the forum, offering help to anyone new to Linux, but I may require help with Ubuntu some time most likely.

Paul

### Post by helmar2 on 2015-11-05
Hi all.
I hope some of you like Grandpa as I am one. Hope I am not too old to learn as Microsoft got me for the last time.:evil:
I am somewhat familiar with Vbulliten so I can get around a little.
I fooled around with FreeBSD a Long time ago so have some of the old stuff still stuck in my head. Meaning I can work a command line and type in commands.

I have also been around computers for quite a few years, not liking MS IOS as its always been broken. I think I started out with IBMDOS 1.0 and then it became MSDOS 2.0
I was around when IBM Warp came out and also the Windows took over the world it seemed.   IBMPC, IBMXT, IBMAT and then onto IBM386, 486, PS1 and on and on. MSDOS was Never able to handle more than the lower 64K so Peter Norton came out with his first Norton Utilities which was able to manage the extra memory in those old computers. 

So here I am wanting and willing to learn Linux Ubuntu.
This old computer I am using and had loaded what I think was Ubuntu 12. something on it.
Having issues understanding some of the terminology used today but hey, like windshield wipers, they all do the same thing, just different in every automobile.

Going to go look for information about how to setup in Gmail as I have a gmail account. For some reason, can Receive it but can't send via smtp..
I even use Seamonkey as its like the Old Old, Netscape 4.07 that was available way back when.
I like that Seamonkey as I can do my email, plus run several widows in it for facebook, amazon, ebay and pop back and forth between them all.

Name is Helmar

### Post by ChuangTzu on 2015-12-07
Hello Everyone,

After a long time away from Ubuntu I recently have found my way back with 15.10.  Not sure if my post from several years ago is still here so I will write a little bit now.

I started with Windows back in the DOS days and stayed with it for a long time, began my Linux adventure with Ubuntu, then began a deeper distro hopping learning tour that included the following distros: Debian (stable, testing and unstable), Arch, openSUSE, Manjaro, Slackware, SalixOS, Mageia, CentOS, Fedora, Trisquel, gNewSense, Crunchbang and probably a few others that I am leaving out.  :)  

Well, I read a few reviews online about Ubuntu over the last few releases and decided to give 15.10 a try as a Live CD, then decided to install it on hard metal.  Played with Unity and felt that it was still a little too heavy for my liking so added Xfce4 (actually Xubuntu-desktop) and everything is running great.  Look forward to sharing and helping the community anyway I can.  

Cheers!

### Post by takegami on 2015-12-10
> **Elfy said:**
> 
**Why not say hello to Why not say hello to everyone? Post your introduction here!? Post your introduction here!**

Hello everyone :-)

1st *nix i'd meet was RedHat in ~1998.. got a CD at work, was very interesting to watch when something loading with a lot of text.. haha) 
Lost some month`s with it, found something good, something bad.. 
Mostly i'd was impressed by fact, that under linux i had not any lags in my favorite game - Quake1 :), while playing at other OS was not comfortable..
Found almost every software i'd need - even fidonet stuff. Next *nix love was Gentoo.. 
And at the ~2008 i'd found Ubuntu.. Was really impressed by fact that there was first *nix, that had support for any my hardware after install - there was no need to scan the Web and compile something from strange sources. Just install Ubuntu - and use. Cool! :) 

From time to time i'd visit this forums to find something.. And there was no need to write anything - all my problems mostly found by someone other :) 
Not so much things that I can`t handle by myself..
So mostly visited forum in RO mode - no posts..

### Post by jimv8673 on 2016-01-20
Goodbye Microsoft, Hello Ubuntu,   Never thought i would say that but since Micronuts tried to jam Windows10 down my throat repeatedly, and in various ways, I have now become a Linux user.

I was quite happy, well mostly anyway, with Windows 7, as it was a great improvement over Vista, IMHO, and my main use of a computer is to access the internet and my own website, Social Media, etc.  with an occasional printing job, or using Microsoft Office for some minor projects.

I wont go into long detail of all i came to hate about Micronuts since their intro of Windows10, which i did in all fairness try (a couple of times) but hated and clean installed back to Windows 7, then wasted tons of time trying to get all the updates reloaded which they seem to have screwed up and mostly now ignore.

At any rate, after a little investigation I have installed Ubuntu via fresh install using a pen drive, and have so far enjoyed every second of the simple change over and install, to adding Chromium to replace Firefox (preference only) and am beginning the learning curve to customize the new system to work for me, and even that has been enjoyable and fun.

So as of this post i have no complaints or issues, im sure i will have questions etc. in the future, so i wanted to get lined up here, but for now i am a very happy camper. Thank God and Ubuntu developers for your fantastic software.  :)   Carry On !!

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2016-02-21
Welcome Robert. Glad you're enjoying it so far.

If you want to try 16.04 LTS, why not try it in a 'live' session (from the install media and choose 'Try Ubuntu'), as a virtual machine in Virtualbox or, if you have a spare partition or the free space to create one, install it there, choose 'Something Else' and put it's grub on the partition you are installing on, not sda.

Once installed, boot to 15.10, open a terminal and 

> sudo update-grub

Reboot. Anyway, this is not a support thread and I'm getting carried away. If you want a hand trying 16.04, just post a new thread in a support area and we'll pitch in.

Good luck and enjoy! :)

### Post by Dennis_Smith on 2016-02-22
hi im very new to Ubuntu and only just joined the site! I have a problem with my apps not working and just wandered if someone could be so kind to assist me please? i crnt seem to find the page where you ask help or write a thread could anyone help

---

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2016-02-22
> **Dennis_Smith said:**
> hi im very new to Ubuntu and only just joined the site! I have a problem with my apps not working and just wandered if someone could be so kind to assist me please? i crnt seem to find the page where you ask help or write a thread could anyone help

Welcome. [Go here]("http://ubuntuforums.org/forum.php"), click on the forum that best suits your issue, hit 'Post Thread' in the top left corner. That's it (but make sure you have a good look here and elsewhere on the net for a fix prior to posting, please). This is a non-support thread in a non-support area, but you'll find what you're looking for at the link. 

When you post a support thread include a thread title that describes your issue and give as much info as you can think of that is relevant to the problem, e.g. what you've tried, links to things you've tried, the exact problem (be specific) and any error messages you are receiving. Relevant hardware and always the flavour and release of Ubuntu you are using.

Good luck and welcome to all newcomers! :D

### Post by Eva_Bouwman on 2016-03-05
Hi Everyone,

Ten years ago, I found my destiny in life and started working in healthcare. I enjoyed my work so much I decided to get my bachelor-degree for nursing. Last December my pc crashed due to a late update from Microsoft. At that moment I was  researching which aspects of safety could be influenced by caregivers for caretakers safely living at home (Dutch healthcare is changing, since January 2015 government applied new lawmaking which foresees in care at home instead of an institution).  It took weeks to troubleshoot the issue after one problem solved the next one arrived. My router has tomato open-source firmware and I red about it being developed in Kubuntu with busybox.  Resulting in me investigating the possibility to use (K)ubuntu.

I wish a had encountered the possibility much earlier in life. Not just because of the many possibilities that are within my reach, but the vision behind open-source states how I like to live my life and perform my job. I felt I was entering a community where many minds and hands taking care of a better world, never thought in my life a would experience this with it-related stuff. 
In healthcare technical additions/devices are used much more these days. Often I felt it is a pitty that some devices and applications are developed so brilliantly but weren't usable because of the complexity is has especially for elderly people. (small buttons difficult to see and to touch is one of the many examples).

I have a bit of programming experience with c++ and when my education is finished I for sure plan refresh my skills and start develop for what is needed at that moment. I am very eager to learn so these forums are great every day I am getting more insight in how to find my way in Kubuntu. Lol tweaking and experimenting has caused me some big crashes I had to solve. I really enjoyed that because I feel my skills are improving one a daily base. I have tried several branches of Ubunty just out of curiosity, now I know I like Kubuntu very much because of the many possibilities it has for appearance. When I start my computer I enjoy the beauty of have it shows.

There is so much more to tell, but I'll stop here because I am afraid you dont want to read who I am because of the length of this post. I'll try to participate as much as i can, but my job is very unpredictable in working hours and my education is also consuming much of my time. In a year from now i can take more responsibility in this community and I am looking forward to do so.

I big HELLO and kind regards, Renee (my given name is Eva)

---

### Post by kb50-k on 2016-03-18
Hello Everyone. I am running 14.04 Xubuntu Xfce desktop. This after like a dozen different distros, has finally become the one I choose. I did set it up, and have tweaked out a bunch of things, and while it still has a few very minor things, it is for the most part
mostly working OOB.

Among the common things I went and fixed, are.
And these are all GUI Xwindows apps.

grub-customizer to aid in making the dual boot screen easy to read, and customized the text to allow it to say what you want it to say. 
numlockx  to turn on the stupid numlock indicator when booting.
removing transmission and replacing it with qbittorrent because it has a nice search engine, and other reasons.
removing parole and replaced it with SMplayer and SMPlayer youtube browser, but go to the site and get the deb which is newer and functions right instead of ubuntu software installer.
Putting in chromium-browser which I need because of chromecast.
It gets my HP proprietary printer, but only after installing hplip from the software center (the GUI) And then you have to go and remove the one that it auto-detected that fails, in the settings manager.
finally gotta have xscreensaver and all of its extras.

Now here is my only last remaining problem.

I have scoured around to find a solution to making chromium-browser stay setup as the default browser.
You can go into settings, preferred applications, and set it using Other and I put it into the slot as chromium-browser and it stays between restarts.
So I found basically when chromium asks about not being the default browser, you just tell it to not remind you again.
That seems to work, but it still sometimes asks.
Also when launching links from some applications it still wants to launch firefox.
I found Gigolo to be a useless application and so uninstalled it.
It might be useful for automounting certain windows shares, but I chose to use gvfs-mount and path to my NAS share using afp (mac) protocol and  place that line into autostart, works great and allows all the apps for the most part, to see it and keeps it as a bookmark in Thunar.

There are a few other minor tweaks and thankfully you can easily install Nvidia proprietary driver in the settings manager.

Installing gadmin-samba changing the load order to lmosts bcast wins   instead of wins lmhosts bcast also fixed the age old problem of Timeout on Network. Just that minor tweak seems to fix everything. Then I am able to get to other Windows computers on the network. My confusion however is that even if you mount the share and choose remember password forever, it never does.
It does for the session but not forever. 
Thats the one grey area that Linux (all distros) still needs to fix. Make windows and linux able to access shares over the network, without having to configure any of it.
Just offer it when installing. Yeah I know Linux can access tons more network items than windows, but if you want it to work in a home environment to an end user, then it should just work initially. 


I am finding Oracle VirtualBox is outdated, and I guess the repository has not updated yet. The latest 5.0.16 is out but the one in the software channel is 4.3.3 and so the new one will not work, because the modules are not
compatible and so forth. Frankly I do not care, and will just let someone else figure it out.

Another (minor) annoyance is that the desktop wallpaper folder by default, is a folder that is read only. For that reason I wind up changing it to another folder. Again silly stuff but it would be better if the default folder were stuck into the home folder instead of a hidden one.

### Post by Joe_Willmann on 2016-05-28
I am new to Ubuntu but not new to Linux.  I am now 62 years old and am getting close to retirement after 34 years as a software engineer. 

I started doing software a long time ago as a machine language programmer (TMS 9900) for a company that did custom testers for the contact manufacturing industry.  Then was thrilled with my first symbolic assembler ( Intel 8080 )!  Went from there to C, then years latter to C++, even taught OO for a while.  What I am doing now if Java development for Aircraft Systems Simulations that run on Linux system.  Along the way there was some Fortran, Pascal, Basic, PLM, RLL languages.  It has been fun.  I actually started in the High Tech industry as an electronics technician and I have a dozen years of doing exrething from design, build, test and debug complex hardware designs.  But that was years ago.

So as part of my getting ready for retirement I need to plan ahead.  I have some health issues that need to be resolved.  One of them, a bad hip, was just resolved.  Total hip replacement two weeks ago.  In a few months I will get a knee replacement.  Then after some convalescence I am planning to retire early next year, or after the next really bad meeting :D

Software has been my life for a long time and have no plan to walk away from it.  What I think I want to do is to give back.  Some of my best times have been when I got to be a mentor for young engineers.  So I have spent a long time thinking about what I want to do.

When I retire, my wife and I are going to move back to rural southern Alabama.  She has followed me around for the last 30 years and she want to go back home where her family is.  So that is what we are going to do.

I think what I want is to reach out to a couple of the local high schools and start up a technology club.  Something that is mixture of hardware and software.  Focused on the Junior high and above and do things like teaching Java, maybe writing apps for smart phones, maybe some hardware stuff using VoCore one one of the other embedded computer designs.

Anyway this will be in the very rural part of Alabama where 80% of the students are on some form of nutritional support.  I have a niece there that is an English teacher and she tells me that in her school they are starting to provide an evening meal in addition to breakfast and lunch because of the level of poverty.

I grew up poor.  In High School I joined a chess club and an Electronics club.  They changed my life.  I want to do the same.



So A couple of weeks ago I had my hip replacement surgeery and to give myself something to do I decided to build up a system.

Antec full size tower case
Coursair RM750i power supply ( looking for quiet)
ASUS Z170-AR motherboard
32 GB of DDR-4
Quad core i5-6600K
2 1TB hard disks

The plan was to install Ubuntu, get everything running, then intsall VmWare and install Windows 7 on that.

So now the everything is there, some problems to still work out.  I just got the wireless working today.  I had to go by a new adapter.  I really like the AC 7260!  650Mb connect rate!

I am working through all of the development tools.  I have currently installed
Git, Bugzilla, Maven, Java, Eclipse.  Most of this is my daily tool set except at work we use Clearcase/Clearcase  instead of Git/Bugzilla.

Next I am going to add some more VMs.  These will be running varius linuz server images for various data base and we server needs.

I have has some problems but have worked through them.  I had to reach out and ask for some help and the community pitched in.  Thanks to all of you that help others.

My main interest right now is putting together this system for development purposes during retirement and as a model for the system I want to sponsor for the high school club.

So if anybody has some input on how to put together this club thing please let me know.  It is about a year off but I need to lay some ground work to get things to happen.

Anyway just wanted to introduce myself, an old grey beard that still has a little game left in him.

---

### Post by Bashing-om on 2016-06-10
candydoudou; Hello; Welcome to the forum.

Safety is no accident . As many times as I have installed 'buntu, I have never experienced a failure and I still always backup my personal data. Murphy's law will get me if I do not make the backups .

Multi-booting is very doable, however as you know, once beyond the point and click stage there is that learning curve to adapt to a different operating system. The effort will be richly rewarded .

If you have difficulties; feel free to ask here on the forum .. open as many threads as needed .

Same goes for me.

I am no genius or an IT pro, but I always tinker with the following OSes whenever time permits, although, I have never mastered any of them.

They are not randomly chose, but they comply with the following list of categories, and they all serve a particular purpose/task ie partitioning, backup etc.

Categories............

Desktop/Workstation only
Server only
LXDE only
Extremely small distros/For old pcs only
Live CD/DVD only
Special purpose Linux distros namely educational, scientific, security, firewall and games etc.
Other OS/Independent OS..

### Post by spidell-renea on 2016-07-24
Hello and Thank You!

I've been lurking on this forum, solving all my Ubuntu problems with already posted threads for about 10 years.  Just last night I got a refurbished computer in the mail.  

It was DOA, didn't turn on!  I cracked it open, and made sure all the wires inside were connected to the motherboard.  Turns out, the power button wasn't connected to the board.  That possibly got loose during the trip to my house.  While I was in there I took out the 4gb RAM and replaced it with 8gb RAM.

After that, Windows booted up and started bothering me.  I hate Windows.  

All was working nicely so I used my new USB Install stick.  Then things got crazy.  I learned that 16.04 on a new HP desktop with secure boot needed to run boot-repair just so it would boot up in Ubuntu, then run the terminal update and upgrade to fix the software center, then run the sound card bug fix to repair the low speaker problem, and then figure out how to connect my Samsung Galaxy s6 to my computer.  All questions answered, all fixed, and my computer that was DOA is now up and running 16.04 with all systems functional.

I must have done about 16 searches through the forum between Friday and Saturday.  I would have been helpless without this community.

You are amazing.  Thank you, again.

---

### Post by k-tech on 2016-07-25
Hi everyone!
Just a brief history, I've been involved with computers as a hobby since the late 80's, I've built dozens for myself and for friends and family, dabbled with various operating systems from Dr. Dos to OS/2 Warp, and my interest with Linux started with Red Hat in the late 90's.
However, I've been out of the game for about 10 years or so, even though I briefly flirted with Ubuntu some time around 8.04.
Sadly, I ended up settling into Windows pretty much full time, mostly losing interest in my old hobby.
Last year I helped my nephew put together a gaming system, and I got the computer building bug again.  I went back and forth trying to decide what to do, and finally, a few weeks ago, I got my butt in gear, ordered a few boxes of parts on line, and put another machine together.
My home computer use is just for web browsing, email, participating in a handful of forums, so I thought something light and clean like Linux would fit the bill nicely.
A few days ago I installed 16.04, got things running, and found out how much I'd forgotten about Linux in general, lol.
I don't have any problems or issues yet, just a few questions, so I thought I'd join this nice community, socialize, and hopefully learn a little more about what's inside the box in my office.
Have a great day!

---

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2016-07-25
Welcome to all new Ubuntu-eers!

> **k-tech said:**
> My home computer use is just for web browsing, email, participating in a handful of forums, so I thought something light and clean like Linux would fit the bill nicely.

If this is all you're doing and you want something light, you could get lighter and still stay in the officially supported Ubuntu family of flavours.

Why not give Xubuntu or Lubuntu a try in a Live session from the boot device ('Try *buntu' from the first options screen). If you really want to go light and for a steep learning curve, why not try [the minimal install]("https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Installation/MinimalCD"), add a lightweight desktop environment (like xfce4 or lxde) and a package manager to install things (like Synaptic) and take it from there. 

If you want to delve into this further and need more help, don't hesitate to post a thread in one of the support areas. Have fun. :)

---

### Post by Roger_Williams on 2016-09-21
Hello
I'm a Network Admin from Canada. I've been using Windows and MacOS for over a decade now. I've tried Linux in the past with Red Hat and Suse but never quite got it. I tried again about a year ago with Mint Linux but never really used it day to day so that went nowhere. I've been trying to increase my skillset and Ethical Hacking is one of the skills that could benefit the security of the company where I work. The courses I'm doing call for Linux so I thought I'm going to give it another shot. In the past month I've installed Kali, used virtualbox, installed Fedora which was a disaster and finally Ubuntu which I'm really diggin. I love the command line, always have but I'm a total noob to the commands right now. It is super exciting to learn new commands and what they can do. Right now I've got a MacBook Pro running MacOS with Windows, Kali and Ubuntu in virtualbox. I've got an older laptop running Ubuntu and I'm hoping soon it will be running Kali in a dual boot setup. I'm hoping to upgrade my MacBook Pro when the new ones arrive later this year and my current one will then be running Ubuntu and Kali. I've made it over the bump and I think I'm here to stay ;)

### Post by LaoziSailor on 2016-10-20
Hello Community!
I am a nerd wannabee lucky enough to have been born at a time such that I was still able to start my data processing career when what existed was tabulators, sorters, collators and keypunch machines which where all driven by motors to enable cams mounted on shafts to time the machines in such a way that punched cards could be read and mechanical counters, relays and type bars could all dance together to do the DP magic :)
I saw vacuum tubes and wired panels to transistors and core memory!
Transaction systems, languages galore and commercial Artificial Intelligence development all part of my "universe blessing" to see an iPhone in my hands thinking WOW...!! this little thing would occupy my 221 unit building completely if implemented with vacuum tubes and transistors..., and the POWER consumption..., hahaha!
anyways, ya'll have Fair Winds and I'll see you around :)

### Post by Koobelakahn on 2016-10-27
Hello everyone,
It's certainly been a hot minute since I was last on these board (and by "hot minute I mean" 6+ years) so I figured that due to my extended hiatus I may as well reintroduce myself.

I started with Linux back when i was in high school in 2008. After Vista botched up my everything, I threatened to live an Amish lifestyle just to ensure such treachery never occurred again. Fortunately my old robotics teacher showed me the beauty of Linux, Ubuntu, and open source, which prevented me from having to forgo such luxuries as digital music, hilarious cat videos, and video games. I dove into the world of Ubuntu with fervor, installing the OS on every device I could. I experimented with other distros, I broke things, i fixed them, and I tried to convert all my friends to Ubuntu as well (with mild success!)

Back in those days i had spare time and a lot of drive. The funny thing about life is that the older you get the busier you get. So life got busy, my hobby of playing with the computer went on the backburner, and my account has been sitting dormant, awaiting faithfully till the day I showed back up. Today is that day.

How have things been on the forums? How are things with Ubuntu now? Anything vitally important I need to know that's been updated since Karmic Koala?

I look forward to many a lively discussions about all things Linux, Ubuntu, open source, and other miscellaneous technological topics.

See y'all around,

Koobelakahn

---

### Post by g33zr on 2016-11-12
I'm returning to these forums after a long hiatus and interim  incorrigible distro-hopping. I think I used to log in as Codger; now I'm just g33zr....:D

Like many Linuxers, I've tried many distros, but stuck mostly with CrunchBang (#!), Mint, and Manjaro the longest. Why did I return here? I recently bought a System76 PC (Sable) with Ubuntu 16.04 LTS pre-installed and decided to try it out since it's been 6 years or so since I last ran Ubuntu on an old MacBook. 

Initially, I had mixed feelings about Unity, but the more I used it, the more I saw the logical ease of use. I now like Ubuntu so much that I then downloaded 16.10 and replaced the LTS version. I'm impressed with the polish and speed of this distro and am now running Ubuntu on my aging System76 Pangolin laptop as well. (Although it, too, came with Ubuntu pre-installed when I bought it, I immediately installed and ran #! as well as Manjaro.) I use my PCs to get things done and am not interested in spending a lot of time tweaking my system's config files and experimenting. Been there and done that. Hat's off to the great work by Ubunut's dev team!:)

---

### Post by Bucky Ball on 2016-11-12
Welcome (back) to the forums g33zr. I guess you are aware that the LTS (long-term support) releases are now supported for five years rather than three whereas the interim releases will have you installing/upgrading every six months or so as they have nine months support now, not the eighteen they used to have back in the day.

Anyhow, enjoy. :)

### Post by neothethird on 2016-12-05
Hey there, one year ago my Mac Mini died on me and i could not afford to buy a new one powerful enough for what i needed, so i settled for a decent PC with Windows 10 preinstalled. It was bad. Really bad. Seriously, i can't believe i spent money on that OS. So, after a month, i decided it was time to ditch windows and set up my pc as a Hackintosh. Bad Idea, not only did it not work, it also destroyed my Windows mbr. So what next? You can't create a windows bootable media from a mac, so my last resort was Linux. So, to be able to accomplish at least some basic tasks until i could get a hand on a Windows Machine to create a recovery stick, i installed Linux Mint on my PC. Everything just worked. It was magical. Two days later, i switched to standard Ubuntu (there i actually had some driver issues during the installation, but the awesome people at ubuntuusers.de were able to help me), and here I am! And even though i set my Windows back up a week later and configured a dual-boot, i haven't used it ever since. I'm never going back!
