### Post by ltoolio on 2017-01-13
Hey all,
Nothing more than an intro post....

Be a long time Windows user (circa 3.1) and have used Ubuntu on and off over the years for specific tasks.  But, never took the plunge full time.

Recently got a new PC (Intel NUC), loaded Win 10 on it and thought "why the hell not dual-boot with Ubuntu since I'm starting fresh."  It's been ~7 days now, and I'm now down to booting into Windows once a day, if that.  

Really enjoying the Ubuntu experience a lot.  There are some things that are taking getting used to (program installation, mouse behavior, window management, terminal, GRUB, working with network resources, some others), but I'm really digging what a clean UX this is. Plus, it's really fun to dive in and learn new stuff.

My assumption here is after a month or so that I'll rarely, if ever, use my Win partition, and I can foresee shrinking it's partition size down to a minimum so 16.04 can have the bulk of the drive.

Looking back, I should have done it sooner!

**Edit - to whoever moved my thread into this forum - thank you!  Didn't see the thread in the Forum (probably should have ready the new user stick.  stupid me), so appreciate you keeping me in the right place.  Off to find & read the starter thread**

---

### Post by lysander6662 on 2017-02-16
Hi all, I suppose now is the time to say a little about myself. First of all though, I should pay respect and thanks to the community here who have been excellent in giving me the help I need. I am truly impressed. Thank you to all who have helped in my threads thus far. 

I am [nearly] a total Linux n00b, though not for wont of trying. Like most here, I have been in and out of computing throughout my life but it was my half brother who introduced me to Linux. He was a Unix whizz in the 80s and talked about Linux a bit, mostly using Slackware, if I remember correctly. When I had my own computer for the first time in the 2000s I attempted to get into Fedora, openSUSE, Mint and Ubunutu through live discs but I could never hold onto installations for any length of time because I was quite a big gamer and needed everything to just work, which it did in Windows. Another issue for me was that I could not grapple with the command line for things like driver installations. Installing things was a headache when I was used to executables.

Last Sunday my W7 install, which I've had since about 2009, was getting way too clogged. I decided a while ago not to use a Windows OS beyond 7 because I am too concerned about privacy these days, and I don't trust W10. I also needed a new OS which worked with Steam so Ubuntu was the only choice - this made it easy because I didn't have to keep looking through distros! It worked excellently off a USB and I really liked the Unity interface. I think Ubuntu is a great way to get into Linux for me and I'm very happy with it as an OS for now. 

However, my finally cracking Linux could not have been done without the community here, or the help of websites such as OMG Ubuntu. I'm very happy to have finally made the switch.

### Post by joepro83 on 2017-03-09
Normally there's an Introduce Yourself portion of the forum. I'm not seeing one. So, I figured I'd introduce myself here.

I'm new to Ubuntu, but not to Unix-type languages. 
Tbh, I have no other serious experience (until now) with programming languages other than Unix, which I haven't used in over 10 years.
I was in the military. The servers we used had Unix kernels. We used them to decrypt foreign enemy transmissions.
Anyway, feels like riding a bike because I'm discovering that a good deal of the language is the same between Unix and Linux.

That said, I pretty much dove into Linux with Ubuntu here. My computer was having too many issues on Windows 10. Blue screening and freezing. 
It's a custom build, but no part is older than maybe 5 years. 8 gigs ram. Decent graphics card. Nothing that should have caused problems.
But I'm now under the impression that when Windows 10 breaks, it seriously breaks.
I was done with it. Seriously, it took me 2 hours to figure out I wasn't going to be able to refresh Win 10 and another 3 hours figuring out how to wipe it and replace it with Ubuntu (between blue screens and freezing).

I might dual boot at some point, put Win 10 onto a separate hard drive or something. I have a lot of games on Steam that I'd like to access again at some point.
But for now, it's all Linux at home.
I'm in IT, so I still have to deal with Windows at work, but luckily, most folks in our company are still on Win 7.

Anyway, I posted in another part of the forum trying to get Steam to work here on Ubuntu. Hopefully a few savvy people can point me in the right direction there.

Thanks for reading. Hope to get to know some of you!
-Joe

---

### Post by cdawley4 on 2017-03-29
Thought I would come by and announce myself as a "New User".  I'm not really a new user, per se.  I haven't used Ubuntu since the 9.10 days, so I feel like a new user all over again.

Anyway, I installed the latest version, 16.04 LTS.  It was different than what I have installed before.

For instance, I installed it on an HP 19-2113w All-In-One that had Windows 8 pre-installed.  Navigating around the bios was fun in order to get the CD drive to be the first boot device.

I remember on older systems, I could go into BIOS and tell it to boot from the DVD drive first, install Ubuntu on the hard drive and it would launch the grub menu and give you the option to boot into Ubuntu or Windows.  Now, with the newer systems, I booted up into Ubuntu from the grub menu, however, when I selected Windows, it changed my bios to boot into Windows every time instead of displaying the grub menu.  I had to go back into my BIOS/UEFI and change it to display the grub menu.

I need to figure that part out and hoping someone can steer me in the right direction for trying to understand these new computers with what I am assuming is UEFI instead of BIOS now...

Anyways, I hope I can stick with this and use Ubuntu 16.04 to the best of my ability.

More to follow as I have more questions...

---

### Post by olia.bn on 2017-04-09
Hello, my name is Olia and I am a linux newbie :)

While I grew up in the glorious command line times (DOS was the word and Norton Commander was the GUI), despite being a developer, I lived in the GUI, however as my rank increases and given that our deployment environments are all linux based, I'm getting fed up with having to ask other people to fix all the permission issues or write / adjust the deployment scripts... so I decided to get some hands-on user experience using one of my old machines.

Not going too bad so far, but since it is an oldie and some of the hardware seems exotic, it does require some manual intervention from time to time ^^

### Post by sevendogs on 2017-04-09
Hello from Texas. Linux and Unix user for nearly 20 years. Not an admin, I have used Linux and FreeBSD as my sole desktop(s) for that entire time. I chose to do so because I do not like either large, commercial OS offering available so have always looked for an alternative. I have used dozens of Linux distros and won't name them all but primarily Gentoo and Debian. I have used Ubuntu a couple of times but the interface is not my favorite so I end up going back to Debian and running Gnome. Used FreeBSD as my desktop for about 6 months and if the Dropbox app were available on it, I would switch back in a heartbeat. Not easy to set up but once it is, it's rock solid reliable. The OS structure is very well thought out and the core OS is virtually separated from all user installed software. Ingenious.

I have bounced around from one Linux distro to another for so long I think I need a 12-step program for compulsive Linux installers...never satisfied. I am trying to stick with one thing that "just works" and Ubuntu does that very well. I just have to get used to the launcher sitting there and taking up screen real estate. I know I can hide it but when I do that, it doesn't always come back...no worries.

I hope to be able to help folks out and to get help. Let's see if I stick around :rolleyes:

---

### Post by rb-smith2 on 2017-05-11
I'm a long time retired software developer (2002) from the IBM mainframe side of the aisle.  A large part of my professional life was spent in network support and systems software development.  The focus of the software development was network management and performance measurement--coding largely in IBM assembly language and C.  There's more, but that's the condensed version of my professional life.  The only development I've done in retirement are some websites--HTML, PHP, SQL, ....

My initial foray on this forum is due to GRUB corruption on a dual boot system--Xubuntu still boots but Windows no longer will.  As an Ubuntu newbie, I reached for boot-repair.  Boot-repair did not resolve the problem.  It did, however, leave me with a URL, [http://paste.unbuntu/24556371/](http://paste.unbuntu/24556371/), that I can't access (no server), but assured me someone could and referred me to this forum for help.  If anyone can tell me what to do next, that would be great!

---

### Post by wildmanne39 on 2017-05-11

The pastebin link did not work.

---

### Post by coffeecat on 2017-05-11
@rb-smith2, if you want to start a support thread as wildmanne39 recommends, please double-check the note you made of the pastebin URL from boot-repair. There are three things wrong with the URL you posted: it should be ubuntu not unbuntu; there should be a .com top-level domain; and even with the correct web address of [noparse]paste.ubuntu.com[/noparse], there is no paste with the number 24556371 on that site.

---

### Post by rb-smith2 on 2017-05-12
Thank you all for your responses to my request for information.  I am sure that in the future your responses will be most helpful; however, I managed to resolve the problem without requiring additional forum resources.  I feel an obligation to explain the resolution; so if you have an interest, read on:

The failure to boot Windows was not due to a corruption of GRUB.  It was actually a corruption of the Win10 Boot Configuration Data store (BCD).  I had just done a local disk to disk restore of a backup copy. The first attempt to reboot Windows after the restore failed, and I mistakenly thought that GRUB had been compromised during the restore.  While it seems likely that the restore had something to do with the problem, it could be merely incidental.  The actual cause will forever remain a mystery. 

 Since there may be many forum members running the same dual boot  Ubuntu/Windows configuration who might be interested in the details of how I reached the correct conclusion and how I managed to recover, upon request, I would be most happy to provide details.  Failing interest, this will be my final post to the Welcome Forum.  I will attempt to understand, utilize, and wisely contribute to the forum.

---

### Post by sdsurfer on 2017-08-04
Hello all from San Diego CA, a short background:

I have been a web developer for over 20 years now, programming mostly in Perl and client side scripting (HTML flavors since HTML 1.0, VBscript, JS, Flash, etc.) I moved from desktop publishing in a print company to an ISP and spent quite a few years as a freelancer on Elance and Guru. I started with Macs when Photoshop was still version 1.0 (!!!) on Mac Classics, then in my second DTP job as part of the tasks learned Windows (3.11) and how to convert them to DTP files. I am currently a "Software Engineer" AKA PHP programmer for a major mortgage company. In my real life I am a watercolor artist with a few local (ish) sales and I love to surf. It's flat today, so I had time to finally post here.

Though I am new to Linux/Ubuntu in terms of personal computing, Linux/Apache is no stranger to me. What I can't figure out is why it took me so long to leave Microsoft and Macintosh behind forever. Over the last week, I have converted my desktop to a dual disk/dual boot Windows 7 pro/Ubuntu 17.04 machine, and am newly the happy owner of a system76 Gazelle, and **I FREAKING LOVE IT.** Where have I been for 20 years?


**First** I would like to give a hat's off to all the developers  who have brought Ubuntu to where it is today. If I had made the jump in  1998, I'm pretty sure the experience might not have been so awesome.

**What I love About Ubuntu**
Total newbie to this aspect of Linux, I first saw a Gnome desktop around 1998 or so and was intrigued, but not enough to kick the Windows habit. So the following are from a total newbie standpoint.

**Everything. Just. Freaking. Works.** I have never even come close to this experience in Windows/Mac. I downloaded 17.04, created an .iso, rebooted, followed the prompts and boom. I was able to sort through the few initial challenges thanks to the documentation kudos below without becoming a support black hole to the system admins I know. :-) 

**Awesome Documentation.** One of the things I totally despise about Windows is when something doesn't work, you can spend hours on Google hunting down an issue that is likely resolved with a single line command, sifting through deprecated documentation that is unclear, downloading and installing repetitively. IMO the times I turned to  a heavy night of drinking I almost entirely blame on Microsoft. :-) With Ubuntu I had a couple issues installing some things, and the clear, concise documentation with a little push from stack overflow got me past them in minutes.

**Totally Easy Dual Boot.** I had a dual boot set up in less than an hour and was moving back and forth between Windows and Ubuntu to check stuff out in two. I elected to get a new 1TB SATA HD for Ubuntu and leave Windows as is.

**Most Things Built In.** Well, not exactly, but native. My working dev environment in Windows for my personal and volunteer projects consists of Netbeans and WAMP for local development (and at work, integration with GIT.) This requires a lot of workarounds. WAMP is awesome but really that's what it is, a workaround for an O.S. that isn't really designed to do what I need to do. While I like WAMP and what it does for me, Linux/Ubuntu has all this native and it was a snap to set up a local apache2 LAMP stack  on both computers. Just awesome.

**Point and Drool App Installation.** That's not really a negative comment, it's just true. The Ubuntu Software center makes installing most programs a totally brainless and simple exercise, and it's tots cool. There have been quite a few programs I've has to install with apt and those weren't difficult either, but this is a feature I hope doesn't get lost in V 18 when it goes back to Gnome (or so I hear.)

**Volume access/Networking is Near Perfect.** I had presumed my first task was to move my files into the ext partition, but this proved false.  Ubuntu gives me access to all the Windows volumes and files without any action, and that saved tons of time. One of the things about Windows I have always hated and was always a pain in the A was networking and keeping it stable. It seems like there was always something that broke local networks. After install of Samba connecting the laptop and desktop were simple, and so far have had no errors or problems - see "everything just works" above.

**Most Programs Have Equivalents.** One of my favorite programs is KeePass (I manage tons of passwords) and this is one example of perfect mirroring, KeepassXc does exactly the same thing and imported my K.P. database perfectly. Same experience with other programs, the only thing I'm losing is Photoshop, Quickbooks, and the mail issue described below. (I've played with Wine for Q.B. and can't get it to work, and am pretty handy with GIMP.)

**No More Monopolies.** For the last week I have only gone back into Windows once or twice to view some settings or other tidbits, I think it was Wednesday that I turned the corner and had no desire to unless I really, really, had to. "Hi, I'm Bill and I was a Windows addict, it has been four days since I had a Windows fix." :-)

**Duke Nukem 3d.** This is a personal thing. I raised my daughter on my own, and a man raising a daughter through her  teen years is way more difficult than you'd ever guess. We networked  DN3D for multiplay and it was a major bonding thing between us. So it  holds a special meaning for me. 

My wife still remembers the childish temper tantrum I had when Windows 98 came along and DN3D would no longer run.  I am not a gamer, but this game was my only fix, hilarious and fun to play. DND Forever was a fail for me on many points, nothing like the original DN3d. I'm not sure why, but I still had the Atomic Edition disk and last night built it out with the help of duke32 and it RUNS! I just need to find time and re-learn the controls lol . . . 

**My Wife Is Jealous.** She is not all that computer savvy, mostly by choice and not education. We dodged the Windows 10 bullet by a month or two, she is on Windows 8 running some program (can't recall it) that emulates the classic Windows desktop. She's been watching and asked, "So when do you install it on mine?" Awesome. Freaking awesome.

**What I Dislike**

Really, there is only one thing: **Email.** I was totally shocked to see the scarcity of desktop GUI mail programs. I also had a disastrous issue attempting to import my mailboxes into one of them and, with notification that it was doing it or word of caution in the documentation, re-sent out ALL MY OLD EMAILS. So I spent the night sending out apology emails once that was resolved. :-\

What I still love to this day is Eudora, and here is why: unlike most mail programs, it allows undocked windows, e.g. I can open the inbox and outbox side-by-side, or make one smaller so I can see the other. All the mail programs I tried are all docked, and look too much like a web-based email client built on Perl or PHP (and some of them probably are.) I dislike Thunderbird the most for that reason. The ability to undock windows in **any** program, especially in a dual monitor environment, is really important.

However, out of this scarcity comes opportunity. I realize I need ot learn how to code for Ubuntu and build it myself. :-)

Hope this info is helpful to the curious or those on the fence, I still keep asking why I hadn't done this years ago. :-) Configurations for the curious:

**Desktop:**

Ubuntu 17.04
Kernel 4.10.0-30-generic
Pentium Dual Core E5500, 2.8 GHZ (MSI board)
Monitor 1: 23" Dell, #2 17" CMV. Monitor 2 is on a split with a 20' extesion that goes to my art table for when I paint.
Windows 500GB WD + 160GB backup
Linux/Ubuntu WD 1TB SATA

**Laptop:**

System76 Gazelle, 15.6" HD monitor (BTW, I LOVE this company, they are awesome) 
Ubuntu 17.04
Kernel 4.10.0-28-generic
Pentium 7th generation 8 Core  (YA THAT'S EIGHT!)7-7700HQ, 2.8 -3-8 GHZ variable (blazing FAST at 2.8, haven't seen it ramp up yet)
Linux/Ubuntu 500GB SSD

---

### Post by guybon on 2017-08-28
Hello.

It is midnight here. When will I stop for tonight.
First time on ubuntu forums.

I do not know if I will be back soon.

A well. A bit about my computer background.

Computer experience from Comodore 64 till now. After Amiga I used Debian. Now I use Ubuntu.
Both distribution have their ways of doing things and as an end user I have not much say in that.
It is possible to make changes to the default installation. But does that give problems ?
There is "Linux From Scratch".


I feel I have to dive in the computer muck from the distro.
Is that my narrow mindedness ?

Sometimes when I am first introduce to a change I get upset, enraged even. After I calm down I realize the change is good.

------------------ A BIT OF DRAMA -------------------------------
RAAA AAARG IIIIII. puf puf . Hey know that I think about it It is not so,bad.
------------------- end of drama ------------------------------------

### Post by gallowglass on 2017-09-01
I was watching the information about the coming Windows 10 operating system when I realized I wasn't going there. Windows 10 represented a horrible blow to a person's ownership of their  computer and the information on it(pay to remove adds on my own computer? Sending themselves the encryption key to my device? Default upload of the user's documents to their cloud?), so the question became what I was  going to do about it. Previously I'd had a pet server that I'd played with through Red Hat/Suse/Mint 9 server, and I knew enough to configure apache with php, log in remotely, and basic tasks like that. 

Being a Windows PC gamer, and being pretty well invested in nVidia hardware which didn't play nice with Mint's newer versions, I knew that cutting that particular bond was going to take quite a bit of know-how. My old pet pc was dying at the time, so early this year I bought myself a laptop from Zareason, updated it to 16.04 LTS, and I've been trying (successfully so far) to do everything non-gaming out of it completely independent of Windows. So far, everything is quite nice excepting perhaps the battery life, and to be honest it bugs me but I haven't looked into doing anything about that yet.

I've been experimenting on my own for a while, and feel fairly stable at the moment, so I figure that to grow, I should contact others (I'm open to suggestions on where to start). I'm also looking forward to upgrading to 17.10 in mid-October, either vanilla or perhaps the dreaded MATE.

I'm interested in secure use and encryption, learning more about how Ubuntu works, and perhaps later on will explore using Ubuntu in the workplace or for gaming on my main computer.

---

### Post by greyfaux01 on 2017-11-24
Whats up guys. Thanks for all your help over the years. Ive used ubuntu since hardy heron, and your community has been invaluable to me. Just now starting an account because i have much less time than i used to to search for each individual answer, and im planning on starting a thread to ask for help for my specific set of problems, which im also hoping others can benefit from with a similar set of problems (after major upgrade, broken things).

Im the type that would rather fix things, and 'win' against a machine opposed to reinstall, and 'lose';). Ive had so many problems that could have easily been fixed by reinstalling, therefore saving me hours upon hours of research/fixing/breaking, but something inside me wont let me do it.. Just recently i fixed my own bad superblock (thanks to you guys of course), and of course most people were just saying 'hard drive is hosed', or 'root partition is hosed', but i just cant accept that. ### Post by galacticstone on 2018-04-20
I have been here since December, but just realized I forgot a formal introduction.  :)

Mike Gilmer. Married for 18 years to my wife Tina. I am 2.5 years away from the big 50, but act like I am 20 - much to the chagrin of my wife.

I worked in retail management for many years, before quitting the rat race and becoming self-employed as a full-time rock, fossil, and meteorite dealer.

Started using computers in the mid-80's with a TRS-80 at school and Commodore 64 at home. I learned C64 machine language, BASIC, and dabbled in Pascal.

I was a DOS and Windoze man until December of 2018 when I finally decided to kick Microsoft to the curb and embrace the world of open source and Linux.

When not tinkering around with flavors and desktops, I study/collect space rocks and watch ice hockey (Tampa Bay Lightning). I have a Florida state permit to hunt fossils on public land and I spend much of my winters down here stomping around in gator and snake infested rivers looking for ancient bones and teeth. My wife and I have a small travel trailer and also enjoy boondocking in remote wilderness areas (no RV parks).

So there you have it. Everything you really didn't need to know.  LOL.  :)

### Post by leo67 on 2018-05-22
Hi everyone,

I am working in the vet industry and have been using/building my own PCs for ages. I have seen a few people of my generation (no way to say it differently...) in this thread who started with computers powered by 8-bit CPUs such as Z80/R6502/M6809 and who learnt BASIC, Assembler and the like  under CP/M like OS. The funniest piece of software I did as a teenager was a fully fledged Forth language built from scratch.

  Sadly, I had been distro hopping for many years (decades...) until last year because there was always something wrong between my hardware and Linux, mostly device drivers at install time but also distro upgrade crashing the system. However, Linux in general and Ubuntu in particular have really caught up in this regard.  Wanting to do some web development, my newly setup Ubuntu 18.04 seems to finally to tick all the boxes (versatile dev platform and plenty of free software available, support, stability as good as my other Manjaro/Arch distro  etc...). Having half of my PCs in dual boot Windows/Ubuntu, over the past year  I had significantly more issues with Windows 10 vs Ubuntu 18.04:  Minimal privacy vs anonymity, virus infestation such as with skype vs none, forced system upgrade of Win10 1900  breaking my RAID vs none, device drivers for a Wifi printer & Wifi card unstable and bloated vs all devices up and running at install time, laborious software installation vs snap(y) package management and the list goes on and on. I know this may not be the place for win/Linux comparison but I keep windows only because of wife/son not willing to dedicate time for a minimal linux ecosystem learning, MS office file format rendering still imperfect (uni assignment) and proprietary video drivers still lagging a bit behind for my HTPC. My productive time is now 90% Linux 10% Windows. Even my router runs a linux based firmware (DD-WRT) and the next project is a Ubuntu Server Box. So don't hesitate to join, now is the time!

---

First time i even remotely tried a linux distro was somewhere back in late 1999 ish.
Just when Red Hat went to the stock market.
And o boy if only i could've profit from that first period.
[ATTACH=CONFIG]280265[/ATTACH]

So now we are in 2018.
And as i am typing this i'm thinking like. *"I'm getting old!"*
Not that 35 is that old but still.

I tried many many many distros in that time.
I could never accurately say i tried them all, But i tried Debian, Fedora as an alternative to Red Hat back in the day.
Used Mandriva for a good year and then went down the Gentoo line.

Eventually went back to Debian, buntu distro mostly and so far thats where i'm glued to.

My World of Warcraft/ Unreal Tournament/ Call of Duty days are long gone.
And with that the need to must have Windows installed.

I haven't learned any programming skills and i am not a cli guru at all.
I mean i know my way around the cli fine just not the more intricate things like piping a lot of stuff together to do womething.
And tho i can see the power of REGEX man..... That just slaps my head round backwards.

Anyways i'm already making this longer then intended.

Thanks for having me,

### Post by gaarro on 2018-08-01
What's up people! I really new to Linux.  I've been a Windows user forever, but I really like Linux and think it's really cool.  Unfortunately (I guess), I'm not gonna be able to completely switch over to Linux due to the fact that I need to use Adobe and AutoCAD based programs.  I know I know, there are a ton of alternatives I can use.  However, I actually still like using Windows 10 and the programs I need on there.

I still really like Manjaro and wish I could use it but it was giving me some hardware issues.  Ubuntu doesn't give me as many. ### Post by coffeefiend on 2018-08-02
I just realized I never did post an introduction. I consider myself fairly new to Linux, and very much a rookie. I am an IT in the US Navy. I have been doing this job for over 22 years now, but the Navy is notorious for being about 10 years behind the civilian sector when it comes to technology. My network is Windows Server 2003 with XP desktops. So yeah, way behind the times. I have a Master's degree in Cybersecurity, an expired Security+ certification (yep, need to renew that), and am in the very beginning stages of studying for my CISSP. I came to Linux because I wanted a usable alternative to Windows. I pretty much hate the overpriced Mac and iPhones, so Apple was out of the question for me. I tried out many different Linux DE's in VM's and liked Ubuntu the most. So here I am! I have already learned a lot from you smart Linux people and look forward to learning even more.

This community seems to be rather calm and helping, so I look forward to more Ubuntu Forum activity!

### Post by cerberus-ace on 2018-10-02
Hey everyone!

Excited to have finally uninstalled Windows 10 and now running Cosmic Cuttlefish Beta!

My first exposure to Linux must have been the early 2000s when my dad brought home a Live and Installation CDs for Warty Warthog...or something older!

cerberus-ace

### Post by davidoroot on 2018-12-04
Hello,

I just wanted to intuduce my self and say couple of words why Linux :)

I'm full time sysadmin and our prod. and test env. always been MS products so i have like 10+ years expirance with networking and system administration. Since 2003 I have always worked with MS products and never fully have had time to try another OS. Is not that I'm point and click guy because I always liked to do things faster and better and you can't achieve that with GUI so I'm CMD guy + 5 years exp. with Powershell. The problem is that I always felt that there is something wrong with all the MS things. So everything you can do in Windows is based on what Microsoft is telling you can do or have access to so if MS says that one thing will be blocked - it's blocked. For couple weeks ago I was reconsidering my point of view and decided that it's time to try something new. The problem is that I'm sysadmin and 90% of systems are Windows based or some kind og MS thing but this didn't stopped me to try out something new. After some time I decided to go for Ubuntu. I like to read about something before even trying it so I bought Linux basic book, downloaded some stuff from the internet and after some days of studing I installed Ubuntu. Things was crazy then. I was like shocked first time. I just DON NOT understand why I didn't try that thing before. You can literally change EVERYTHING in the system however you want it to and make it however you want it to be. It feels like it is YOUR system. The journey just started so I'm just newbee here but to know that this system is like your own "child" feels fuc** good. The best part of Linux, after what I'm seeing, is community. You have a problem? You ask. You get answer. I know that some folks can be pretty annoying but to see how you guys communicate whit each other makes me thing that Linux com. is like one big family, kind of :) After some years with powershell, where its based on "human words", terminal seems complicated at first BUT when you really want to, spend couple of days with ONLY terminal and no GUI  and things become easy and much more understandable. I really really starting to love Linux and concept it stands behind. So much that ubuntu is my daily-driver and I have decided to reinstall on half of my computers. I'm also soon will try out some other stuff, more server related in my test env. It is not always easy when you don't have all of your tools but you have always access to RDP :) 

Thanks :)

---

### Post by clementc on 2018-12-31
Hi fellow Ubuntu and other distros users,

I just wanted to introduce myself to you on this New Year's Eve.

My name is Clement, I am a Windows developer (mainly C++, C# and PowerShell) and am currently trialing Ubuntu 18.10 on a very small configuration (32Gb MMC/4Gb RAM 14" Netbook that I have just bought for this purpose).

After compiling and installing audio and Bluetooth drivers found on GitHub for my specific hardware, I am glad to report that everything is working great so far. Coming from the Windows world (started on 95 and still using Win10 as my daily driver, for now), it is very interesting to see how Linux and more specifically its Ubuntu flavor works and behaves.

I am a newbie in the Linux world but hope to make the most out of it, and why not, one day, make the big jump and move to Linux definitely (at least on my personal computers).

Thank you for the help you provide on a daily basis to everyone. I hope to be able to share a bit of my knowledge and help others as well as I further dwell into this new environment.

Clement.

### Post by josephfg on 2019-05-10
Hi everyone.  I recently purchased this HP laptop at a local pawn shop, and it came with Windows 10.  I liked that, for a Microsoft product, but some things about it were annoying.  Like, correct me if I'm wrong, but it seems to me Windows 10 is AI, so it tries to "learn" your habits and preferences and tailor its actions accordingly.  Unfortunately a result of this approach is that things keep changing, and especially if MY style is still evolving, this can throw you for a loop.  Ironic, since they are ostensibly trying to supply a better and easier user experience.  In retrospect, I really like an OS that just does what you want it to do without all the bells and whistles.

So a friend of mine introduced me to Linux Mint Cinnamon (Tessa).  I installed it after creating a Windows bootable reinstall thumb drive.  I liked it a lot, and sat with that for several weeks, in the meanwhile doing a little research into all the different flavors of Linux.  I finally settled on Ubuntu Studio (Disco Dingo).  I installed it and it has been operating pretty smoothly.  I'm getting better at using the terminal.  I even ran a few commands, successfully, without help from the forums.  So I'm starting to become familiar with the Linux kernel itself, and I note that once you start to get the hang of it it's pretty intuitive,

Recently I wanted more storage space so I reformatted the stick I had had Windows 10 reinstall on.  So that was my decision to renounce Windows entirely and not look back.  And the road ahead looks sunny!  So this is my intro post, and thanks a million to everyone who helps here on the forum!  I look forward to many happy years!

---

### Post by kydrakid on 2019-05-27
Hello,
I been using Linux for several years. I started with Mint but found it unreliable connecting to my D-Link NAS so changed to Ubuntu at about version 16 to get with the strength. Connection now reliable but involves extra steps. My local linux group mostly favours Mint so they are not much help with some aspects of Ubuntu. I find Nautilus useful for file handling with RPI. I am using them for measurement tasks as a precursor to some control. I had a career in instrumentation and control so IOT seems a modern version of that discipline. I have used RPI for measurement locally around a swimming pool and remotely over a satelite link with control to follow.  So far robustness and reliability seem to have a long way to go but I am learning importance of configuration and power supply reliability.;)

My present problem with Ubuntu is **_printing_**. I have a Fuji-Xerox Docuprint M255-z. It is wireless network printer.  It works but I cannot select duplex (double sided) printing. This function did work with the earier versions of Ubuntu in the same configuration (network printer). Duplex printing works on the same machine using Windows10 

kydrakid

### Post by lordofscripts on 2019-10-28
It has been a very long time since I last used Linux. I started using it when having a 2x CD (not DVD) was already great.My first distro was Yggdrassil 1997 I think, I stuck to it for a few years until I switched to Red Had having tried Slackware and Ubuntu once. Basically I loved the original Linux distros that had Gnome and did no effort in emulating Microsoft Windows stupidity.

Back then I enjoyed developing open software for Linux having several packages (many of which I no longer remember) to my name like ChkLogs, SmartInst (that RedHat copied the concept and then the others) and even co-developed one of the most successful Microsoft Messenger ports for Linux that was wrongly named aMSN by my co-developer.

And then out of work I was seduced by the dark forces and joined Microsoft because of its promising .NET platform. But here I am willing to try Linux again for other stuff.

### Post by Alex_Marcus on 2019-11-07
Hi there!

I've been an Ubuntu user and forum lurker since about 2005 - much longer than this account indicates!  I started using Ubuntu in college as I explored the world of open source software and looked for ways to save money.

Since then I've become a full-time software engineer, as well as a side-project tinkerer.  I love using Ubuntu whenever I get the chance, which is much more frequently on my side-projects than professional life.

I'm interested in:
[LIST]
[*]FOSS contributions
[*]Multimedia experimentation
[*]Solving problems
[*]Getting sh*t done
[/LIST]

I hope I can help contribute back to this wonderful community that has helped me so frequently over the years.  Thanks!

### Post by jamespal on 2019-12-29
Hello, Jim Palmer here. I'm a nueb to the forum and Ubuntu. I live in Bellevue WA which is a suburb of Seattle. My home is about 2 miles from the Microsoft mother ship (Redmond). I am old enough to span back to the pre-internet days and remember what the business world was like when the only computers were mainframes and ran by specialist operators, programers and data input was a career choice. My goal is not to break completely free of Microsoft's domination, but to learn new skills in the open source communities. My home network consists of three home built desktop PCs that run Win10, one desktop running Ubuntu 19.10, two laptops on Win10, one laptop on Ubuntu 16.04 (I think) and two tablets. There is some smart enabled devices: a couple of Dots, two wifi lamp switches and a smart thermostat. My other hobbies include classic Honda motorcycles and radio control models and drones. I would like very much to include micro computers in each of these hobbies. The current project is to set up a video surveillance system around the house. Linux is something I have always wanted to learn. I started with the Ubuntu laptop a couple of years ago. I was trying to edit video with it and the PC just didn't have the needed power and I found the Linux command line very intimidating so I went back to Windows. Since then we have decided to build the home security system. I have put together a PC from parts I have laying around the house. I have been looking at ZoneMinder as the app. It requires Docker to be installed and other installations at the command line. I have been reading as much as I can about the Linux syntax and are starting to get it. Any help is going to be be very appreciated and I hope to be able to give back to the community in the near future. JimP

### Post by therealdps on 2020-01-25
Hello Everyone!

  I have been around the Ubuntu and Linux based operating systems community for about 6-7 months now, but am just now really getting into the forums (although I am a heavy contributor on the "Unofficial" Ubuntu Hideout Discord server, and have just recently been recognized for my contributions (mainly just helping people, not anything too fancy) and thought that I could come here and do the same! 

  Just as a little introduction, I would like to say how it is that I came to use Ubuntu, and become so passionate about this ecosystem and community:

 I have always been into technology, and I actually made a small living off of refurbishing old computers and building new ones (back in the AM3+ days lol). Well, some things took a turn for the worst and I ended up in a dark place in my life, but eventually came out of it and decided to turn things around. I eventually started getting back into computers and building them, and came across a free Dell Inspiron 15. But the laptop had a small issue...

  Well, the thing had a broken internal serial ATA connector, so I was incapable of using anything that depended on it. After spending about a week on trying to get Windows 10 installed to a USB stick and an external HDD (USB 3.0), I finally gave up on it. I did some more research and tried searching for something along the lines of "how to install an operating system to a USB drive", and happened across something called Linux Mint. I had never heard of Linux or Linux Mint at the time, but I ended up following the instructions and getting it installed. I immediately fell in love with how everything worked.
  
So, long story short, here I am now. After the short time that I've been working with Linux, I have been through a lot of issues and learned a lot of very useful things. I have also been lucky enough to get a deep understanding of the passion behind open source, and have come to really truly appreciate it. I know this part may sound cheesy, but I don't think that there is anything more beautiful than what I see here in the open source community (minus the crappy elitist stuff that I have come across).

  I have been trying to learn more about the whole contributions thing, and have followed the tutorials on the wiki about how to create and import PGP keys and SSH keys. Although, since the time that I signed the CoC, I have completely erased my min rig and lost all of the stuff that was on it, including the keys. So I am not sure how to proceed on that one haha. I have a lot to learn, but that is where I am at on that one. I really need to get a good understanding on the concept of them, and the usage of them and how I should handle them.

Ok, to wrap this up here is a small list of the machines that I have running, and not including the extra hardware that I have in boxes still in my workshop (yes, I said boxes. I have TONS of computer hardware, old and new):


[LIST]
[*]Main rig: Laptop ; i7-7700HQ, GTX 1060 6GB, 16GB of DDR4 memory, 256GB ADATA XPG NVMe SSD (and an extra as backup), 180GB Intel SSD (for storage). 
[*]Secondary rig (office/workshop rig): i7-2600, XFX Radeon R5 220 2GB, 8GB of DDR3 memory, 480GB Crucial BX500 SSD. 
[*]Travel Laptop: HP Elitebook 840; i5-5300U, 8GB of DDR3 memory, and it has a slot for a WWAN card and a 40mm PCIe NVMe SSD. (soon to be populated) 
[*]A couple of different Raspberry Pi's (3,4, and some zero's). Deciding what to do with them still. 
[*]Main storage Server (all local, not exposed): Core2Do E7400, 8GB of DDR3 memory, 4TB HDD (cant remember the brand). 
[/LIST]

I have a few other rigs up and running, but I would have to get up to find out the specs haha. Well, there you have it. Sorry the post is so long, but I thought it would make a decent into into who I am, or at least some of what I am doing here :)!

Cheers!

---

### Post by usetheswartz on 2020-02-12
Hey Everyone,

I just joined the forum and wanted to introduce myself to the forum.

I've been messing around with Ubuntu since probably late 2010 or so, and I have been getting more into Linux-based OS lately. It is both a hobby and practical for me, as I am a self-employed technician on the side, and I really enjoy messing with as much technology as I can get my hands on.

Some recent projects I've been working on include working with Linux distributions from the command line directly, and learning as much as I can about the use of package managers and repositories and how these are used across different distributions. I am also working on creating my own distribution for personal use and experimenting with various builds using *TinyCore* Linux to create a super lightweight environment for basic browser use for end users primarily accessing web-based apps. Hopefully one day this will grow into a full distribution that I can contribute to the open source community. Until then, I am looking forward to getting more involved, and I figured this forum was a great place to start as Ubuntu is my primary distribution of choice for both personal and recommended usage to clients seeking an alternative operating system to fit their needs.

Some projects I would really like to get into are building servers and more networking-based activities, as this is an area I do not have a ton of experience in, but really feel I should learn how to work with. Other than consumer, home-based networks, I haven't worked with a whole lot of advanced networking concepts. Most of what I do is at the hardware and OS level only. 

\\:D/ Anyway, I'm happy to be here, and I hope this was posted in the correct area! If not, please let me know and I will correct it!

### Post by spswitzer on 2020-03-23
Hey everyone.

Just installed Ubuntu 19.10 as a single boot on my old MacMini, and as a dual boot, along side of macOS Catalina on my 2012 MacBook Pro 13".  Works like a charm.  I haven't abandoned Apple and still have an 2014 iMac 27" and a 2018 MacMini running macOS Catalina.  All my machines back-up to a 2TB drive attached to my Synology RT2600ac Router.  I'm really looking forward to the release of Ubuntu 20. Life is good.

---

### Post by spswitzer on 2020-03-23
Hey everyone.

I'm coming over from Linux Mint, and while Mint is great and offers lots, I found the Cinnamon desktop to be very sluggish, so here I am.  I've installed Ubuntu 19.10 as a single boot on my older MacMini, and as a dual boot along side macOS catalina on my 2012 13" MacBook Pro.  Both machines back-up to a 2TB drive attached to my Synology RT2600ac Router.

One thing that I've noticed is that if I select to use the Broardcom BCM4331 Driver offered in "Software and Updates" my blue-tooth connection to my Apple Keyboard and mouse will disconnect.  I found out that if I select "Do not use the device" my mouse and key board work perfectly.  Because of this I don't have a WiFi connection but I don't need it as I use a Wired connection.

Cheers

Stephanie

### Post by inundated on 2020-04-20
I am new to Ubuntu and this forum, though I have used Linux Mint since its version 19.1.

I was curious about the improvements in 20.04, so I installed it to an external hard drive just to play around with it. So far, so good. I noticed immediately that Ubuntu 20.04 automatically picked up the scanner side of my Canon TS3322 MFP, where I had been struggling with it on Linux Mint for months.

Here's why it works on Ubuntu 20.04 by the way:

[https://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=Ubuntu-AirScan-SANE-Work](https://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=Ubuntu-AirScan-SANE-Work)

And a forum member over on the Linux Mint forum showed me how to get AirScan working on LM 19.3.

Still playing around, and haven't yet figured out if I want to make Ubuntu 20.04 my daily driver after release (which of course is scheduled for Thursday). I may just stay with LM and take the 20.04-based LM 20 upgrade in a few months.

### Post by annihil2 on 2020-05-05
Well -- this looks like the correct forum for these sort of things. New forum user introductions appear to be a thing of the past but important for community building, in my opinion.


[LIST]
[*]I'm 50;
[*]Worked in data entry and finance as a payments manager for a large corporation for nearly 30 years;
[*]In my spare time I study martial arts (although my wife would say it's been my life), Chito-Ryu Karate, Japanese Jiu Jitsu, Mi Jong Law Horn Kung-Fu, and Yang style Tai Chi;
[*]I consider myself a Taoist or Zen Buddhist (woah, that's a range) - lets just say, I believe firmly in peace (contradiction of my martial training, eh? -- oh, I'm Canadian too).
[*]Recently stepped down to start relaxing before retirement, now working with what has always been a hobby of mine - computers, manager of Information, Data Management, and Technology;
[*]Was heavy into the IRC scene many, MANY, years ago under the same nick, as well as Grapes, Grapez, and Annihilati0n -- just incase some remember (you never know, the internet is HUGE but the world is small).
[*]Got heavy into computers with dialup bulletin boards and was a Telegard BBS Alpha tester.
[/LIST]

As far as Ubuntu goes - I've toyed on an off with it for years and always ended up deleting it / removing it / going back to Windows. Now, I'm running it on all my personal computers with the exception of my kids' laptops which still runs Windows as I couldn't, for the life of me, get Roblox to work! (heaven forbid!).

So, what brings me here? I've got an issue with Apache and was looking for answers but - figured I'd start off with an introduction before digging into the unknown.

...Peace!

### Post by kennyh7279 on 2020-07-30

### Post by oldschool-snowboarder on 2020-08-13
Hello Forums... I also was a 2006-2007 ubuntu regular that had a lot of posts, mostly just desktop screenshots and helping new users with simple answers. I haven't used my old account name since those days and am going to start fresh with this new account...

I've mostly been using Archlinux and other Arch distros, and have recently been using clearlinux on my new intel NUC10i7. I really liked how fast and modern clearlinux felt, but I don't feel like building everything from scratch at this moment so I decided to give ubuntu a try again after all of these years.

So far I am very glad that I did.

Everything has been rock solid and works perfectly and also has a very new and modern polished feel.

I'm only having one issue which is with secure boot, and that is probably because of the non-traditional way that I installed everything. As long as I select F10 on the intel NUC boot menu from the bios screen, then it (skips grub?) and goes straight into my luks2 password screen (which looks beautiful with the nice ubuntu logo branding) and after that everything works perfectly normal from there.

I'm going to make another post about the secure boot issue and figure it out later.

### Post by terraclast on 2020-12-06
Hi everyone! I’m Chris. I started a couple months ago with 18.04 in a VM on my Mac to teach myself the command line, to play around with it, and to teach myself C++. I’ve learned a little about emacs and g++ in the process. 
I don’t know why, but the terminal feels very comfortable now, after my trials of getting a wifi nic to work on a new workstation I got for more intense vm work. 
I have been a musician for the last 20 years and because of the pandemic, and prospects on touring and live gigs not to mention rehearsals and teaching looking bad for at least the next year I decided to go back to school for something that is recession-proof and relatively pandemic proof: infosec. 
Started out like most on Windows, but that ended with XP when I made the switch to mac. I still love OSX but Linux is coming up quick and as I learn more and increase my use of it’s functionality, I’m really looking forward to all the learning and opportunities available working with and around Ubuntu and the other Linux flavors. 
Always down for some learning and getting better at whatever the thing is. 
Super stoked to be here around all these masters and I hope I can eventually give back to the community.

### Post by skoggo on 2021-10-15
Hello all, I am a life long user of windows that had to flee because of appalling privacy/security vulnerabilities. I still have hope that I can return to windows one day.

I installed Ubuntu this morning, and have spent the day trying to become more familiar with the operating system. For decades I thought of Linux was an obscure operating system for geeks, I have always been a bit of a geek myself so its ironic that I have just now made the switch to Linux.

I had to ditch Windows 10 because my computer got owned. The story around this is completely insane, things like who was monitoring me and why.

I have read some things on the web that claim moving to Ubuntu is futile given that my machine has already been owned on windows 10.

I am not looking to the community for all the answers. I will continue to study, and learn as much as I can, however if somebody could point me in the right direction that would be very helpful. I am not a total novice today I have been learning about the command terminal and things such as 'w' 'who' 'iftop' 'netstat' 'history'.

My goal is to contribute to a safe, and enjoyable computing experience for everyone without extreme paranoia that they are being observed.

---

### Post by yancek on 2021-10-15
This forum is an excellent source of information and help and I would suggest tht in the future, if you have specific questions, you ask them here.

The link below takes you to the official Ubuntu site for documentation on Ubuntu.  One thing I would suggest is that you stay with the LTS (Long Term Support) version which is always the version released in April of even years, currently 20.04.

 [https://help.ubuntu.com/](https://help.ubuntu.com/)

The link below has some instructions on using a terminal and the command line.

[https://ubuntu.com/tutorials/command-line-for-beginners#1-overview](https://ubuntu.com/tutorials/command-line-for-beginners#1-overview)

Good luck.

### Post by grahammechanical on 2021-10-15
Welcome

I have been using Linux/Ubuntu since 2007. I have one machine. I am a home user, an ordinary user. The phrase "Ubuntu for humans" used to be used years ago. It would be a misunderstanding to view Ubuntu users as technical people highly skilled in computer security. There are members of this forum who are very experienced in computer server systems and security at both the corporate and personal level. But there are also many members like myself who are just personal computer users who through experience have learned a thing or two.

By all means study Linux to assure yourself of the security measures that are coded into Linux. Confirm the response that Linux developers take when security vulnerabilities are revealed. But also remember that criminals like easy pickings. They target the operating system that has many millions of users many of whom lack any understanding of security precautions. If it was worth their time and effort criminals would target Linux. Then we might not feel as secure as we do.

In this page please find the heading Updates and Security.

[https://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=2467687](https://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?t=2467687)

Regular updates are essential for safety

Regards

---

### Post by zeroidnull on 2021-11-12
Luckily Apple quit support for my Macbook Air bought in 2011. I could not update the OS and got complains when installing other tools (e.g. language compilers) freely available. It really made me angry.

The installation went smoothly. And I found good replacements for all software I need. Most issues I encountered (e.g. enabling external DVD drive) I could solve myself with help from the internet. I am quite keen to dive deeper into the Linux  and open-source world.
Many thanks.

### Post by yetimon_64 on 2022-02-13
> **jmsanders-9 said:**
> Hi.. I'm Marcelo Favio, ...I want to know how can I add a friend in this forum? I visit some profile and no for such button exists?Hi Marcelo and welcome, when you visit a user's profile there are 4 tabs and on the far right is a "Friends" tab. Click this tab to see the user's friends list and you will see a link at the top right of the tab to "Befriend <user>". That is the link you need to send a "friend invitation" to another user. See the screenshots below for a more visual help.

**Edit**: you can also add a friend from your own user profile > Settings > Networking > Friends & Contacts; you can add a user's name at the bottom of the page and click "Add Friend" and a "Friend request" will be sent to the user.

Regards, yeti.

### Post by fiveeyed on 2022-08-27
Hello from a long time lurker,

   I figured its about time I made one of these, I may have even done one in the past but don't remember honestly. I'm Larry, 32 years old and live in Pennsylvania. I first got a computer when i was about 12 or 13 years old (2001 or 2002) and had had a few computer classes in early grade school. I quickly fell in love with technology after that, I learned some visual basic in school, taught myself html in my free time after school, learned a little bit of js and c, and taught myself binary to be the "nerdy" kid in computer labs.

    I met a friend in school who I would say changed my view on computers and technology, while I was making web pages and little scripts to move files on my computers and only ever touched windows and a few very early apple systems, he was running linux, writing "viruses" for fun, building web pages I could never dream of building, he was just a guru at everything computer related.  Around 2005 he got me a disk with Ubuntu(Hoary if i remember right) on it and I began putting it on every computer I had, I remember my parents thinking I broke the family pc when I first dual booted it and they couldn't figure out how to boot up windows. This got me to fall in love even more with computers, I felt like I joined a group of people who could do whatever they wanted through a computer if they just learned a little more than they already knew. 

    I decided I wanted to develop software around the time I was in 9th grade, but my parents had firm beliefs that "computers were just a passing trend, and there would be no jobs related to them in the future." so I ended up going to college with the intent of getting a degree in chemical engineering, chemistry was always my best class in school. To make this already long story short, I had some events happen right before my senior year in college and had a bit of a break down, dropped out of school and found a job to get by on. I let myself become depressed and began drinking and using cannabis to cope with everything, and I let this go on for years (about 8 years actually) and lived just kind of getting by, I got away from computers and learning about them, still ran Ubuntu though and toyed around here and there, but for the most part the most I used a computer for in that period was to play games and watch videos. About a year and a half ago I recognized that things weren't working and made a conscious choice to change things. Since then I have been teaching myself python, c++, css (css wasn't really a thing when i first learned html), and re-learning the stuff I knew in the past, and have been forcing myself to get more in depth with the os on my computers. Wanted to make this post to come out of hiding after all these years, and to share a bit about myself as I am a bit of a hermit in person.

    As a project I am currently working on what I am calling my "Super router" which consists of a Raspberry Pi 4b 8gb running Ubuntu server 22.04 acting as a dhcp and dns server, connected to a tp-link archer c54 router with its dhcp disabled, connected to a netgear gs108 switch. The network is not hooked to the internet at the moment, just a local network, Currently the pi is handling all dhcp assignments and dns request for the local network, running an apache2 server, and I can connect to all devices on the network. The end goal is to have the rpi handle all the router functions as well as hosting a servers, and just use the router as a connection interface to the pi basically. But I feel getting more in depth on this gets away from the scope of this intro, so I will make another post later about the router. 

Anyways, Hello Everybody, I look forward to being an active member of the community! Sorry this ended up being so long!

---

### Post by whiskey76 on 2023-07-31
Hello from Memphis. I've been using Linux since 2003, from the days of Mandrake 8.1 which I bought at a CompUSA. Everything worked except the network card. Someone walked me through installing a new card, and I've been building my own rigs since then.

I look forward to chatting with you all.

### Post by mrmonteith on 2023-12-03
I started as Unix sysadmin in 97 on HP-UX.  Later they had me doing support for Solaris.  Since 2105 I've been a Sr. Web Application Developer.  However they have me supporting systems running older versions of Linus dating back to 2010.  Lately I decided to install WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux).  I wanted to see if I can install the latest and greatest of Ubuntu, database drivers, etc, and convert these applications running on the older version.  It was challenging and yet fun. 

 I also have an extensive background in electronics.   I've designed circuit boards, test equipment, and used various microcontrollers.

### Post by mpavsic on 2023-12-11
Hello all,

I'm Miha from Slovenia. I began using Linux over 20 years ago, initially experimenting with Slackware to learn the basics of the command line etc. During my university studies, I mostly had to use Windows, but I was revisiting Linux from time to time (openSUSE, RedHat). Later, due to work requirements (running specific software for structural biology + complete MS Office compatibility), I embraced macOS. A few weeks ago, I made the decision to go all-Linux at home (Ubuntu on my laptop, plus a few Raspberry Pi computers running Raspberry Pi OS), while keeping macOS on my work laptop. Additionally, I run Linux on my work desktop station for longer calculations.

I must admit that I was somewhat ignorant of what was happening in the Linux world for some time. Therefore, I was pleasantly surprised when I installed Ubuntu 23.10 + GNOME on my new ThinkPad T14, which I bought about two weeks ago. Everything seems to work nicely, fast, and smoothly :) However, being "ignorant" for some time has raised some questions for which I hope to find answers here :)

### Post by tommy-luciano on 2024-01-04
Hello everyone, my name is Tommy, I am thirty seven years old (on January fourteenth) and I am from the UK.

I am a music producer and game developer in my free time. Being a game developer and a music producer is great because the two go hand-in-hand, I can make music for my games without having to pay anyone for the rights to use their music! I also volunteer three times a week in my local charity shop. I don't mind volunteering my free time because it is for a really good cause, the charity helps to build homes and retirement communities for the elderly, which I think is amazing! I am also somewhat of a train enthusiast, however, I do not consider myself a so called "train-spotter" because I don't do what train-spotters typically do, I do like researching them though and watching train-related videos on YouTube.

I have been a Linux user for about two years now and I really love it. I plan to one day buy a new computer and a blank hard drive and install Ubuntu on it. For now, I will stick with VirtualBox. I typically spend more time in a Linux virtual machine than I do my host machine, I don't want to say too much, especially after reading through the entire forum CodeOfConduct post, let's just say I have some rather strong opinions and leave it at that, I do not want to **bash **any operating systems because they all have their pros and cons.

[B]Final notes:
[/B]You may have noticed that I made a word in the last sentence bold. Yes, that was my awful attempt at making a Linux joke. I'm sorry...

### Post by werewulf75 on 2024-05-11
'lo, I'm werewulf75 or werewulf or wulf for short. New to the forums, though not to Ubuntu or Linux or *nix type OSes. Running Ubuntu as my main OS on 2 aging laptops and 2 desktops (1 ancient!) but sadly also have to use Windows 10 for a very few specialist apps and support for specialist hardware that sadly are not available in Linux so it's multi-booted on 1 laptop and the one slightly more modern desktop, and even then both Windows installations sport 1 or 2 Ubuntu VMs. Principal OS everywhere is vanilla Ubuntu, but also multi-booting Ubuntu Mate, (and lately) Ubuntu Unity (always liked Unity!), and on some machines also Debian and/or Fedora, and experimentally and likely temporarily Arch again on one machine after a long time. Ubuntu user since last official Unity LTS release, but always kept an eye on Ubuntu since around its original release.  First tried Linux around '96 with Slackware which was then supposed to be the mutt's nuts. It wasn't - very unstable, took ages to set up WM/Desktop without it falling to pieces. Gave up on it after a few months but  came back to Linux periodically, until I initially settled on Fedora, and eventually, Ubuntu Unity - home! :)

### Post by keshara283 on 2024-05-11
Hii everyone!

Glad to be joining you all here! I've been a Linux user for around 8-10 years at this point, and a serious Desktop Linux user for the last 6 years. I first started out with Ubuntu, and have since recently returned (23.10 for my laptop which is now on 24.04, and just recently swapped out Arch on my desktop for Ubuntu 24.04). 
I'm from Sydney Australia, and work as a Technical IT Consultant, with some specific focuses on Application Development, DevOps, ITSM, and recently started my journey on Hardware/Software Asset Management.

In my spare time, I enjoy using Linux for all manner of projects and uses; gaming, game development on Gotdot 4, CTF's (pentesting), etc.

### Post by taxus on 2024-05-22
Wow, what a coincidence, old timer here as well! :-D Began around 5.04 Hoary Hedgehog, switched to Kubuntu when Ubuntu went Unity. I quit Linux around 8 years ago because of bad experiences with all releases from 14.10 to 16.04 on my hardware. I wasn't active much on this forum because I was rather active on the French ones.

Windows 11 made me come back. :-D I had changed my career track, in which I now spend my time in Linux, and decided to clean the windows out. I first installed Manjaro KDE (too much maintenance and instability), then Pop!_OS (nice, but still GNOME 3 with its lack of native customization), KDE Neon (really nice for a while), and the unstable Plasma 6 release made me come back to Kubuntu.

I was pleasantly surprised by Mantic. And now, by Noble.

I have to say though: I wasn't missing vBulletin. :-D

### Post by crtroy83 on 2024-06-28
Hello Everyone,
My name is Troy. I first started using Ubuntu about 2 years ago.  At the time I was a painter, and that winter business was a little slow.  My boss had gotten into the pallets auctions at Cargo Largo and Retail Rebel.  I had found a pallet that looked like it mostly contained DVDs (which no one gets as they don't sell).  But under the DVDs was a large box that had a small hole in it.  Through that hole I noticed a computer monitor, a set of headphones various wires and what I thought at first was a small air hockey table.  We got the pallets for $45.   It had 15 laptops, 5 pair of Sennheiser Wireless ANC headphones numerous Jabra headsets the "air Hockey" table turned out to be a 70" Sharp high definition computer monitor.   The laptops all came from Solar Winds IT company out of OK.  I had tried all the standard tricks for reinstalling windows, but It kept installing Windows pro and wanted a domain login, as this would make them harder to sell, my boss asked if we could just replace the hard drives.  I answered yes, but it was then that I remembered about Linux.  So I did a little research and decided to go with Ubuntu.  This let me install a new OS overwriting Windows then reinstall Windows on the laptops that we would be selling.  I ended up making a sell to my local computer repair guy for $600.  Awesome profit margin so as a bonus my boss let me keep a total of 5 laptops, though 2 of those were a bit older and didn't have a very good resale value, one being a low end Lenovo yoga touchscreen and the other an Asus T100T transformer running Win 8.  The others, Lenovo Thinkpad T420, Dell Latitude 5490 Intel Core i7 with thunderbolt port, and an HP Zbook Intel Core i5.  Having those to play with I checked out various Linux Distros and eventually settled on Ubuntu.  Mow I am working a factory job making hot sauce while taking classes online at FullSail for cyber security, having been awarded a scholarship that cover whatever is left over after exhausting all student aide.  I am also pursuing my own extracurricular studies involving learning Linux in depth and then moving on to programming and networking.  My current computers are a Lenovo Ideapad 3 with AMD Risen 7 and Radeon graphics upgraded to 20GB RAM, and the new MacBook Pro I was given by FullSail as part of my tuition.  I lokforward to interacting with everyone (not Literally of course) here.

---
